In {{ill|1876|ja|1876年}} (Meiji 9), during the {{ill|Ise Riot|ja|伊勢暴動}}, the sacred object from {{ill|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|ja|月夜見宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya}} was temporarily moved to Kaze-no-Miya to protect it from a nearby fire.<ref>Kaneko (1983): p.214</ref>
== Festivals == The {{ill|Kazahi-no-Matsuri|ja|風日祈祭}}, praying for favorable weather and abundant harvests, is held annually on May 14 and August 4.
== Shrine Building == The shrine building of Kaze-no-Miya faces south and is constructed in the {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|ja|神明造}} style with a thatched roof, featuring {{ill|Chigi and Katsuogi|ja|千木・鰹木|lt=chigi and katsuogi}} with five odd-numbered katsuogi, following the Gekū’s design. The old shrine site for reconstruction (shikinen sengū) is adjacent to the east and west.
== Access == * Nearest Station: About a 12-minute walk from {{ill|Ise-shi Station|ja|伊勢市駅}} via {{ill|JR Sangu Line|ja|参宮線}} and {{ill|Kintetsu|ja|近畿日本鉄道}}. * Nearest Bus Stop: About a 5-minute walk from {{ill|Mie Kotsu|ja|三重交通}} Gekū-mae Bus Stop. * Nearest Interchange: About 2 km from {{ill|Ise Nishi Interchange|ja|伊勢西インターチェンジ}} on the {{ill|Ise Expressway|ja|伊勢自動車道}}. * Parking: Free parking available.
== References == * Kaneko Nobuo. *Tamaki Town History, Volume II – History and Traditions of South Ise.* Mie Prefecture Local History Series No. 91, Mie Prefecture Local History Publication Association, December 10, 1983. * {{ill|Katsunoshin Sakurai|ja|桜井勝之進}}. *Ise Grand Shrine.* Gakuseisha, May 20, 1969. * Katsunoshin Sakurai. *The Shrine of the Great deity of Ise.* Hori Shoten, March 25, 1973.
== Related Pages == {{commonscat|Kaze-no-miya (Geku)}} * {{ill|Shinatsuhiko|ja|シナツヒコ}} * {{ill|Kamikaze|ja|神風}} * {{ill|Mongol invasions of Japan|ja|元寇}} * {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮}} * {{ill|List of Shrines and Facilities of Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮を構成する神社と施設|1=List of 125 Shrines of Ise}}
== External Links == * [http://www.isejingu.or.jp/gegu/gegu5.htm Ise Jingu Official Site]

revision.text:
{{Infobox shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[image:Ise-Shrine Geku-Kazenomiya.jpg|256px|Kazenomiya]] | Name = Kazenomiya | location = Toyokawa-cho, [[Ise]], [[Mie Prefecture]], Japan | deity = [[Shinatsuhiko]] and [[Shinatobe]] | type = {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮}} auxiliary shrine (*betsugū*) | Established = Before 997 CE | Main Hall = [[Shinmei-zukuri]] | coordinates = {{coord|34|29|9.1|N|136|42|14.2|E|region:JP|display=inline,title}} | festival = Kazahi-no-Matsuri (*Wind Day Prayer Festival*) }}
'''Kaze-no-Miya''' (風宮) is an auxiliary shrine (*betsugū*) located within the precincts of {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮}} of the [[Ise Grand Shrine]] in [[Toyokawa-cho]], [[Ise]], [[Mie Prefecture]], Japan.
== Overview== [[File:Kame-ish 01.JPG|left|thumb|180px|Kame-ishi (Turtle Stone)]] '''Kazenomiya''' is a {{ill|Betsugū (detached shrine)|ja|別宮}} of the {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮}} (Gekū), located at the foot of **Hinokioyama** (檜尾山), south of the main shrine. The enshrined deities are {{ill|Shinatsuhiko|ja|級長津彦命}} and {{ill|Shinatobe|ja|級長戸辺命}}, the same as those in the {{ill|Kazahinomi-no-Miya|ja|風日祈宮}}, a detached shrine of the {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮}} (Naikū).
'''Kazenomiya''' is situated to the left after crossing the **Kame-ishi** (亀石), a stone along the approach to {{ill|Taga-no-Miya|ja|多賀宮}}, next to the pond in front of the main shrine. It is said that Kame-ishi was transported from the entrance of the **Ama-no-Iwato** (天岩戸) on {{ill|Mount Takakura (Ise)|ja|高倉山 (伊勢市)}}.
The term *Betsugū* (別宮) means "separate shrine," considered second in reverence only to the main shrine. Apart from '''Kazenomiya''', the Gekū has other Betsugū: {{ill|Taka-no-Miya|ja|多賀宮}} and {{ill|Tsuchi-no-Miya|ja|土宮}} within the precincts, and {{ill|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|ja|月夜見宮|8=ja|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya}} outside the precincts. Kazamiya was the last to be designated as a Betsugū in {{ill|1293|ja|1293年}} (Shōō 6) and is ranked fourth among the four Betsugū.
Originally, it was known as **Kazeya-shiro** (風社) or **Kazejinja** (風神社), a shrine of lesser rank. However, following the {{ill|Mongol invasions of Japan|ja|元寇}} in {{ill|1281|ja|1281年}} (Kōan 4), when divine winds (*kamikaze*) protected Japan, it was elevated to the status of Betsugū.
== Enshrined Deities == The enshrined deities of {{ill|Kaze-no-Miya|ja|風宮}} are {{ill|Shinatsuhiko|ja|シナツヒコ}} and {{ill|Shinatobe|ja|シナトベ}}, gods who govern wind and rain. Originally worshipped as deities bringing favorable weather for agriculture, after the Mongol invasions, they became objects of prayers for the salvation of Japan in times of national crisis.
== History == The origins of Kaze-no-Miya are unclear, but the earliest reference appears in the "Chōtoku Kenroku" from {{ill|997|ja|997年}} (Chōtoku 3), citing it as "Kaze-sha," ranked 4th among 32 rice field shrines under the jurisdiction of the Gekū, as noted in {{ill|Watarai Ieyuki|ja|度会家行}}'s "Ruiju Jingi Hongen." "Rice field shrines" correspond to the current末社 (massha), considered lower than sessha (auxiliary shrines).
Although initially ranked as a末社, due to its deities’ importance in agriculture, the {{ill|Kazahi-no-Matsuri|ja|風日祈祭}} was held, and offerings of "kakechikara" (bundled rice stalks) were made during the {{ill|Kannamesai|ja|神嘗祭}}.
During the {{ill|Mongol invasions of Japan|ja|元寇}} in {{ill|1281|ja|1281年}} (Kōan 4), the court dispatched the Dainagon Tameuji to perform prayers at both the Naikū's Kaze-sha and this shrine. After the retreat of the Mongol forces, the victory was attributed to the "Divine Wind" ({{ill|Kamikaze|ja|神風}}), and in {{ill|1293|ja|1293年}} (Shōō 6), the shrine was elevated to a betsu-gū (detached shrine), named {{ill|Kazahi-no-Miya|ja|風日祈宮}} and Kaze-no-Miya respectively.
During the late {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, when Western nations visited Japan, the court held 15 days of prayers for the expulsion of foreigners at Kaze-no-Miya and Kazahi-no-Miya in {{ill|1863|ja|1863年}} (Bunkyū 3).
In {{ill|1876|ja|1876年}} (Meiji 9), during the {{ill|Ise Riot|ja|伊勢暴動}}, the sacred object from {{ill|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|ja|月夜見宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya}} was temporarily moved to Kaze-no-Miya to protect it from a nearby fire.<ref>Kaneko (1983): p.214</ref>
== Festivals == The {{ill|Kazahi-no-Matsuri|ja|風日祈祭}}, praying for favorable weather and abundant harvests, is held annually on May 14 and August 4.
== Shrine Building == The shrine building of Kaze-no-Miya faces south and is constructed in the {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|ja|神明造}} style with a thatched roof, featuring {{ill|Chigi and Katsuogi|ja|千木・鰹木|lt=chigi and katsuogi}} with five odd-numbered katsuogi, following the Gekū’s design. The old shrine site for reconstruction (shikinen sengū) is adjacent to the east and west.
== Access == * Nearest Station: About a 12-minute walk from {{ill|Ise-shi Station|ja|伊勢市駅}} via {{ill|JR Sangu Line|ja|参宮線}} and {{ill|Kintetsu|ja|近畿日本鉄道}}. * Nearest Bus Stop: About a 5-minute walk from {{ill|Mie Kotsu|ja|三重交通}} Gekū-mae Bus Stop. * Nearest Interchange: About 2 km from {{ill|Ise Nishi Interchange|ja|伊勢西インターチェンジ}} on the {{ill|Ise Expressway|ja|伊勢自動車道}}. * Parking: Free parking available.
== References == * Kaneko Nobuo. *Tamaki Town History, Volume II – History and Traditions of South Ise.* Mie Prefecture Local History Series No. 91, Mie Prefecture Local History Publication Association, December 10, 1983. * {{ill|Katsunoshin Sakurai|ja|桜井勝之進}}. *Ise Grand Shrine.* Gakuseisha, May 20, 1969. * Katsunoshin Sakurai. *The Shrine of the Great deity of Ise.* Hori Shoten, March 25, 1973.
== Related Pages == {{commonscat|Kaze-no-miya (Geku)}} * {{ill|Shinatsuhiko|ja|シナツヒコ}} * {{ill|Kamikaze|ja|神風}} * {{ill|Mongol invasions of Japan|ja|元寇}} * {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮}} * {{ill|List of Shrines and Facilities of Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮を構成する神社と施設|1=List of 125 Shrines of Ise}}
== External Links == * [http://www.isejingu.or.jp/gegu/gegu5.htm Ise Jingu Official Site]

'''Kazenomiya''' is situated to the left after crossing the **Kame-ishi** (亀石), a stone along the approach to {{ill|Taga-no-Miya|ja|多賀宮}}, next to the pond in front of the main shrine. It is said that Kame-ishi was transported from the entrance of the **Ama-no-Iwato** (天岩戸) on {{ill|Mount Takakura (Ise)|ja|高倉山 (伊勢市)}}.
The term *Betsugū* (別宮) means "separate shrine," considered second in reverence only to the main shrine. Apart from '''Kazenomiya''', the Gekū has other Betsugū: {{ill|Taka-no-Miya|ja|多賀宮}} and {{ill|Tsuchi-no-Miya|ja|土宮}} within the precincts, and {{ill|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|ja|月夜見宮|8=ja|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya}} outside the precincts. Kazamiya was the last to be designated as a Betsugū in {{ill|1293|ja|1293年}} (Shōō 6) and is ranked fourth among the four Betsugū.
Originally, it was known as **Kazeya-shiro** (風社) or **Kazejinja** (風神社), a shrine of lesser rank. However, following the {{ill|Mongol invasions of Japan|ja|元寇}} in {{ill|1281|ja|1281年}} (Kōan 4), when divine winds (*kamikaze*) protected Japan, it was elevated to the status of Betsugū.
== Enshrined Deities == The enshrined deities of {{ill|Kaze-no-Miya|ja|風宮}} are {{ill|Shinatsuhiko|ja|シナツヒコ}} and {{ill|Shinatobe|ja|シナトベ}}, gods who govern wind and rain. Originally worshipped as deities bringing favorable weather for agriculture, after the Mongol invasions, they became objects of prayers for the salvation of Japan in times of national crisis.
== History == The origins of Kaze-no-Miya are unclear, but the earliest reference appears in the "Chōtoku Kenroku" from {{ill|997|ja|997年}} (Chōtoku 3), citing it as "Kaze-sha," ranked 4th among 32 rice field shrines under the jurisdiction of the Gekū, as noted in {{ill|Watarai Ieyuki|ja|度会家行}}'s "Ruiju Jingi Hongen." "Rice field shrines" correspond to the current末社 (massha), considered lower than sessha (auxiliary shrines).
Although initially ranked as a末社, due to its deities’ importance in agriculture, the {{ill|Kazahi-no-Matsuri|ja|風日祈祭}} was held, and offerings of "kakechikara" (bundled rice stalks) were made during the {{ill|Kannamesai|ja|神嘗祭}}.
During the {{ill|Mongol invasions of Japan|ja|元寇}} in {{ill|1281|ja|1281年}} (Kōan 4), the court dispatched the Dainagon Tameuji to perform prayers at both the Naikū's Kaze-sha and this shrine. After the retreat of the Mongol forces, the victory was attributed to the "Divine Wind" ({{ill|Kamikaze|ja|神風}}), and in {{ill|1293|ja|1293年}} (Shōō 6), the shrine was elevated to a betsu-gū (detached shrine), named {{ill|Kazahi-no-Miya|ja|風日祈宮}} and Kaze-no-Miya respectively.
During the late {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, when Western nations visited Japan, the court held 15 days of prayers for the expulsion of foreigners at Kaze-no-Miya and Kazahi-no-Miya in {{ill|1863|ja|1863年}} (Bunkyū 3).
In {{ill|1876|ja|1876年}} (Meiji 9), during the {{ill|Ise Riot|ja|伊勢暴動}}, the sacred object from {{ill|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|ja|月夜見宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya}} was temporarily moved to Kaze-no-Miya to protect it from a nearby fire.<ref>Kaneko (1983): p.214</ref>
== Festivals == The {{ill|Kazahi-no-Matsuri|ja|風日祈祭}}, praying for favorable weather and abundant harvests, is held annually on May 14 and August 4.
== Shrine Building == The shrine building of Kaze-no-Miya faces south and is constructed in the {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|ja|神明造}} style with a thatched roof, featuring {{ill|Chigi and Katsuogi|ja|千木・鰹木|lt=chigi and katsuogi}} with five odd-numbered katsuogi, following the Gekū’s design. The old shrine site for reconstruction (shikinen sengū) is adjacent to the east and west.
== Access == * Nearest Station: About a 12-minute walk from {{ill|Ise-shi Station|ja|伊勢市駅}} via {{ill|JR Sangu Line|ja|参宮線}} and {{ill|Kintetsu|ja|近畿日本鉄道}}. * Nearest Bus Stop: About a 5-minute walk from {{ill|Mie Kotsu|ja|三重交通}} Gekū-mae Bus Stop. * Nearest Interchange: About 2 km from {{ill|Ise Nishi Interchange|ja|伊勢西インターチェンジ}} on the {{ill|Ise Expressway|ja|伊勢自動車道}}. * Parking: Free parking available.
== References == * Kaneko Nobuo. *Tamaki Town History, Volume II – History and Traditions of South Ise.* Mie Prefecture Local History Series No. 91, Mie Prefecture Local History Publication Association, December 10, 1983. * {{ill|Katsunoshin Sakurai|ja|桜井勝之進}}. *Ise Grand Shrine.* Gakuseisha, May 20, 1969. * Katsunoshin Sakurai. *The Shrine of the Great deity of Ise.* Hori Shoten, March 25, 1973.

{{Infobox shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[image:Ise-Shrine Geku-Kazenomiya.jpg|256px|Kazenomiya]] | Name = Kazenomiya | location = Toyokawa-cho, [[Ise]], [[Mie Prefecture]], Japan | deity = [[Shinatsuhiko]] and [[Shinatobe]] | type = {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮}} auxiliary shrine (*betsugū*) | Established = Before 997 CE | Main Hall = [[Shinmei-zukuri]] | coordinates = {{coord|34|29|9.1|N|136|42|14.2|E|region:JP|display=inline,title}} | festival = Kazahi-no-Matsuri (*Wind Day Prayer Festival*) }}
'''Kaze-no-Miya''' (風宮) is an auxiliary shrine (*betsugū*) located within the precincts of {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮}} of the [[Ise Grand Shrine]] in [[Toyokawa-cho]], [[Ise]], [[Mie Prefecture]], Japan.
== Overview== [[File:Kame-ish 01.JPG|left|thumb|180px|Kame-ishi (Turtle Stone)]] '''Kazenomiya''' is a {{ill|Betsugū (detached shrine)|ja|別宮}} of the {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮}} (Gekū), located at the foot of **Hinokioyama** (檜尾山), south of the main shrine. The enshrined deities are {{ill|Shinatsuhiko|ja|級長津彦命}} and {{ill|Shinatobe|ja|級長戸辺命}}, the same as those in the {{ill|Kazahinomi-no-Miya|ja|風日祈宮}}, a detached shrine of the {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮}} (Naikū).
'''Kazenomiya''' is situated to the left after crossing the **Kame-ishi** (亀石), a stone along the approach to {{ill|Taga-no-Miya|ja|多賀宮}}, next to the pond in front of the main shrine. It is said that Kame-ishi was transported from the entrance of the **Ama-no-Iwato** (天岩戸) on {{ill|Mount Takakura (Ise)|ja|高倉山 (伊勢市)}}.
The term *Betsugū* (別宮) means "separate shrine," considered second in reverence only to the main shrine. Apart from '''Kazenomiya''', the Gekū has other Betsugū: {{ill|Taka-no-Miya|ja|多賀宮}} and {{ill|Tsuchi-no-Miya|ja|土宮}} within the precincts, and {{ill|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|ja|月夜見宮|8=ja|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya}} outside the precincts. Kazamiya was the last to be designated as a Betsugū in {{ill|1293|ja|1293年}} (Shōō 6) and is ranked fourth among the four Betsugū.
Originally, it was known as **Kazeya-shiro** (風社) or **Kazejinja** (風神社), a shrine of lesser rank. However, following the {{ill|Mongol invasions of Japan|ja|元寇}} in {{ill|1281|ja|1281年}} (Kōan 4), when divine winds (*kamikaze*) protected Japan, it was elevated to the status of Betsugū.
== Enshrined Deities == The enshrined deities of {{ill|Kaze-no-Miya|ja|風宮}} are {{ill|Shinatsuhiko|ja|シナツヒコ}} and {{ill|Shinatobe|ja|シナトベ}}, gods who govern wind and rain. Originally worshipped as deities bringing favorable weather for agriculture, after the Mongol invasions, they became objects of prayers for the salvation of Japan in times of national crisis.
== History == The origins of Kaze-no-Miya are unclear, but the earliest reference appears in the "Chōtoku Kenroku" from {{ill|997|ja|997年}} (Chōtoku 3), citing it as "Kaze-sha," ranked 4th among 32 rice field shrines under the jurisdiction of the Gekū, as noted in {{ill|Watarai Ieyuki|ja|度会家行}}'s "Ruiju Jingi Hongen." "Rice field shrines" correspond to the current末社 (massha), considered lower than sessha (auxiliary shrines).
Although initially ranked as a末社, due to its deities’ importance in agriculture, the {{ill|Kazahi-no-Matsuri|ja|風日祈祭}} was held, and offerings of "kakechikara" (bundled rice stalks) were made during the {{ill|Kannamesai|ja|神嘗祭}}.
During the {{ill|Mongol invasions of Japan|ja|元寇}} in {{ill|1281|ja|1281年}} (Kōan 4), the court dispatched the Dainagon Tameuji to perform prayers at both the Naikū's Kaze-sha and this shrine. After the retreat of the Mongol forces, the victory was attributed to the "Divine Wind" ({{ill|Kamikaze|ja|神風}}), and in {{ill|1293|ja|1293年}} (Shōō 6), the shrine was elevated to a betsu-gū (detached shrine), named {{ill|Kazahi-no-Miya|ja|風日祈宮}} and Kaze-no-Miya respectively.
During the late {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, when Western nations visited Japan, the court held 15 days of prayers for the expulsion of foreigners at Kaze-no-Miya and Kazahi-no-Miya in {{ill|1863|ja|1863年}} (Bunkyū 3).
In {{ill|1876|ja|1876年}} (Meiji 9), during the {{ill|Ise Riot|ja|伊勢暴動}}, the sacred object from {{ill|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|ja|月夜見宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya}} was temporarily moved to Kaze-no-Miya to protect it from a nearby fire.<ref>Kaneko (1983): p.214</ref>
== Festivals == The {{ill|Kazahi-no-Matsuri|ja|風日祈祭}}, praying for favorable weather and abundant harvests, is held annually on May 14 and August 4.
== Shrine Building == The shrine building of Kaze-no-Miya faces south and is constructed in the {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|ja|神明造}} style with a thatched roof, featuring {{ill|Chigi and Katsuogi|ja|千木・鰹木|lt=chigi and katsuogi}} with five odd-numbered katsuogi, following the Gekū’s design. The old shrine site for reconstruction (shikinen sengū) is adjacent to the east and west.
== Access == * Nearest Station: About a 12-minute walk from {{ill|Ise-shi Station|ja|伊勢市駅}} via {{ill|JR Sangu Line|ja|参宮線}} and {{ill|Kintetsu|ja|近畿日本鉄道}}. * Nearest Bus Stop: About a 5-minute walk from {{ill|Mie Kotsu|ja|三重交通}} Gekū-mae Bus Stop. * Nearest Interchange: About 2 km from {{ill|Ise Nishi Interchange|ja|伊勢西インターチェンジ}} on the {{ill|Ise Expressway|ja|伊勢自動車道}}. * Parking: Free parking available.
== References == * Kaneko Nobuo. *Tamaki Town History, Volume II – History and Traditions of South Ise.* Mie Prefecture Local History Series No. 91, Mie Prefecture Local History Publication Association, December 10, 1983. * {{ill|Katsunoshin Sakurai|ja|桜井勝之進}}. *Ise Grand Shrine.* Gakuseisha, May 20, 1969. * Katsunoshin Sakurai. *The Shrine of the Great deity of Ise.* Hori Shoten, March 25, 1973.
== Related Pages == {{commonscat|Kaze-no-miya (Geku)}} * {{ill|Shinatsuhiko|ja|シナツヒコ}} * {{ill|Kamikaze|ja|神風}} * {{ill|Mongol invasions of Japan|ja|元寇}} * {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮}} * {{ill|List of Shrines and Facilities of Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮を構成する神社と施設|1=List of 125 Shrines of Ise}}
== External Links == * [http://www.isejingu.or.jp/gegu/gegu5.htm Ise Jingu Official Site]

The old shrine site for reconstruction (shikinen sengū) is adjacent to the east and west.
== Access == * Nearest Station: About a 12-minute walk from {{ill|Ise-shi Station|ja|伊勢市駅}} via {{ill|JR Sangu Line|ja|参宮線}} and {{ill|Kintetsu|ja|近畿日本鉄道}}. * Nearest Bus Stop: About a 5-minute walk from {{ill|Mie Kotsu|ja|三重交通}} Gekū-mae Bus Stop. * Nearest Interchange: About 2 km from {{ill|Ise Nishi Interchange|ja|伊勢西インターチェンジ}} on the {{ill|Ise Expressway|ja|伊勢自動車道}}. * Parking: Free parking available.
== References == * Kaneko Nobuo. *Tamaki Town History, Volume II – History and Traditions of South Ise.* Mie Prefecture Local History Series No. 91, Mie Prefecture Local History Publication Association, December 10, 1983. * {{ill|Katsunoshin Sakurai|ja|桜井勝之進}}. *Ise Grand Shrine.* Gakuseisha, May 20, 1969. * Katsunoshin Sakurai. *The Shrine of the Great deity of Ise.* Hori Shoten, March 25, 1973.
== Related Pages == {{commonscat|Kaze-no-miya (Geku)}} * {{ill|Shinatsuhiko|ja|シナツヒコ}} * {{ill|Kamikaze|ja|神風}} * {{ill|Mongol invasions of Japan|ja|元寇}} * {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮}} * {{ill|List of Shrines and Facilities of Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮を構成する神社と施設|1=List of 125 Shrines of Ise}}

revision.text:
{{Infobox shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[image:Ise-Shrine Geku-Kazenomiya.jpg|256px|Kazenomiya]] | Name = Kazenomiya | location = Toyokawa-cho, [[Ise]], [[Mie Prefecture]], Japan | deity = [[Shinatsuhiko]] and [[Shinatobe]] | type = {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮}} auxiliary shrine (*betsugū*) | Established = Before 997 CE | Main Hall = [[Shinmei-zukuri]] | coordinates = {{coord|34|29|9.1|N|136|42|14.2|E|region:JP|display=inline,title}} | festival = Kazahi-no-Matsuri (*Wind Day Prayer Festival*) }}
'''Kaze-no-Miya''' (風宮) is an auxiliary shrine (*betsugū*) located within the precincts of {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮}} of the [[Ise Grand Shrine]] in [[Toyokawa-cho]], [[Ise]], [[Mie Prefecture]], Japan.
== Overview== [[File:Kame-ish 01.JPG|left|thumb|180px|Kame-ishi (Turtle Stone)]] '''Kazenomiya''' is a {{ill|Betsugū (detached shrine)|ja|別宮}} of the {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮}} (Gekū), located at the foot of **Hinokioyama** (檜尾山), south of the main shrine. The enshrined deities are {{ill|Shinatsuhiko|ja|級長津彦命}} and {{ill|Shinatobe|ja|級長戸辺命}}, the same as those in the {{ill|Kazahinomi-no-Miya|ja|風日祈宮}}, a detached shrine of the {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮}} (Naikū).
'''Kazenomiya''' is situated to the left after crossing the **Kame-ishi** (亀石), a stone along the approach to {{ill|Taga-no-Miya|ja|多賀宮}}, next to the pond in front of the main shrine. It is said that Kame-ishi was transported from the entrance of the **Ama-no-Iwato** (天岩戸) on {{ill|Mount Takakura (Ise)|ja|高倉山 (伊勢市)}}.
The term *Betsugū* (別宮) means "separate shrine," considered second in reverence only to the main shrine. Apart from '''Kazenomiya''', the Gekū has other Betsugū: {{ill|Taka-no-Miya|ja|多賀宮}} and {{ill|Tsuchi-no-Miya|ja|土宮}} within the precincts, and {{ill|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|ja|月夜見宮|8=ja|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya}} outside the precincts. Kazamiya was the last to be designated as a Betsugū in {{ill|1293|ja|1293年}} (Shōō 6) and is ranked fourth among the four Betsugū.
Originally, it was known as **Kazeya-shiro** (風社) or **Kazejinja** (風神社), a shrine of lesser rank. However, following the {{ill|Mongol invasions of Japan|ja|元寇}} in {{ill|1281|ja|1281年}} (Kōan 4), when divine winds (*kamikaze*) protected Japan, it was elevated to the status of Betsugū.
== Enshrined Deities == The enshrined deities of {{ill|Kaze-no-Miya|ja|風宮}} are {{ill|Shinatsuhiko|ja|シナツヒコ}} and {{ill|Shinatobe|ja|シナトベ}}, gods who govern wind and rain. Originally worshipped as deities bringing favorable weather for agriculture, after the Mongol invasions, they became objects of prayers for the salvation of Japan in times of national crisis.
== History == The origins of Kaze-no-Miya are unclear, but the earliest reference appears in the "Chōtoku Kenroku" from {{ill|997|ja|997年}} (Chōtoku 3), citing it as "Kaze-sha," ranked 4th among 32 rice field shrines under the jurisdiction of the Gekū, as noted in {{ill|Watarai Ieyuki|ja|度会家行}}'s "Ruiju Jingi Hongen." "Rice field shrines" correspond to the current末社 (massha), considered lower than sessha (auxiliary shrines).
Although initially ranked as a末社, due to its deities’ importance in agriculture, the {{ill|Kazahi-no-Matsuri|ja|風日祈祭}} was held, and offerings of "kakechikara" (bundled rice stalks) were made during the {{ill|Kannamesai|ja|神嘗祭}}.
During the {{ill|Mongol invasions of Japan|ja|元寇}} in {{ill|1281|ja|1281年}} (Kōan 4), the court dispatched the Dainagon Tameuji to perform prayers at both the Naikū's Kaze-sha and this shrine. After the retreat of the Mongol forces, the victory was attributed to the "Divine Wind" ({{ill|Kamikaze|ja|神風}}), and in {{ill|1293|ja|1293年}} (Shōō 6), the shrine was elevated to a betsu-gū (detached shrine), named {{ill|Kazahi-no-Miya|ja|風日祈宮}} and Kaze-no-Miya respectively.
During the late {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, when Western nations visited Japan, the court held 15 days of prayers for the expulsion of foreigners at Kaze-no-Miya and Kazahi-no-Miya in {{ill|1863|ja|1863年}} (Bunkyū 3).
In {{ill|1876|ja|1876年}} (Meiji 9), during the {{ill|Ise Riot|ja|伊勢暴動}}, the sacred object from {{ill|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|ja|月夜見宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya}} was temporarily moved to Kaze-no-Miya to protect it from a nearby fire.<ref>Kaneko (1983): p.214</ref>
== Festivals == The {{ill|Kazahi-no-Matsuri|ja|風日祈祭}}, praying for favorable weather and abundant harvests, is held annually on May 14 and August 4.
== Shrine Building == The shrine building of Kaze-no-Miya faces south and is constructed in the {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|ja|神明造}} style with a thatched roof, featuring {{ill|Chigi and Katsuogi|ja|千木・鰹木|lt=chigi and katsuogi}} with five odd-numbered katsuogi, following the Gekū’s design. The old shrine site for reconstruction (shikinen sengū) is adjacent to the east and west.
== Access == * Nearest Station: About a 12-minute walk from {{ill|Ise-shi Station|ja|伊勢市駅}} via {{ill|JR Sangu Line|ja|参宮線}} and {{ill|Kintetsu|ja|近畿日本鉄道}}. * Nearest Bus Stop: About a 5-minute walk from {{ill|Mie Kotsu|ja|三重交通}} Gekū-mae Bus Stop. * Nearest Interchange: About 2 km from {{ill|Ise Nishi Interchange|ja|伊勢西インターチェンジ}} on the {{ill|Ise Expressway|ja|伊勢自動車道}}. * Parking: Free parking available.
== References == * Kaneko Nobuo. *Tamaki Town History, Volume II – History and Traditions of South Ise.* Mie Prefecture Local History Series No. 91, Mie Prefecture Local History Publication Association, December 10, 1983. * {{ill|Katsunoshin Sakurai|ja|桜井勝之進}}. *Ise Grand Shrine.* Gakuseisha, May 20, 1969. * Katsunoshin Sakurai. *The Shrine of the Great deity of Ise.* Hori Shoten, March 25, 1973.
== Related Pages == {{commonscat|Kaze-no-miya (Geku)}} * {{ill|Shinatsuhiko|ja|シナツヒコ}} * {{ill|Kamikaze|ja|神風}} * {{ill|Mongol invasions of Japan|ja|元寇}} * {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮}} * {{ill|List of Shrines and Facilities of Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮を構成する神社と施設|1=List of 125 Shrines of Ise}}
== External Links == * [http://www.isejingu.or.jp/gegu/gegu5.htm Ise Jingu Official Site]

== Enshrined Deities == The enshrined deities of {{ill|Kaze-no-Miya|ja|風宮}} are {{ill|Shinatsuhiko|ja|シナツヒコ}} and {{ill|Shinatobe|ja|シナトベ}}, gods who govern wind and rain. Originally worshipped as deities bringing favorable weather for agriculture, after the Mongol invasions, they became objects of prayers for the salvation of Japan in times of national crisis.
== History == The origins of Kaze-no-Miya are unclear, but the earliest reference appears in the "Chōtoku Kenroku" from {{ill|997|ja|997年}} (Chōtoku 3), citing it as "Kaze-sha," ranked 4th among 32 rice field shrines under the jurisdiction of the Gekū, as noted in {{ill|Watarai Ieyuki|ja|度会家行}}'s "Ruiju Jingi Hongen." "Rice field shrines" correspond to the current末社 (massha), considered lower than sessha (auxiliary shrines).
Although initially ranked as a末社, due to its deities’ importance in agriculture, the {{ill|Kazahi-no-Matsuri|ja|風日祈祭}} was held, and offerings of "kakechikara" (bundled rice stalks) were made during the {{ill|Kannamesai|ja|神嘗祭}}.
During the {{ill|Mongol invasions of Japan|ja|元寇}} in {{ill|1281|ja|1281年}} (Kōan 4), the court dispatched the Dainagon Tameuji to perform prayers at both the Naikū's Kaze-sha and this shrine. After the retreat of the Mongol forces, the victory was attributed to the "Divine Wind" ({{ill|Kamikaze|ja|神風}}), and in {{ill|1293|ja|1293年}} (Shōō 6), the shrine was elevated to a betsu-gū (detached shrine), named {{ill|Kazahi-no-Miya|ja|風日祈宮}} and Kaze-no-Miya respectively.
During the late {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, when Western nations visited Japan, the court held 15 days of prayers for the expulsion of foreigners at Kaze-no-Miya and Kazahi-no-Miya in {{ill|1863|ja|1863年}} (Bunkyū 3).
In {{ill|1876|ja|1876年}} (Meiji 9), during the {{ill|Ise Riot|ja|伊勢暴動}}, the sacred object from {{ill|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|ja|月夜見宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya}} was temporarily moved to Kaze-no-Miya to protect it from a nearby fire.<ref>Kaneko (1983): p.214</ref>
== Festivals == The {{ill|Kazahi-no-Matsuri|ja|風日祈祭}}, praying for favorable weather and abundant harvests, is held annually on May 14 and August 4.
== Shrine Building == The shrine building of Kaze-no-Miya faces south and is constructed in the {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|ja|神明造}} style with a thatched roof, featuring {{ill|Chigi and Katsuogi|ja|千木・鰹木|lt=chigi and katsuogi}} with five odd-numbered katsuogi, following the Gekū’s design. The old shrine site for reconstruction (shikinen sengū) is adjacent to the east and west.
== Access == * Nearest Station: About a 12-minute walk from {{ill|Ise-shi Station|ja|伊勢市駅}} via {{ill|JR Sangu Line|ja|参宮線}} and {{ill|Kintetsu|ja|近畿日本鉄道}}. * Nearest Bus Stop: About a 5-minute walk from {{ill|Mie Kotsu|ja|三重交通}} Gekū-mae Bus Stop. * Nearest Interchange: About 2 km from {{ill|Ise Nishi Interchange|ja|伊勢西インターチェンジ}} on the {{ill|Ise Expressway|ja|伊勢自動車道}}. * Parking: Free parking available.
== References == * Kaneko Nobuo. *Tamaki Town History, Volume II – History and Traditions of South Ise.* Mie Prefecture Local History Series No. 91, Mie Prefecture Local History Publication Association, December 10, 1983. * {{ill|Katsunoshin Sakurai|ja|桜井勝之進}}. *Ise Grand Shrine.* Gakuseisha, May 20, 1969. * Katsunoshin Sakurai. *The Shrine of the Great deity of Ise.* Hori Shoten, March 25, 1973.
== Related Pages == {{commonscat|Kaze-no-miya (Geku)}} * {{ill|Shinatsuhiko|ja|シナツヒコ}} * {{ill|Kamikaze|ja|神風}} * {{ill|Mongol invasions of Japan|ja|元寇}} * {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮}} * {{ill|List of Shrines and Facilities of Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮を構成する神社と施設|1=List of 125 Shrines of Ise}}
== External Links == * [http://www.isejingu.or.jp/gegu/gegu5.htm Ise Jingu Official Site]

{{Infobox shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[image:Ise-Shrine Geku-Kazenomiya.jpg|256px|Kazenomiya]] | Name = Kazenomiya | location = Toyokawa-cho, [[Ise]], [[Mie Prefecture]], Japan | deity = [[Shinatsuhiko]] and [[Shinatobe]] | type = {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮}} auxiliary shrine (*betsugū*) | Established = Before 997 CE | Main Hall = [[Shinmei-zukuri]] | coordinates = {{coord|34|29|9.1|N|136|42|14.2|E|region:JP|display=inline,title}} | festival = Kazahi-no-Matsuri (*Wind Day Prayer Festival*) }}
'''Kaze-no-Miya''' (風宮) is an auxiliary shrine (*betsugū*) located within the precincts of {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮}} of the [[Ise Grand Shrine]] in [[Toyokawa-cho]], [[Ise]], [[Mie Prefecture]], Japan.
== Overview== [[File:Kame-ish 01.JPG|left|thumb|180px|Kame-ishi (Turtle Stone)]] '''Kazenomiya''' is a {{ill|Betsugū (detached shrine)|ja|別宮}} of the {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮}} (Gekū), located at the foot of **Hinokioyama** (檜尾山), south of the main shrine. The enshrined deities are {{ill|Shinatsuhiko|ja|級長津彦命}} and {{ill|Shinatobe|ja|級長戸辺命}}, the same as those in the {{ill|Kazahinomi-no-Miya|ja|風日祈宮}}, a detached shrine of the {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮}} (Naikū).
'''Kazenomiya''' is situated to the left after crossing the **Kame-ishi** (亀石), a stone along the approach to {{ill|Taga-no-Miya|ja|多賀宮}}, next to the pond in front of the main shrine. It is said that Kame-ishi was transported from the entrance of the **Ama-no-Iwato** (天岩戸) on {{ill|Mount Takakura (Ise)|ja|高倉山 (伊勢市)}}.
The term *Betsugū* (別宮) means "separate shrine," considered second in reverence only to the main shrine. Apart from '''Kazenomiya''', the Gekū has other Betsugū: {{ill|Taka-no-Miya|ja|多賀宮}} and {{ill|Tsuchi-no-Miya|ja|土宮}} within the precincts, and {{ill|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|ja|月夜見宮|8=ja|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya}} outside the precincts. Kazamiya was the last to be designated as a Betsugū in {{ill|1293|ja|1293年}} (Shōō 6) and is ranked fourth among the four Betsugū.
Originally, it was known as **Kazeya-shiro** (風社) or **Kazejinja** (風神社), a shrine of lesser rank. However, following the {{ill|Mongol invasions of Japan|ja|元寇}} in {{ill|1281|ja|1281年}} (Kōan 4), when divine winds (*kamikaze*) protected Japan, it was elevated to the status of Betsugū.

In {{ill|1876|ja|1876年}} (Meiji 9), during the {{ill|Ise Riot|ja|伊勢暴動}}, the sacred object from {{ill|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|ja|月夜見宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya}} was temporarily moved to Kaze-no-Miya to protect it from a nearby fire.<ref>Kaneko (1983): p.214</ref>

{{Infobox shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[image:Ise-Shrine Geku-Kazenomiya.jpg|256px|Kazenomiya]] | Name = Kazenomiya | location = Toyokawa-cho, [[Ise]], [[Mie Prefecture]], Japan | deity = [[Shinatsuhiko]] and [[Shinatobe]] | type = {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮}} auxiliary shrine (*betsugū*) | Established = Before 997 CE | Main Hall = [[Shinmei-zukuri]] | coordinates = {{coord|34|29|9.1|N|136|42|14.2|E|region:JP|display=inline,title}} | festival = Kazahi-no-Matsuri (*Wind Day Prayer Festival*) }}
'''Kaze-no-Miya''' (風宮) is an auxiliary shrine (*betsugū*) located within the precincts of {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮}} of the [[Ise Grand Shrine]] in [[Toyokawa-cho]], [[Ise]], [[Mie Prefecture]], Japan.
== Overview== [[File:Kame-ish 01.JPG|left|thumb|180px|Kame-ishi (Turtle Stone)]] '''Kazenomiya''' is a {{ill|Betsugū (detached shrine)|ja|別宮}} of the {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮}} (Gekū), located at the foot of **Hinokioyama** (檜尾山), south of the main shrine. The enshrined deities are {{ill|Shinatsuhiko|ja|級長津彦命}} and {{ill|Shinatobe|ja|級長戸辺命}}, the same as those in the {{ill|Kazahinomi-no-Miya|ja|風日祈宮}}, a detached shrine of the {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮}} (Naikū).
'''Kazenomiya''' is situated to the left after crossing the **Kame-ishi** (亀石), a stone along the approach to {{ill|Taga-no-Miya|ja|多賀宮}}, next to the pond in front of the main shrine. It is said that Kame-ishi was transported from the entrance of the **Ama-no-Iwato** (天岩戸) on {{ill|Mount Takakura (Ise)|ja|高倉山 (伊勢市)}}.
The term *Betsugū* (別宮) means "separate shrine," considered second in reverence only to the main shrine. Apart from '''Kazenomiya''', the Gekū has other Betsugū: {{ill|Taka-no-Miya|ja|多賀宮}} and {{ill|Tsuchi-no-Miya|ja|土宮}} within the precincts, and {{ill|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|ja|月夜見宮|8=ja|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya}} outside the precincts. Kazamiya was the last to be designated as a Betsugū in {{ill|1293|ja|1293年}} (Shōō 6) and is ranked fourth among the four Betsugū.
Originally, it was known as **Kazeya-shiro** (風社) or **Kazejinja** (風神社), a shrine of lesser rank. However, following the {{ill|Mongol invasions of Japan|ja|元寇}} in {{ill|1281|ja|1281年}} (Kōan 4), when divine winds (*kamikaze*) protected Japan, it was elevated to the status of Betsugū.
== Enshrined Deities == The enshrined deities of {{ill|Kaze-no-Miya|ja|風宮}} are {{ill|Shinatsuhiko|ja|シナツヒコ}} and {{ill|Shinatobe|ja|シナトベ}}, gods who govern wind and rain. Originally worshipped as deities bringing favorable weather for agriculture, after the Mongol invasions, they became objects of prayers for the salvation of Japan in times of national crisis.
== History == The origins of Kaze-no-Miya are unclear, but the earliest reference appears in the "Chōtoku Kenroku" from {{ill|997|ja|997年}} (Chōtoku 3), citing it as "Kaze-sha," ranked 4th among 32 rice field shrines under the jurisdiction of the Gekū, as noted in {{ill|Watarai Ieyuki|ja|度会家行}}'s "Ruiju Jingi Hongen." "Rice field shrines" correspond to the current末社 (massha), considered lower than sessha (auxiliary shrines).
Although initially ranked as a末社, due to its deities’ importance in agriculture, the {{ill|Kazahi-no-Matsuri|ja|風日祈祭}} was held, and offerings of "kakechikara" (bundled rice stalks) were made during the {{ill|Kannamesai|ja|神嘗祭}}.
During the {{ill|Mongol invasions of Japan|ja|元寇}} in {{ill|1281|ja|1281年}} (Kōan 4), the court dispatched the Dainagon Tameuji to perform prayers at both the Naikū's Kaze-sha and this shrine. After the retreat of the Mongol forces, the victory was attributed to the "Divine Wind" ({{ill|Kamikaze|ja|神風}}), and in {{ill|1293|ja|1293年}} (Shōō 6), the shrine was elevated to a betsu-gū (detached shrine), named {{ill|Kazahi-no-Miya|ja|風日祈宮}} and Kaze-no-Miya respectively.
During the late {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, when Western nations visited Japan, the court held 15 days of prayers for the expulsion of foreigners at Kaze-no-Miya and Kazahi-no-Miya in {{ill|1863|ja|1863年}} (Bunkyū 3).
In {{ill|1876|ja|1876年}} (Meiji 9), during the {{ill|Ise Riot|ja|伊勢暴動}}, the sacred object from {{ill|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|ja|月夜見宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya}} was temporarily moved to Kaze-no-Miya to protect it from a nearby fire.<ref>Kaneko (1983): p.214</ref>
== Festivals == The {{ill|Kazahi-no-Matsuri|ja|風日祈祭}}, praying for favorable weather and abundant harvests, is held annually on May 14 and August 4.
== Shrine Building == The shrine building of Kaze-no-Miya faces south and is constructed in the {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|ja|神明造}} style with a thatched roof, featuring {{ill|Chigi and Katsuogi|ja|千木・鰹木|lt=chigi and katsuogi}} with five odd-numbered katsuogi, following the Gekū’s design. The old shrine site for reconstruction (shikinen sengū) is adjacent to the east and west.
== Access == * Nearest Station: About a 12-minute walk from {{ill|Ise-shi Station|ja|伊勢市駅}} via {{ill|JR Sangu Line|ja|参宮線}} and {{ill|Kintetsu|ja|近畿日本鉄道}}. * Nearest Bus Stop: About a 5-minute walk from {{ill|Mie Kotsu|ja|三重交通}} Gekū-mae Bus Stop. * Nearest Interchange: About 2 km from {{ill|Ise Nishi Interchange|ja|伊勢西インターチェンジ}} on the {{ill|Ise Expressway|ja|伊勢自動車道}}. * Parking: Free parking available.
== References == * Kaneko Nobuo. *Tamaki Town History, Volume II – History and Traditions of South Ise.* Mie Prefecture Local History Series No. 91, Mie Prefecture Local History Publication Association, December 10, 1983. * {{ill|Katsunoshin Sakurai|ja|桜井勝之進}}. *Ise Grand Shrine.* Gakuseisha, May 20, 1969. * Katsunoshin Sakurai. *The Shrine of the Great deity of Ise.* Hori Shoten, March 25, 1973.
== Related Pages == {{commonscat|Kaze-no-miya (Geku)}} * {{ill|Shinatsuhiko|ja|シナツヒコ}} * {{ill|Kamikaze|ja|神風}} * {{ill|Mongol invasions of Japan|ja|元寇}} * {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮}} * {{ill|List of Shrines and Facilities of Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮を構成する神社と施設|1=List of 125 Shrines of Ise}}
== External Links == * [http://www.isejingu.or.jp/gegu/gegu5.htm Ise Jingu Official Site]

== Access == * Nearest Station: About a 12-minute walk from {{ill|Ise-shi Station|ja|伊勢市駅}} via {{ill|JR Sangu Line|ja|参宮線}} and {{ill|Kintetsu|ja|近畿日本鉄道}}. * Nearest Bus Stop: About a 5-minute walk from {{ill|Mie Kotsu|ja|三重交通}} Gekū-mae Bus Stop. * Nearest Interchange: About 2 km from {{ill|Ise Nishi Interchange|ja|伊勢西インターチェンジ}} on the {{ill|Ise Expressway|ja|伊勢自動車道}}. * Parking: Free parking available.
== References == * Kaneko Nobuo. *Tamaki Town History, Volume II – History and Traditions of South Ise.* Mie Prefecture Local History Series No. 91, Mie Prefecture Local History Publication Association, December 10, 1983. * {{ill|Katsunoshin Sakurai|ja|桜井勝之進}}. *Ise Grand Shrine.* Gakuseisha, May 20, 1969. * Katsunoshin Sakurai. *The Shrine of the Great deity of Ise.* Hori Shoten, March 25, 1973.
== Related Pages == {{commonscat|Kaze-no-miya (Geku)}} * {{ill|Shinatsuhiko|ja|シナツヒコ}} * {{ill|Kamikaze|ja|神風}} * {{ill|Mongol invasions of Japan|ja|元寇}} * {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮}} * {{ill|List of Shrines and Facilities of Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮を構成する神社と施設|1=List of 125 Shrines of Ise}}

{{Infobox shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[image:Ise-Shrine Geku-Kazenomiya.jpg|256px|Kazenomiya]] | Name = Kazenomiya | location = Toyokawa-cho, [[Ise]], [[Mie Prefecture]], Japan | deity = [[Shinatsuhiko]] and [[Shinatobe]] | type = {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮}} auxiliary shrine (*betsugū*) | Established = Before 997 CE | Main Hall = [[Shinmei-zukuri]] | coordinates = {{coord|34|29|9.1|N|136|42|14.2|E|region:JP|display=inline,title}} | festival = Kazahi-no-Matsuri (*Wind Day Prayer Festival*) }}
'''Kaze-no-Miya''' (風宮) is an auxiliary shrine (*betsugū*) located within the precincts of {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮}} of the [[Ise Grand Shrine]] in [[Toyokawa-cho]], [[Ise]], [[Mie Prefecture]], Japan.
== Overview== [[File:Kame-ish 01.JPG|left|thumb|180px|Kame-ishi (Turtle Stone)]] '''Kazenomiya''' is a {{ill|Betsugū (detached shrine)|ja|別宮}} of the {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮}} (Gekū), located at the foot of **Hinokioyama** (檜尾山), south of the main shrine. The enshrined deities are {{ill|Shinatsuhiko|ja|級長津彦命}} and {{ill|Shinatobe|ja|級長戸辺命}}, the same as those in the {{ill|Kazahinomi-no-Miya|ja|風日祈宮}}, a detached shrine of the {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮}} (Naikū).
'''Kazenomiya''' is situated to the left after crossing the **Kame-ishi** (亀石), a stone along the approach to {{ill|Taga-no-Miya|ja|多賀宮}}, next to the pond in front of the main shrine. It is said that Kame-ishi was transported from the entrance of the **Ama-no-Iwato** (天岩戸) on {{ill|Mount Takakura (Ise)|ja|高倉山 (伊勢市)}}.
The term *Betsugū* (別宮) means "separate shrine," considered second in reverence only to the main shrine. Apart from '''Kazenomiya''', the Gekū has other Betsugū: {{ill|Taka-no-Miya|ja|多賀宮}} and {{ill|Tsuchi-no-Miya|ja|土宮}} within the precincts, and {{ill|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|ja|月夜見宮|8=ja|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya}} outside the precincts. Kazamiya was the last to be designated as a Betsugū in {{ill|1293|ja|1293rd year }} (Shōō 6) and is ranked fourth among the four Betsugū.
Originally, it was known as **Kazeya-shiro** (風社) or **Kazejinja** (風神社), a shrine of lesser rank. However, following the {{ill|Mongol invasions of Japan|ja|元寇}} in {{ill|1281|ja|1281st year }} (Kōan 4), when divine winds (*kamikaze*) protected Japan, it was elevated to the status of Betsugū.
== Enshrined Deities == The enshrined deities of {{ill|Kaze-no-Miya|ja|風宮}} are {{ill|Shinatsuhiko|ja|シナツヒコ}} and {{ill|Shinatobe|ja|シナトベ}}, gods who govern wind and rain. Originally worshipped as deities bringing favorable weather for agriculture, after the Mongol invasions, they became objects of prayers for the salvation of Japan in times of national crisis.
== History == The origins of Kaze-no-Miya are unclear, but the earliest reference appears in the "Chōtoku Kenroku" from {{ill|997|ja|997th year }} (Chōtoku 3), citing it as "Kaze-sha," ranked 4th among 32 rice field shrines under the jurisdiction of the Gekū, as noted in {{ill|Watarai Ieyuki|ja|度会家行}}'s "Ruiju Jingi Hongen." "Rice field shrines" correspond to the current末社 (massha), considered lower than sessha (auxiliary shrines).
Although initially ranked as a末社, due to its deities’ importance in agriculture, the {{ill|Kazahi-no-Matsuri|ja|風日祈祭}} was held, and offerings of "kakechikara" (bundled rice stalks) were made during the {{ill|Kannamesai|ja|神嘗祭}}.
During the {{ill|Mongol invasions of Japan|ja|元寇}} in {{ill|1281|ja|1281st year }} (Kōan 4), the court dispatched the Dainagon Tameuji to perform prayers at both the Naikū's Kaze-sha and this shrine. After the retreat of the Mongol forces, the victory was attributed to the "Divine Wind" ({{ill|Kamikaze|ja|神風}}), and in {{ill|1293|ja|1293rd year }} (Shōō 6), the shrine was elevated to a betsu-gū (detached shrine), named {{ill|Kazahi-no-Miya|ja|風日祈宮}} and Kaze-no-Miya respectively.
During the late {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, when Western nations visited Japan, the court held 15 days of prayers for the expulsion of foreigners at Kaze-no-Miya and Kazahi-no-Miya in {{ill|1863|ja|1863rd year }} (Bunkyū 3).
In {{ill|1876|ja|1876年}} (Meiji 9), during the {{ill|Ise Riot|ja|伊勢暴動}}, the sacred object from {{ill|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|ja|月夜見宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya}} was temporarily moved to Kaze-no-Miya to protect it from a nearby fire.<ref>Kaneko (1983): p.214</ref>
== Festivals == The {{ill|Kazahi-no-Matsuri|ja|風日祈祭}}, praying for favorable weather and abundant harvests, is held annually on May 14 and August 4.
== Shrine Building == The shrine building of Kaze-no-Miya faces south and is constructed in the {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|ja|神明造}} style with a thatched roof, featuring {{ill|Chigi and Katsuogi|ja|千木・鰹木|lt=chigi and katsuogi}} with five odd-numbered katsuogi, following the Gekū’s design. The old shrine site for reconstruction (shikinen sengū) is adjacent to the east and west.
== Access == * Nearest Station: About a 12-minute walk from {{ill|Ise-shi Station|ja|伊勢市駅}} via {{ill|JR Sangu Line|ja|参宮線}} and {{ill|Kintetsu|ja|近畿日本鉄道}}. * Nearest Bus Stop: About a 5-minute walk from {{ill|Mie Kotsu|ja|三重交通}} Gekū-mae Bus Stop. * Nearest Interchange: About 2 km from {{ill|Ise Nishi Interchange|ja|伊勢西インターチェンジ}} on the {{ill|Ise Expressway|ja|伊勢自動車道}}. * Parking: Free parking available.
== References == * Kaneko Nobuo. *Tamaki Town History, Volume II – History and Traditions of South Ise.* Mie Prefecture Local History Series No. 91, Mie Prefecture Local History Publication Association, December 10, 1983. * {{ill|Katsunoshin Sakurai|ja|桜井勝之進}}. *Ise Grand Shrine.* Gakuseisha, May 20, 1969. * Katsunoshin Sakurai. *The Shrine of the Great deity of Ise.* Hori Shoten, March 25, 1973.

== Enshrined Deities == The enshrined deities of {{ill|Kaze-no-Miya|ja|風宮}} are {{ill|Shinatsuhiko|ja|シナツヒコ}} and {{ill|Shinatobe|ja|シナトベ}}, gods who govern wind and rain. Originally worshipped as deities bringing favorable weather for agriculture, after the Mongol invasions, they became objects of prayers for the salvation of Japan in times of national crisis.
== History == The origins of Kaze-no-Miya are unclear, but the earliest reference appears in the "Chōtoku Kenroku" from {{ill|997|ja|997th year }} (Chōtoku 3), citing it as "Kaze-sha," ranked 4th among 32 rice field shrines under the jurisdiction of the Gekū, as noted in {{ill|Watarai Ieyuki|ja|度会家行}}'s "Ruiju Jingi Hongen." "Rice field shrines" correspond to the current末社 (massha), considered lower than sessha (auxiliary shrines).
Although initially ranked as a末社, due to its deities’ importance in agriculture, the {{ill|Kazahi-no-Matsuri|ja|風日祈祭}} was held, and offerings of "kakechikara" (bundled rice stalks) were made during the {{ill|Kannamesai|ja|神嘗祭}}.
During the {{ill|Mongol invasions of Japan|ja|元寇}} in {{ill|1281|ja|1281st year }} (Kōan 4), the court dispatched the Dainagon Tameuji to perform prayers at both the Naikū's Kaze-sha and this shrine. After the retreat of the Mongol forces, the victory was attributed to the "Divine Wind" ({{ill|Kamikaze|ja|神風}}), and in {{ill|1293|ja|1293rd year }} (Shōō 6), the shrine was elevated to a betsu-gū (detached shrine), named {{ill|Kazahi-no-Miya|ja|風日祈宮}} and Kaze-no-Miya respectively.
During the late {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, when Western nations visited Japan, the court held 15 days of prayers for the expulsion of foreigners at Kaze-no-Miya and Kazahi-no-Miya in {{ill|1863|ja|1863rd year }} (Bunkyū 3).
In {{ill|1876|ja|1876th year }} (Meiji 9), during the {{ill|Ise Riot|ja|伊勢暴動}}, the sacred object from {{ill|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|ja|月夜見宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya}} was temporarily moved to Kaze-no-Miya to protect it from a nearby fire.<ref>Kaneko (1983): p.214</ref>
== Festivals == The {{ill|Kazahi-no-Matsuri|ja|風日祈祭}}, praying for favorable weather and abundant harvests, is held annually on May 14 and August 4.
== Shrine Building == The shrine building of Kaze-no-Miya faces south and is constructed in the {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|ja|神明造}} style with a thatched roof, featuring {{ill|Chigi and Katsuogi|ja|千木・鰹木|lt=chigi and katsuogi}} with five odd-numbered katsuogi, following the Gekū’s design. The old shrine site for reconstruction (shikinen sengū) is adjacent to the east and west.
== Access == * Nearest Station: About a 12-minute walk from {{ill|Ise-shi Station|ja|伊勢市駅}} via {{ill|JR Sangu Line|ja|参宮線}} and {{ill|Kintetsu|ja|近畿日本鉄道}}. * Nearest Bus Stop: About a 5-minute walk from {{ill|Mie Kotsu|ja|三重交通}} Gekū-mae Bus Stop. * Nearest Interchange: About 2 km from {{ill|Ise Nishi Interchange|ja|伊勢西インターチェンジ}} on the {{ill|Ise Expressway|ja|伊勢自動車道}}. * Parking: Free parking available.
== References == * Kaneko Nobuo. *Tamaki Town History, Volume II – History and Traditions of South Ise.* Mie Prefecture Local History Series No. 91, Mie Prefecture Local History Publication Association, December 10, 1983. * {{ill|Katsunoshin Sakurai|ja|桜井勝之進}}. *Ise Grand Shrine.* Gakuseisha, May 20, 1969. * Katsunoshin Sakurai. *The Shrine of the Great deity of Ise.* Hori Shoten, March 25, 1973.
== Related Pages == {{commonscat|Kaze-no-miya (Geku)}} * {{ill|Shinatsuhiko|ja|シナツヒコ}} * {{ill|Kamikaze|ja|神風}} * {{ill|Mongol invasions of Japan|ja|元寇}} * {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮}} * {{ill|List of Shrines and Facilities of Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮を構成する神社と施設|1=List of 125 Shrines of Ise}}
== External Links == * [http://www.isejingu.or.jp/gegu/gegu5.htm Ise Jingu Official Site]

{{Infobox shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[image:Ise-Shrine Geku-Kazenomiya.jpg|256px|Kazenomiya]] | Name = Kazenomiya | location = Toyokawa-cho, [[Ise]], [[Mie Prefecture]], Japan | deity = [[Shinatsuhiko]] and [[Shinatobe]] | type = {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮}} auxiliary shrine (*betsugū*) | Established = Before 997 CE | Main Hall = [[Shinmei-zukuri]] | coordinates = {{coord|34|29|9.1|N|136|42|14.2|E|region:JP|display=inline,title}} | festival = Kazahi-no-Matsuri (*Wind Day Prayer Festival*) }}
'''Kaze-no-Miya''' (風宮) is an auxiliary shrine (*betsugū*) located within the precincts of {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮}} of the [[Ise Grand Shrine]] in [[Toyokawa-cho]], [[Ise]], [[Mie Prefecture]], Japan.
== Overview== [[File:Kame-ish 01.JPG|left|thumb|180px|Kame-ishi (Turtle Stone)]] '''Kazenomiya''' is a {{ill|Betsugū (detached shrine)|ja|別宮}} of the {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮}} (Gekū), located at the foot of **Hinokioyama** (檜尾山), south of the main shrine. The enshrined deities are {{ill|Shinatsuhiko|ja|級長津彦命}} and {{ill|Shinatobe|ja|級長戸辺命}}, the same as those in the {{ill|Kazahinomi-no-Miya|ja|風日祈宮}}, a detached shrine of the {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮}} (Naikū).
'''Kazenomiya''' is situated to the left after crossing the **Kame-ishi** (亀石), a stone along the approach to {{ill|Taga-no-Miya|ja|多賀宮}}, next to the pond in front of the main shrine. It is said that Kame-ishi was transported from the entrance of the **Ama-no-Iwato** (天岩戸) on {{ill|Mount Takakura (Ise)|ja|高倉山 (伊勢市)}}.
The term *Betsugū* (別宮) means "separate shrine," considered second in reverence only to the main shrine. Apart from '''Kazenomiya''', the Gekū has other Betsugū: {{ill|Taka-no-Miya|ja|多賀宮}} and {{ill|Tsuchi-no-Miya|ja|土宮}} within the precincts, and {{ill|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|ja|月夜見宮|8=ja|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya}} outside the precincts. Kazamiya was the last to be designated as a Betsugū in {{ill|1293|ja|1293rd year }} (Shōō 6) and is ranked fourth among the four Betsugū.
Originally, it was known as **Kazeya-shiro** (風社) or **Kazejinja** (風神社), a shrine of lesser rank. However, following the {{ill|Mongol invasions of Japan|ja|元寇}} in {{ill|1281|ja|1281st year }} (Kōan 4), when divine winds (*kamikaze*) protected Japan, it was elevated to the status of Betsugū.

{{Infobox shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[image:Ise-Shrine Geku-Kazenomiya.jpg|256px|Kazenomiya]] | Name = Kazenomiya | location = Toyokawa-cho, [[Ise]], [[Mie Prefecture]], Japan | deity = [[Shinatsuhiko]] and [[Shinatobe]] | type = {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|12=draft}} auxiliary shrine (*betsugū*) | Established = Before 997 CE | Main Hall = [[Shinmei-zukuri]] | coordinates = {{coord|34|29|9.1|N|136|42|14.2|E|region:JP|display=inline,title}} | festival = Kazahi-no-Matsuri (*Wind Day Prayer Festival*) }}
'''Kaze-no-Miya''' (風宮) is an auxiliary shrine (*betsugū*) located within the precincts of {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|12=draft}} of the [[Ise Grand Shrine]] in [[Toyokawa-cho]], [[Ise]], [[Mie Prefecture]], Japan.
== Overview== [[File:Kame-ish 01.JPG|left|thumb|180px|Kame-ishi (Turtle Stone)]] '''Kazenomiya''' is a {{ill|Betsugū (detached shrine)|ja|別宮|12=draft}} of the {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|12=draft}} (Gekū), located at the foot of **Hinokioyama** (檜尾山), south of the main shrine. The enshrined deities are {{ill|Shinatsuhiko|ja|級長津彦命|12=draft}} and {{ill|Shinatobe|ja|級長戸辺命|12=draft}}, the same as those in the {{ill|Kazahinomi-no-Miya|ja|風日祈宮|12=draft}}, a detached shrine of the {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|12=draft}} (Naikū).
'''Kazenomiya''' is situated to the left after crossing the **Kame-ishi** (亀石), a stone along the approach to {{ill|Taga-no-Miya|ja|多賀宮|12=draft}}, next to the pond in front of the main shrine. It is said that Kame-ishi was transported from the entrance of the **Ama-no-Iwato** (天岩戸) on {{ill|Mount Takakura (Ise)|ja|高倉山 (伊勢市)|12=draft}}.
The term *Betsugū* (別宮) means "separate shrine," considered second in reverence only to the main shrine. Apart from '''Kazenomiya''', the Gekū has other Betsugū: {{ill|Taka-no-Miya|ja|多賀宮|12=draft}} and {{ill|Tsuchi-no-Miya|ja|土宮|12=draft}} within the precincts, and {{ill|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|ja|月夜見宮|8=ja|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|12=draft}} outside the precincts. Kazamiya was the last to be designated as a Betsugū in {{ill|1293|ja|1293rd year|12=draft}} (Shōō 6) and is ranked fourth among the four Betsugū.
Originally, it was known as **Kazeya-shiro** (風社) or **Kazejinja** (風神社), a shrine of lesser rank. However, following the {{ill|Mongol invasions of Japan|ja|元寇|12=draft}} in {{ill|1281|ja|1281st year|12=draft}} (Kōan 4), when divine winds (*kamikaze*) protected Japan, it was elevated to the status of Betsugū.
== Enshrined Deities == The enshrined deities of {{ill|Kaze-no-Miya|ja|風宮|12=draft}} are {{ill|Shinatsuhiko|ja|シナツヒコ|12=draft}} and {{ill|Shinatobe|ja|シナトベ|12=draft}}, gods who govern wind and rain. Originally worshipped as deities bringing favorable weather for agriculture, after the Mongol invasions, they became objects of prayers for the salvation of Japan in times of national crisis.
== History == The origins of Kaze-no-Miya are unclear, but the earliest reference appears in the "Chōtoku Kenroku" from {{ill|997|ja|997th year|12=draft}} (Chōtoku 3), citing it as "Kaze-sha," ranked 4th among 32 rice field shrines under the jurisdiction of the Gekū, as noted in {{ill|Watarai Ieyuki|ja|度会家行|12=draft}}'s "Ruiju Jingi Hongen." "Rice field shrines" correspond to the current末社 (massha), considered lower than sessha (auxiliary shrines).
Although initially ranked as a末社, due to its deities’ importance in agriculture, the {{ill|Kazahi-no-Matsuri|ja|風日祈祭|12=draft}} was held, and offerings of "kakechikara" (bundled rice stalks) were made during the {{ill|Kannamesai|ja|神嘗祭|12=draft}}.
During the {{ill|Mongol invasions of Japan|ja|元寇|12=draft}} in {{ill|1281|ja|1281st year|12=draft}} (Kōan 4), the court dispatched the Dainagon Tameuji to perform prayers at both the Naikū's Kaze-sha and this shrine. After the retreat of the Mongol forces, the victory was attributed to the "Divine Wind" ({{ill|Kamikaze|ja|神風|12=draft}}), and in {{ill|1293|ja|1293rd year|12=draft}} (Shōō 6), the shrine was elevated to a betsu-gū (detached shrine), named {{ill|Kazahi-no-Miya|ja|風日祈宮|12=draft}} and Kaze-no-Miya respectively.
During the late {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|12=draft}}, when Western nations visited Japan, the court held 15 days of prayers for the expulsion of foreigners at Kaze-no-Miya and Kazahi-no-Miya in {{ill|1863|ja|1863rd year|12=draft}} (Bunkyū 3).
In {{ill|1876|ja|1876th year|12=draft}} (Meiji 9), during the {{ill|Ise Riot|ja|伊勢暴動|12=draft}}, the sacred object from {{ill|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|ja|月夜見宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|12=draft}} was temporarily moved to Kaze-no-Miya to protect it from a nearby fire.<ref>Kaneko (1983): p.214</ref>
== Festivals == The {{ill|Kazahi-no-Matsuri|ja|風日祈祭|12=draft}}, praying for favorable weather and abundant harvests, is held annually on May 14 and August 4.
== Shrine Building == The shrine building of Kaze-no-Miya faces south and is constructed in the {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|ja|神明造|12=draft}} style with a thatched roof, featuring {{ill|Chigi and Katsuogi|ja|千木・鰹木|lt=chigi and katsuogi|12=draft}} with five odd-numbered katsuogi, following the Gekū’s design. The old shrine site for reconstruction (shikinen sengū) is adjacent to the east and west.
== Access == * Nearest Station: About a 12-minute walk from {{ill|Ise-shi Station|ja|伊勢市駅|12=draft}} via {{ill|JR Sangu Line|ja|参宮線|12=draft}} and {{ill|Kintetsu|ja|近畿日本鉄道|12=draft}}. * Nearest Bus Stop: About a 5-minute walk from {{ill|Mie Kotsu|ja|三重交通|12=draft}} Gekū-mae Bus Stop. * Nearest Interchange: About 2 km from {{ill|Ise Nishi Interchange|ja|伊勢西インターチェンジ|12=draft}} on the {{ill|Ise Expressway|ja|伊勢自動車道|12=draft}}. * Parking: Free parking available.
== References == * Kaneko Nobuo. *Tamaki Town History, Volume II – History and Traditions of South Ise.* Mie Prefecture Local History Series No. 91, Mie Prefecture Local History Publication Association, December 10, 1983. * {{ill|Katsunoshin Sakurai|ja|桜井勝之進|12=draft}}. *Ise Grand Shrine.* Gakuseisha, May 20, 1969. * Katsunoshin Sakurai. *The Shrine of the Great deity of Ise.* Hori Shoten, March 25, 1973.
== Related Pages == {{commonscat|Kaze-no-miya (Geku)}} * {{ill|Shinatsuhiko|ja|シナツヒコ|12=draft}} * {{ill|Kamikaze|ja|神風|12=draft}} * {{ill|Mongol invasions of Japan|ja|元寇|12=draft}} * {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|12=draft}} * {{ill|List of Shrines and Facilities of Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮を構成する神社と施設|1=List of 125 Shrines of Ise|12=draft}}
== External Links == * [http://www.isejingu.or.jp/gegu/gegu5.htm Ise Jingu Official Site]

== References == * Kaneko Nobuo. *Tamaki Town History, Volume II – History and Traditions of South Ise.* Mie Prefecture Local History Series No. 91, Mie Prefecture Local History Publication Association, December 10, 1983. * {{ill|Katsunoshin Sakurai|ja|桜井勝之進|12=draft}}. *Ise Grand Shrine.* Gakuseisha, May 20, 1969. * Katsunoshin Sakurai. *The Shrine of the Great deity of Ise.* Hori Shoten, March 25, 1973.
== Related Pages == {{commonscat|Kaze-no-miya (Geku)}} * {{ill|Shinatsuhiko|ja|シナツヒコ|12=draft}} * {{ill|Kamikaze|ja|神風|12=draft}} * {{ill|Mongol invasions of Japan|ja|元寇|12=draft}} * {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|12=draft}} * {{ill|List of Shrines and Facilities of Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮を構成する神社と施設|1=List of 125 Shrines of Ise|12=draft}}

{{draft categories| [[Category:Shrines in Ise]] [[Category:125 Shrines of Ise]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:伊勢市の神社]] [[Category:Kaze-no-Miya]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:category refresh]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]] }} [[sn:Kaze-no-Miya]] [[ja:風宮]] [[en:Kaze-no-Miya]]

revision.comment: Removing from [[Category:Refreshing imported pages]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
{{Infobox shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[image:Ise-Shrine Geku-Kazenomiya.jpg|256px|Kazenomiya]] | Name = Kazenomiya | location = Toyokawa-cho, [[Ise]], [[Mie Prefecture]], Japan | deity = [[Shinatsuhiko]] and [[Shinatobe]] | type = {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|12=draft}} auxiliary shrine (*betsugū*) | Established = Before 997 CE | Main Hall = [[Shinmei-zukuri]] | coordinates = {{coord|34|29|9.1|N|136|42|14.2|E|region:JP|display=inline,title}} | festival = Kazahi-no-Matsuri (*Wind Day Prayer Festival*) }}
'''Kaze-no-Miya''' (風宮) is an auxiliary shrine (*betsugū*) located within the precincts of {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|12=draft}} of the [[Ise Grand Shrine]] in [[Toyokawa-cho]], [[Ise]], [[Mie Prefecture]], Japan. == Overview== [[File:Kame-ish 01.JPG|left|thumb|180px|Kame-ishi (Turtle Stone)]] '''Kazenomiya''' is a {{ill|Betsugū (detached shrine)|ja|別宮|12=draft}} of the {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|12=draft}} (Gekū), located at the foot of **Hinokioyama** (檜尾山), south of the main shrine. The enshrined deities are {{ill|Shinatsuhiko|ja|級長津彦命|12=draft}} and {{ill|Shinatobe|ja|級長戸辺命|12=draft}}, the same as those in the {{ill|Kazahinomi-no-Miya|ja|風日祈宮|12=draft}}, a detached shrine of the {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|12=draft}} (Naikū).
'''Kazenomiya''' is situated to the left after crossing the **Kame-ishi** (亀石), a stone along the approach to {{ill|Taga-no-Miya|ja|多賀宮|12=draft}}, next to the pond in front of the main shrine. It is said that Kame-ishi was transported from the entrance of the **Ama-no-Iwato** (天岩戸) on {{ill|Mount Takakura (Ise)|ja|高倉山 (伊勢市)|12=draft}}.
The term *Betsugū* (別宮) means "separate shrine," considered second in reverence only to the main shrine. Apart from '''Kazenomiya''', the Gekū has other Betsugū: {{ill|Taka-no-Miya|ja|多賀宮|12=draft}} and {{ill|Tsuchi-no-Miya|ja|土宮|12=draft}} within the precincts, and {{ill|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|ja|月夜見宮|8=ja|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|12=draft}} outside the precincts. Kazamiya was the last to be designated as a Betsugū in {{ill|1293|ja|1293rd year|12=draft}} (Shōō 6) and is ranked fourth among the four Betsugū.
Originally, it was known as **Kazeya-shiro** (風社) or **Kazejinja** (風神社), a shrine of lesser rank. However, following the {{ill|Mongol invasions of Japan|ja|元寇|12=draft}} in {{ill|1281|ja|1281st year|12=draft}} (Kōan 4), when divine winds (*kamikaze*) protected Japan, it was elevated to the status of Betsugū.
== Enshrined Deities == The enshrined deities of {{ill|Kaze-no-Miya|ja|風宮|12=draft}} are {{ill|Shinatsuhiko|ja|シナツヒコ|12=draft}} and {{ill|Shinatobe|ja|シナトベ|12=draft}}, gods who govern wind and rain. Originally worshipped as deities bringing favorable weather for agriculture, after the Mongol invasions, they became objects of prayers for the salvation of Japan in times of national crisis.
== History == The origins of Kaze-no-Miya are unclear, but the earliest reference appears in the "Chōtoku Kenroku" from {{ill|997|ja|997th year|12=draft}} (Chōtoku 3), citing it as "Kaze-sha," ranked 4th among 32 rice field shrines under the jurisdiction of the Gekū, as noted in {{ill|Watarai Ieyuki|ja|度会家行|12=draft}}'s "Ruiju Jingi Hongen." "Rice field shrines" correspond to the current末社 (massha), considered lower than sessha (auxiliary shrines).
Although initially ranked as a末社, due to its deities’ importance in agriculture, the {{ill|Kazahi-no-Matsuri|ja|風日祈祭|12=draft}} was held, and offerings of "kakechikara" (bundled rice stalks) were made during the {{ill|Kannamesai|ja|神嘗祭|12=draft}}.
During the {{ill|Mongol invasions of Japan|ja|元寇|12=draft}} in {{ill|1281|ja|1281st year|12=draft}} (Kōan 4), the court dispatched the Dainagon Tameuji to perform prayers at both the Naikū's Kaze-sha and this shrine. After the retreat of the Mongol forces, the victory was attributed to the "Divine Wind" ({{ill|Kamikaze|ja|神風|12=draft}}), and in {{ill|1293|ja|1293rd year|12=draft}} (Shōō 6), the shrine was elevated to a betsu-gū (detached shrine), named {{ill|Kazahi-no-Miya|ja|風日祈宮|12=draft}} and Kaze-no-Miya respectively.
During the late {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|12=draft}}, when Western nations visited Japan, the court held 15 days of prayers for the expulsion of foreigners at Kaze-no-Miya and Kazahi-no-Miya in {{ill|1863|ja|1863rd year|12=draft}} (Bunkyū 3).
In {{ill|1876|ja|1876th year|12=draft}} (Meiji 9), during the {{ill|Ise Riot|ja|伊勢暴動|12=draft}}, the sacred object from {{ill|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|ja|月夜見宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|12=draft}} was temporarily moved to Kaze-no-Miya to protect it from a nearby fire.<ref>Kaneko (1983): p.214</ref>
== Festivals == The {{ill|Kazahi-no-Matsuri|ja|風日祈祭|12=draft}}, praying for favorable weather and abundant harvests, is held annually on May 14 and August 4.
== Shrine Building == The shrine building of Kaze-no-Miya faces south and is constructed in the {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|ja|神明造|12=draft}} style with a thatched roof, featuring {{ill|Chigi and Katsuogi|ja|千木・鰹木|lt=chigi and katsuogi|12=draft}} with five odd-numbered katsuogi, following the Gekū’s design. The old shrine site for reconstruction (shikinen sengū) is adjacent to the east and west.
== Access == * Nearest Station: About a 12-minute walk from {{ill|Ise-shi Station|ja|伊勢市駅|12=draft}} via {{ill|JR Sangu Line|ja|参宮線|12=draft}} and {{ill|Kintetsu|ja|近畿日本鉄道|12=draft}}. * Nearest Bus Stop: About a 5-minute walk from {{ill|Mie Kotsu|ja|三重交通|12=draft}} Gekū-mae Bus Stop. * Nearest Interchange: About 2 km from {{ill|Ise Nishi Interchange|ja|伊勢西インターチェンジ|12=draft}} on the {{ill|Ise Expressway|ja|伊勢自動車道|12=draft}}. * Parking: Free parking available.
== References == * Kaneko Nobuo. *Tamaki Town History, Volume II – History and Traditions of South Ise.* Mie Prefecture Local History Series No. 91, Mie Prefecture Local History Publication Association, December 10, 1983. * {{ill|Katsunoshin Sakurai|ja|桜井勝之進|12=draft}}. *Ise Grand Shrine.* Gakuseisha, May 20, 1969. * Katsunoshin Sakurai. *The Shrine of the Great deity of Ise.* Hori Shoten, March 25, 1973.
== Related Pages == {{commonscat|Kaze-no-miya (Geku)}} * {{ill|Shinatsuhiko|ja|シナツヒコ|12=draft}} * {{ill|Kamikaze|ja|神風|12=draft}} * {{ill|Mongol invasions of Japan|ja|元寇|12=draft}} * {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|12=draft}} * {{ill|List of Shrines and Facilities of Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮を構成する神社と施設|1=List of 125 Shrines of Ise|12=draft}}
== External Links == * [http://www.isejingu.or.jp/gegu/gegu5.htm Ise Jingu Official Site]

During the {{ill|Mongol invasions of Japan|ja|元寇|12=draft}} in {{ill|1281|ja|1281st year|12=draft}} (Kōan 4), the court dispatched the Dainagon Tameuji to perform prayers at both the Naikū's Kaze-sha and this shrine. After the retreat of the Mongol forces, the victory was attributed to the "Divine Wind" ({{ill|Kamikaze|ja|神風|12=draft}}), and in {{ill|1293|ja|1293rd year|12=draft}} (Shōō 6), the shrine was elevated to a betsu-gū (detached shrine), named {{ill|Kazahi-no-Miya|ja|風日祈宮|12=draft}} and Kaze-no-Miya respectively.
During the late {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|12=draft}}, when Western nations visited Japan, the court held 15 days of prayers for the expulsion of foreigners at Kaze-no-Miya and Kazahi-no-Miya in {{ill|1863|ja|1863rd year|12=draft}} (Bunkyū 3).
In {{ill|1876|ja|1876th year|12=draft}} (Meiji 9), during the {{ill|Ise Riot|ja|伊勢暴動|12=draft}}, the sacred object from {{ill|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|ja|月夜見宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|12=draft}} was temporarily moved to Kaze-no-Miya to protect it from a nearby fire.<ref>Kaneko (1983): p.214</ref>
== Festivals == The {{ill|Kazahi-no-Matsuri|ja|風日祈祭|12=draft}}, praying for favorable weather and abundant harvests, is held annually on May 14 and August 4.
== Shrine Building == The shrine building of Kaze-no-Miya faces south and is constructed in the {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|ja|神明造|12=draft}} style with a thatched roof, featuring {{ill|Chigi and Katsuogi|ja|千木・鰹木|lt=chigi and katsuogi|12=draft}} with five odd-numbered katsuogi, following the Gekū’s design. The old shrine site for reconstruction (shikinen sengū) is adjacent to the east and west.
== Access == * Nearest Station: About a 12-minute walk from {{ill|Ise-shi Station|ja|伊勢市駅|12=draft}} via {{ill|JR Sangu Line|ja|参宮線|12=draft}} and {{ill|Kintetsu|ja|近畿日本鉄道|12=draft}}. * Nearest Bus Stop: About a 5-minute walk from {{ill|Mie Kotsu|ja|三重交通|12=draft}} Gekū-mae Bus Stop. * Nearest Interchange: About 2 km from {{ill|Ise Nishi Interchange|ja|伊勢西インターチェンジ|12=draft}} on the {{ill|Ise Expressway|ja|伊勢自動車道|12=draft}}. * Parking: Free parking available.
== References == * Kaneko Nobuo. *Tamaki Town History, Volume II – History and Traditions of South Ise.* Mie Prefecture Local History Series No. 91, Mie Prefecture Local History Publication Association, December 10, 1983. * {{ill|Katsunoshin Sakurai|ja|桜井勝之進|12=draft}}. *Ise Grand Shrine.* Gakuseisha, May 20, 1969. * Katsunoshin Sakurai. *The Shrine of the Great deity of Ise.* Hori Shoten, March 25, 1973.
== Related Pages == {{commonscat|Kaze-no-miya (Geku)}} * {{ill|Shinatsuhiko|ja|シナツヒコ|12=draft}} * {{ill|Kamikaze|ja|神風|12=draft}} * {{ill|Mongol invasions of Japan|ja|元寇|12=draft}} * {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|12=draft}} * {{ill|List of Shrines and Facilities of Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮を構成する神社と施設|1=List of 125 Shrines of Ise|12=draft}}
== External Links == * [http://www.isejingu.or.jp/gegu/gegu5.htm Ise Jingu Official Site]

{{Infobox shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[image:Ise-Shrine Geku-Kazenomiya.jpg|256px|Kazenomiya]] | Name = Kazenomiya | location = Toyokawa-cho, [[Ise]], [[Mie Prefecture]], Japan | deity = [[Shinatsuhiko]] and [[Shinatobe]] | type = {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|12=draft}} auxiliary shrine (*betsugū*) | Established = Before 997 CE | Main Hall = [[Shinmei-zukuri]] | coordinates = {{coord|34|29|9.1|N|136|42|14.2|E|region:JP|display=inline,title}} | festival = Kazahi-no-Matsuri (*Wind Day Prayer Festival*) }}
'''Kaze-no-Miya''' (風宮) is an auxiliary shrine (*betsugū*) located within the precincts of {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|12=draft}} of the [[Ise Grand Shrine]] in [[Toyokawa-cho]], [[Ise]], [[Mie Prefecture]], Japan. == Overview== [[File:Kame-ish 01.JPG|left|thumb|180px|Kame-ishi (Turtle Stone)]] '''Kazenomiya''' is a {{ill|Betsugū (detached shrine)|ja|別宮|12=draft}} of the {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|12=draft}} (Gekū), located at the foot of **Hinokioyama** (檜尾山), south of the main shrine. The enshrined deities are {{ill|Shinatsuhiko|ja|級長津彦命|12=draft}} and {{ill|Shinatobe|ja|級長戸辺命|12=draft}}, the same as those in the {{ill|Kazahinomi-no-Miya|ja|風日祈宮|12=draft}}, a detached shrine of the {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|12=draft}} (Naikū).
'''Kazenomiya''' is situated to the left after crossing the **Kame-ishi** (亀石), a stone along the approach to {{ill|Taga-no-Miya|ja|多賀宮|12=draft}}, next to the pond in front of the main shrine. It is said that Kame-ishi was transported from the entrance of the **Ama-no-Iwato** (天岩戸) on {{ill|Mount Takakura (Ise)|ja|高倉山 (伊勢市)|12=draft}}.
The term *Betsugū* (別宮) means "separate shrine," considered second in reverence only to the main shrine. Apart from '''Kazenomiya''', the Gekū has other Betsugū: {{ill|Taka-no-Miya|ja|多賀宮|12=draft}} and {{ill|Tsuchi-no-Miya|ja|土宮|12=draft}} within the precincts, and {{ill|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|ja|月夜見宮|8=ja|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|12=draft}} outside the precincts. Kazamiya was the last to be designated as a Betsugū in {{ill|1293|ja|1293rd year|12=draft}} (Shōō 6) and is ranked fourth among the four Betsugū.
Originally, it was known as **Kazeya-shiro** (風社) or **Kazejinja** (風神社), a shrine of lesser rank. However, following the {{ill|Mongol invasions of Japan|ja|元寇|12=draft}} in {{ill|1281|ja|1281st year|12=draft}} (Kōan 4), when divine winds (*kamikaze*) protected Japan, it was elevated to the status of Betsugū.
== Enshrined Deities == The enshrined deities of {{ill|Kaze-no-Miya|ja|風宮|12=draft}} are {{ill|Shinatsuhiko|ja|シナツヒコ|12=draft}} and {{ill|Shinatobe|ja|シナトベ|12=draft}}, gods who govern wind and rain. Originally worshipped as deities bringing favorable weather for agriculture, after the Mongol invasions, they became objects of prayers for the salvation of Japan in times of national crisis.
== History == The origins of Kaze-no-Miya are unclear, but the earliest reference appears in the "Chōtoku Kenroku" from {{ill|997|ja|997th year|12=draft}} (Chōtoku 3), citing it as "Kaze-sha," ranked 4th among 32 rice field shrines under the jurisdiction of the Gekū, as noted in {{ill|Watarai Ieyuki|ja|度会家行|12=draft}}'s "Ruiju Jingi Hongen." "Rice field shrines" correspond to the current末社 (massha), considered lower than sessha (auxiliary shrines).
Although initially ranked as a末社, due to its deities’ importance in agriculture, the {{ill|Kazahi-no-Matsuri|ja|風日祈祭|12=draft}} was held, and offerings of "kakechikara" (bundled rice stalks) were made during the {{ill|Kannamesai|ja|神嘗祭|12=draft}}.
During the {{ill|Mongol invasions of Japan|ja|元寇|12=draft}} in {{ill|1281|ja|1281st year|12=draft}} (Kōan 4), the court dispatched the Dainagon Tameuji to perform prayers at both the Naikū's Kaze-sha and this shrine. After the retreat of the Mongol forces, the victory was attributed to the "Divine Wind" ({{ill|Kamikaze|ja|神風|12=draft}}), and in {{ill|1293|ja|1293rd year|12=draft}} (Shōō 6), the shrine was elevated to a betsu-gū (detached shrine), named {{ill|Kazahi-no-Miya|ja|風日祈宮|12=draft}} and Kaze-no-Miya respectively.
During the late {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|12=draft}}, when Western nations visited Japan, the court held 15 days of prayers for the expulsion of foreigners at Kaze-no-Miya and Kazahi-no-Miya in {{ill|1863|ja|1863rd year|12=draft}} (Bunkyū 3).
In {{ill|1876|ja|1876th year|12=draft}} (Meiji 9), during the {{ill|Ise Riot|ja|伊勢暴動|12=draft}}, the sacred object from {{ill|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|ja|月夜見宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|12=draft}} was temporarily moved to Kaze-no-Miya to protect it from a nearby fire.<ref>Kaneko (1983): p.214</ref>
== Festivals == The {{ill|Kazahi-no-Matsuri|ja|風日祈祭|12=draft}}, praying for favorable weather and abundant harvests, is held annually on May 14 and August 4.
== Shrine Building == The shrine building of Kaze-no-Miya faces south and is constructed in the {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|ja|神明造|12=draft}} style with a thatched roof, featuring {{ill|Chigi and Katsuogi|ja|千木・鰹木|lt=chigi and katsuogi|12=draft}} with five odd-numbered katsuogi, following the Gekū’s design. The old shrine site for reconstruction (shikinen sengū) is adjacent to the east and west.
== Access == * Nearest Station: About a 12-minute walk from {{ill|Ise-shi Station|ja|伊勢市駅|12=draft}} via {{ill|JR Sangu Line|ja|参宮線|12=draft}} and {{ill|Kintetsu|ja|近畿日本鉄道|12=draft}}. * Nearest Bus Stop: About a 5-minute walk from {{ill|Mie Kotsu|ja|三重交通|12=draft}} Gekū-mae Bus Stop. * Nearest Interchange: About 2 km from {{ill|Ise Nishi Interchange|ja|伊勢西インターチェンジ|12=draft}} on the {{ill|Ise Expressway|ja|伊勢自動車道|12=draft}}. * Parking: Free parking available.
== References == * Kaneko Nobuo. *Tamaki Town History, Volume II – History and Traditions of South Ise.* Mie Prefecture Local History Series No. 91, Mie Prefecture Local History Publication Association, December 10, 1983. * {{ill|Katsunoshin Sakurai|ja|桜井勝之進|12=draft}}. *Ise Grand Shrine.* Gakuseisha, May 20, 1969. * Katsunoshin Sakurai. *The Shrine of the Great deity of Ise.* Hori Shoten, March 25, 1973.
== Related Pages == {{commonscat|Kaze-no-miya (Geku)}} * {{ill|Shinatsuhiko|ja|シナツヒコ|12=draft}} * {{ill|Kamikaze|ja|神風|12=draft}} * {{ill|Mongol invasions of Japan|ja|元寇|12=draft}} * {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|12=draft}} * {{ill|List of Shrines and Facilities of Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮を構成する神社と施設|1=List of 125 Shrines of Ise|12=draft}}
== External Links == * [http://www.isejingu.or.jp/gegu/gegu5.htm Ise Jingu Official Site]

{{Infobox shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[image:Ise-Shrine Geku-Kazenomiya.jpg|256px|Kazenomiya]] | Name = Kazenomiya | location = Toyokawa-cho, [[Ise]], [[Mie Prefecture]], Japan | deity = [[Shinatsuhiko]] and [[Shinatobe]] | type = {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|12=draft}} auxiliary shrine (*betsugū*) | Established = Before 997 CE | Main Hall = [[Shinmei-zukuri]] | coordinates = {{coord|34|29|9.1|N|136|42|14.2|E|region:JP|display=inline,title}} | festival = Kazahi-no-Matsuri (*Wind Day Prayer Festival*) }}
