It is said that Kame-ishi was transported from the entrance of the **Ama-no-Iwato** (天岩戸) on {{ill|Mount Takakura (Ise)|ja|高倉山 (伊勢市)|12=draft}}.
The term *Betsugū* (別宮) means "separate shrine," considered second in reverence only to the main shrine. Apart from '''Kazenomiya''', the Gekū has other Betsugū: {{ill|Taka-no-Miya|ja|多賀宮|12=draft}} and {{ill|Tsuchi-no-Miya|ja|土宮|12=draft}} within the precincts, and {{ill|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|ja|月夜見宮|8=ja|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|12=draft}} outside the precincts. Kazamiya was the last to be designated as a Betsugū in {{ill|1293|ja|1293rd year|12=draft}} (Shōō 6) and is Ranked fourth among the four Betsugū.
Originally, it was known as **Kazeya-shiro** (風社) or **Kazejinja** (風神社), a shrine of lesser Rank. However, following the {{ill|Mongol invasions of Japan|ja|元寇|12=draft}} in {{ill|1281|ja|1281st year|12=draft}} (Kōan 4), when divine winds (*kamikaze*) protected Japan, it was elevated to the status of Betsugū.
== Enshrined Deities == The enshrined deities of {{ill|Kaze-no-Miya|ja|風宮|12=draft}} are {{ill|Shinatsuhiko|ja|シナツヒコ|12=draft}} and {{ill|Shinatobe|ja|シナトベ|12=draft}}, gods who govern wind and rain. Originally worshipped as deities bringing favorable weather for agriculture, after the Mongol invasions, they became objects of prayers for the salvation of Japan in times of national crisis.
== History == The origins of Kaze-no-Miya are unclear, but the earliest reference appears in the "Chōtoku Kenroku" from {{ill|997|ja|997th year|12=draft}} (Chōtoku 3), citing it as "Kaze-sha," Ranked 4th among 32 rice field shrines under the jurisdiction of the Gekū, as noted in {{ill|Watarai Ieyuki|ja|度会家行|12=draft}}'s "Ruiju Jingi Hongen." "Rice field shrines" correspond to the current末社 (massha), considered lower than sessha (auxiliary shrines).
Although initially Ranked as a末社, due to its deities’ importance in agriculture, the {{ill|Kazahi-no-Matsuri|ja|風日祈祭|12=draft}} was held, and offerings of "kakechikara" (bundled rice stalks) were made during the {{ill|Kannamesai|ja|神嘗祭|12=draft}}.
During the {{ill|Mongol invasions of Japan|ja|元寇|12=draft}} in {{ill|1281|ja|1281st year|12=draft}} (Kōan 4), the court dispatched the Dainagon Tameuji to perform prayers at both the Naikū's Kaze-sha and this shrine. After the retreat of the Mongol forces, the victory was attributed to the "Divine Wind" ({{ill|Kamikaze|ja|神風|12=draft}}), and in {{ill|1293|ja|1293rd year|12=draft}} (Shōō 6), the shrine was elevated to a betsu-gū (detached shrine), named {{ill|Kazahi-no-Miya|ja|風日祈宮|12=draft}} and Kaze-no-Miya respectively.
During the late {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|12=draft}}, when Western nations visited Japan, the court held 15 days of prayers for the expulsion of foreigners at Kaze-no-Miya and Kazahi-no-Miya in {{ill|1863|ja|1863rd year|12=draft}} (Bunkyū 3).
In {{ill|1876|ja|1876th year|12=draft}} (Meiji 9), during the {{ill|Ise Riot|ja|伊勢暴動|12=draft}}, the sacred object from {{ill|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|ja|月夜見宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|12=draft}} was temporarily moved to Kaze-no-Miya to protect it from a nearby fire.<ref>Kaneko (1983): p.214</ref>
== Festivals == The {{ill|Kazahi-no-Matsuri|ja|風日祈祭|12=draft}}, praying for favorable weather and abundant harvests, is held annually on May 14 and August 4.
== Shrine Building == The shrine building of Kaze-no-Miya faces south and is constructed in the {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|ja|神明造|12=draft}} style with a thatched roof, featuring {{ill|Chigi and Katsuogi|ja|千木・鰹木|lt=chigi and katsuogi|12=draft}} with five odd-numbered katsuogi, following the Gekū’s design. The old shrine site for reconstruction (shikinen sengū) is adjacent to the east and west.
== Access == * Nearest Station: About a 12-minute walk from {{ill|Ise-shi Station|ja|伊勢市駅|12=draft}} via {{ill|JR Sangu Line|ja|参宮線|12=draft}} and {{ill|Kintetsu|ja|近畿日本鉄道|12=draft}}. * Nearest Bus Stop: About a 5-minute walk from {{ill|Mie Kotsu|ja|三重交通|12=draft}} Gekū-mae Bus Stop. * Nearest Interchange: About 2 km from {{ill|Ise Nishi Interchange|ja|伊勢西インターチェンジ|12=draft}} on the {{ill|Ise Expressway|ja|伊勢自動車道|12=draft}}. * Parking: Free parking available.
== References == * Kaneko Nobuo. *Tamaki Town History, Volume II – History and Traditions of South Ise.* Mie Prefecture Local History Series No. 91, Mie Prefecture Local History Publication Association, December 10, 1983. * {{ill|Katsunoshin Sakurai|ja|桜井勝之進|12=draft}}. *Ise Grand Shrine.* Gakuseisha, May 20, 1969. * Katsunoshin Sakurai. *The Shrine of the Great deity of Ise.* Hori Shoten, March 25, 1973.
== Related Pages == {{commonscat|Kaze-no-miya (Geku)}} * {{ill|Shinatsuhiko|ja|シナツヒコ|12=draft}} * {{ill|Kamikaze|ja|神風|12=draft}} * {{ill|Mongol invasions of Japan|ja|元寇|12=draft}} * {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|12=draft}} * {{ill|List of Shrines and Facilities of Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮を構成する神社と施設|1=List of 125 Shrines of Ise|12=draft}}
== External Links == * [http://www.isejingu.or.jp/gegu/gegu5.htm Ise Jingu Official Site]

{{Infobox shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[image:Ise-Shrine Geku-Kazenomiya.jpg|256px|Kazenomiya]] | Name = Kazenomiya | location = Toyokawa-cho, [[Ise]], [[Mie Prefecture]], Japan | deity = [[Shinatsuhiko]] and [[Shinatobe]] | type = {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮}} auxiliary shrine (*betsugū*) | Established = Before 997 CE | Main Hall = [[Shinmei-zukuri]] | coordinates = {{coord|34|29|9.1|N|136|42|14.2|E|region:JP|display=inline,title}} | festival = Kazahi-no-Matsuri (*Wind Day Prayer Festival*) }}
'''Kaze-no-Miya''' (風宮) is an auxiliary shrine (*betsugū*) located within the precincts of {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮}} of the [[Ise Grand Shrine]] in [[Toyokawa-cho]], [[Ise]], [[Mie Prefecture]], Japan. == Overview== [[File:Kame-ish 01.JPG|left|thumb|180px|Kame-ishi (Turtle Stone)]] '''Kazenomiya''' is a {{ill|Betsugū (detached shrine)|ja|別宮}} of the {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮}} (Gekū), located at the foot of **Hinokioyama** (檜尾山), south of the main shrine. The enshrined deities are {{ill|Shinatsuhiko|ja|級長津彦命}} and {{ill|Shinatobe|ja|級長戸辺命}}, the same as those in the {{ill|Kazahinomi-no-Miya|ja|風日祈宮}}, a detached shrine of the {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮}} (Naikū).
'''Kazenomiya''' is situated to the left after crossing the **Kame-ishi** (亀石), a stone along the approach to {{ill|Taga-no-Miya|ja|多賀宮}}, next to the pond in front of the main shrine. It is said that Kame-ishi was transported from the entrance of the **Ama-no-Iwato** (天岩戸) on {{ill|Mount Takakura (Ise)|ja|高倉山 (伊勢市)}}.

revision.comment: 16 revisions imported: Bot: import full history from ja:風宮 up to rev 98026745
revision.text:
{{脚注の不足|date=2020-07-19}} {{神社 | 画像=[[Image:Ise-Shrine Geku-Kazenomiya.jpg|256px|風宮]] | 名称=風宮 | 所在地=[[三重県]][[伊勢市]][[豊川町 (伊勢市)|豊川町]] | 祭神=[[シナツヒコ|級長津彦命・級長戸辺命]] | 社格=[[豊受大神宮]]別宮 | 創建=[[997年]]以前 | 本殿=[[神明造]] | 位置={{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|34|29|9.1|N|136|42|14.2|E|region:JP|display=inline,title}} | 例祭=風日祈祭 }} '''風宮'''（かぜのみや）は、[[三重県]][[伊勢市]][[豊川町 (伊勢市)|豊川町]]に存在する[[豊受大神宮|外宮（豊受大神宮）]]の境内別宮である。
== 概要 == [[Image:Kame-ish 01.JPG|left|thumb|180px|亀石]] 風宮は外宮正宮南方の'''檜尾山'''（ひのきおやま）の麓に位置する外宮の別宮である。祭神は[[皇大神宮|内宮（皇大神宮）]]別宮の[[風日祈宮]]と同じ[[シナツヒコ|級長津彦命・級長戸辺命]]で、外宮正宮前の池の横の多賀宮への参道にある'''亀石'''を渡った左側に風宮がある。亀石は[[高倉山 (伊勢市)|高倉山の天岩戸]]の入り口の岩を運んだと伝えられている。
別宮とは「'''わけみや'''」の意味で、正宮に次ぎ尊いとされる。外宮の別宮は風宮のほか境内に[[多賀宮]]（たかのみや）と[[土宮]]（つちのみや）、境外に[[月夜見宮]]（つきよみのみや）があるが、風宮が別宮となったのは[[1293年]]（[[正応]]6年）と最後であり、4別宮の中で第4位とされる。
古くは現在の末社格の'''風社'''（かぜのやしろ、風神社とも）であったが、[[1281年]]（[[弘安]]4年）の[[元寇]]の時に[[神風]]を起こし日本を守ったとして別宮に昇格した。
== 祭神 == 風宮の祭神は、風雨を司る神とされる[[シナツヒコ|級長津彦命と級長戸辺命]]（しなつひこのみこと、しなとべのみこと）である。本来は農耕に適した風雨をもたらす神であったが、元寇以降は日本の国難に際して日本を救う祈願の対象となった。
== 歴史 == 風宮の由緒は定かではないが、[[997年]]（[[長徳]]3年）の『長徳検録』に外宮所管の田社32前の4位の'''風社'''と記されたとの、[[度会家行]]による『類聚神祇本源』での引用が最古である。「田社」は現在の末社に相当し、現在の摂社以下の扱いであった。
風神社は末社相当であったが、祭神が農耕に都合のよい風雨をもたらす神であることから'''風日祈祭'''が行なわれ、[[神嘗祭]]では'''懸税'''（かけちから、稲穂）が供えられるなど重視された。
[[1281年]]（[[弘安]]4年）の[[元寇]]では[[朝廷 (日本)|朝廷]]より為氏大納言が勅使として神宮に派遣され、内宮の'''風神社'''と風社で祈祷を行なった。日本に押し寄せた元軍は退却し日本にとっての国難は去り、これを'''神風'''による勝利として[[1293年]]（[[正応]]6年）に風神社と風社は別宮に昇格され、'''風日祈宮'''と'''風宮'''となった。
[[江戸時代]]末期に欧米諸国が日本を訪れるようになり、[[1863年]]（[[文久]]3年）5月に朝廷は[[風日祈宮]]と風宮で[[攘夷]]の祈願を15日間行なった。
[[1876年]]（明治9年）の[[伊勢暴動]]の際には、[[月夜見宮]]近隣の町が炎上し、宮への類焼が懸念されたことから、月夜見宮の[[神体]]が一時的に風宮へ移された<ref>金子（1983）：214ページ</ref>。
== 祭事 == 毎年5月14日と8月4日に風雨の順調と五穀の豊穣を祈る'''風日祈祭'''が行なわれる。
== 社殿 == 風宮の社殿は外宮に準じ[[千木・鰹木|外削ぎの千木と、5本で奇数の鰹木]]を持つ萱葺の[[神明造]]で南面している。遷宮のための古殿地は東西に隣接している。
== 交通 == * 最寄駅：[[東海旅客鉄道|JR]][[参宮線]]・[[近畿日本鉄道|近鉄]][[伊勢市駅]]から徒歩約12分。 * 最寄バス停：[[三重交通]]外宮前バス停下車徒歩約5分。 * 最寄インターチェンジ：[[伊勢自動車道]][[伊勢西インターチェンジ]]から約2km。 * 駐車場：無料駐車場がある。
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
== 参考資料 == * 金子延夫『玉城町史 二巻 －南伊勢の歴史と伝承－』三重県郷土資料叢書第91集、三重県郷土資料刊行会、昭和58年12月10日 * [[桜井勝之進]]『伊勢神宮』学生社、昭和44年5月20日発行 * 桜井勝之進『伊勢の大神の宮』堀書店、昭和48年3月25日発行
== 関連項目 == {{commonscat|Kaze-no-miya (Geku)}} * [[シナツヒコ]] * [[神風]] * [[元寇]] * [[豊受大神宮]] * [[伊勢神宮を構成する神社と施設]]
== 外部リンク == * [http://www.isejingu.or.jp/gegu/gegu5.htm 伊勢の神宮]
{{神宮125社}} {{神道 横}}
{{デフォルトソート:かせのみや}} [[Category:伊勢市の神社]] [[Category:神宮125社]]

{{デフォルトソート:かせのみや}} [[Category:伊勢市の神社]] [[Category:神宮125社]]

{{Infobox shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = | Name = Kazenomiya | location = Toyokawa-cho, [[Ise]], [[Mie Prefecture]], Japan | deity = [[Shinatsuhiko]] and [[Shinatobe]] | type = {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} auxiliary shrine (*betsugū*) | Established = Before 997 CE | Main Hall = [[Shinmei-zukuri]] | coordinates = {{coord|34|29|9.1|N|136|42|14.2|E|region:JP|display=inline,title}} | festival = Kazahi-no-Matsuri (*Wind Day Prayer Festival*) }}
'''Kaze-no-Miya''' (風宮) is an auxiliary shrine (*betsugū*) located within the precincts of {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} of the [[Ise Grand Shrine]] in [[Toyokawa-cho]], [[Ise]], [[Mie Prefecture]], Japan. == Overview== [[File:Kame-ish 01.JPG|left|thumb|180px|Kame-ishi (Turtle Stone)]] '''Kazenomiya''' is a {{ill|Betsugū (detached shrine)|ja|別宮|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} of the {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} (Gekū), located at the foot of **Hinokioyama** (檜尾山), south of the main shrine. The enshrined deities are {{ill|Shinatsuhiko|ja|級長津彦命|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Shinatobe|ja|級長戸辺命|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, the same as those in the {{ill|Kazahinomi-no-Miya|ja|風日祈宮|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, a detached shrine of the {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} (Naikū).
'''Kazenomiya''' is situated to the left after crossing the **Kame-ishi** (亀石), a stone along the approach to {{ill|Taga-no-Miya|ja|多賀宮|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, next to the pond in front of the main shrine. It is said that Kame-ishi was transported from the entrance of the **Ama-no-Iwato** (天岩戸) on {{ill|Mount Takakura (Ise)|ja|高倉山 (伊勢市)|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}.
The term *Betsugū* (別宮) means "separate shrine," considered second in reverence only to the main shrine. Apart from '''Kazenomiya''', the Gekū has other Betsugū: {{ill|Taka-no-Miya|ja|多賀宮|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Tsuchi-no-Miya|ja|土宮|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} within the precincts, and {{ill|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|ja|月夜見宮|8=ja|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} outside the precincts. Kazamiya was the last to be designated as a Betsugū in {{ill|1293|ja|1293rd year|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} (Shōō 6) and is Ranked fourth among the four Betsugū.
Originally, it was known as **Kazeya-shiro** (風社) or **Kazejinja** (風神社), a shrine of lesser Rank. However, following the {{ill|Mongol invasions of Japan|ja|元寇|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} in {{ill|1281|ja|1281st year|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} (Kōan 4), when divine winds (*kamikaze*) protected Japan, it was elevated to the status of Betsugū.
== Enshrined Deities == The enshrined deities of {{ill|Kaze-no-Miya|ja|風宮|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} are {{ill|Shinatsuhiko|ja|シナツヒコ|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Shinatobe|ja|シナトベ|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, gods who govern wind and rain. Originally worshipped as deities bringing favorable weather for agriculture, after the Mongol invasions, they became objects of prayers for the salvation of Japan in times of national crisis.
== History == The origins of Kaze-no-Miya are unclear, but the earliest reference appears in the "Chōtoku Kenroku" from {{ill|997|ja|997th year|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} (Chōtoku 3), citing it as "Kaze-sha," Ranked 4th among 32 rice field shrines under the jurisdiction of the Gekū, as noted in {{ill|Watarai Ieyuki|ja|度会家行|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}'s "Ruiju Jingi Hongen." "Rice field shrines" correspond to the current末社 (massha), considered lower than sessha (auxiliary shrines).
Although initially Ranked as a末社, due to its deities’ importance in agriculture, the {{ill|Kazahi-no-Matsuri|ja|風日祈祭|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} was held, and offerings of "kakechikara" (bundled rice stalks) were made during the {{ill|Kannamesai|ja|神嘗祭|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}.
During the {{ill|Mongol invasions of Japan|ja|元寇|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} in {{ill|1281|ja|1281st year|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} (Kōan 4), the court dispatched the Dainagon Tameuji to perform prayers at both the Naikū's Kaze-sha and this shrine. After the retreat of the Mongol forces, the victory was attributed to the "Divine Wind" ({{ill|Kamikaze|ja|神風|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}), and in {{ill|1293|ja|1293rd year|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} (Shōō 6), the shrine was elevated to a betsu-gū (detached shrine), named {{ill|Kazahi-no-Miya|ja|風日祈宮|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} and Kaze-no-Miya respectively.
During the late {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, when Western nations visited Japan, the court held 15 days of prayers for the expulsion of foreigners at Kaze-no-Miya and Kazahi-no-Miya in {{ill|1863|ja|1863rd year|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} (Bunkyū 3).
In {{ill|1876|ja|1876th year|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} (Meiji 9), during the {{ill|Ise Riot|ja|伊勢暴動|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, the sacred object from {{ill|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|ja|月夜見宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} was temporarily moved to Kaze-no-Miya to protect it from a nearby fire.<ref>Kaneko (1983): p.214</ref>
== Festivals == The {{ill|Kazahi-no-Matsuri|ja|風日祈祭|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, praying for favorable weather and abundant harvests, is held annually on May 14 and August 4.
== Shrine Building == The shrine building of Kaze-no-Miya faces south and is constructed in the {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|ja|神明造|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} style with a thatched roof, featuring {{ill|Chigi and Katsuogi|ja|千木・鰹木|lt=chigi and katsuogi|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} with five odd-numbered katsuogi, following the Gekū’s design. The old shrine site for reconstruction (shikinen sengū) is adjacent to the east and west.
== Access == * Nearest Station: About a 12-minute walk from {{ill|Ise-shi Station|ja|伊勢市駅|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} via {{ill|JR Sangu Line|ja|参宮線|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Kintetsu|ja|近畿日本鉄道|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}. * Nearest Bus Stop: About a 5-minute walk from {{ill|Mie Kotsu|ja|三重交通|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} Gekū-mae Bus Stop. * Nearest Interchange: About 2 km from {{ill|Ise Nishi Interchange|ja|伊勢西インターチェンジ|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} on the {{ill|Ise Expressway|ja|伊勢自動車道|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}. * Parking: Free parking available.
== References == * Kaneko Nobuo. *Tamaki Town History, Volume II – History and Traditions of South Ise.* Mie Prefecture Local History Series No. 91, Mie Prefecture Local History Publication Association, December 10, 1983. * {{ill|Katsunoshin Sakurai|ja|桜井勝之進|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}. *Ise Grand Shrine.* Gakuseisha, May 20, 1969. * Katsunoshin Sakurai. *The Shrine of the Great deity of Ise.* Hori Shoten, March 25, 1973.
== Related Pages == {{commonscat|Kaze-no-miya (Geku)}} * {{ill|Shinatsuhiko|ja|シナツヒコ|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} * {{ill|Kamikaze|ja|神風|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} * {{ill|Mongol invasions of Japan|ja|元寇|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} * {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} * {{ill|List of Shrines and Facilities of Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮を構成する神社と施設|1=List of 125 Shrines of Ise|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}
== External Links == * [http://www.isejingu.or.jp/gegu/gegu5.htm Ise Jingu Official Site]

{{Infobox shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = | Name = Kazenomiya | location = Toyokawa-cho, [[Ise]], [[Mie Prefecture]], Japan | deity = [[Shinatsuhiko]] and [[Shinatobe]] | type = {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} auxiliary shrine (*betsugū*) | Established = Before 997 CE | Main Hall = [[Shinmei-zukuri]] | coordinates = {{coord|34|29|9.1|N|136|42|14.2|E|region:JP|display=inline,title}} | festival = Kazahi-no-Matsuri (*Wind Day Prayer Festival*) }}
'''Kaze-no-Miya''' (風宮) is an auxiliary shrine (*betsugū*) located within the precincts of {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} of the [[Ise Grand Shrine]] in [[Toyokawa-cho]], [[Ise]], [[Mie Prefecture]], Japan. == Overview== [[File:Kame-ish 01.JPG|left|thumb|180px|Kame-ishi (Turtle Stone)]] '''Kazenomiya''' is a {{ill|Betsugū (detached shrine)|ja|別宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} of the {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Gekū), located at the foot of **Hinokioyama** (檜尾山), south of the main shrine. The enshrined deities are {{ill|Shinatsuhiko|ja|級長津彦命|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Shinatobe|ja|級長戸辺命|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the same as those in the {{ill|Kazahinomi-no-Miya|ja|風日祈宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, a detached shrine of the {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Naikū).
'''Kazenomiya''' is situated to the left after crossing the **Kame-ishi** (亀石), a stone along the approach to {{ill|Taga-no-Miya|ja|多賀宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, next to the pond in front of the main shrine. It is said that Kame-ishi was transported from the entrance of the **Ama-no-Iwato** (天岩戸) on {{ill|Mount Takakura (Ise)|ja|高倉山 (伊勢市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
The term *Betsugū* (別宮) means "separate shrine," considered second in reverence only to the main shrine. Apart from '''Kazenomiya''', the Gekū has other Betsugū: {{ill|Taka-no-Miya|ja|多賀宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Tsuchi-no-Miya|ja|土宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} within the precincts, and {{ill|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|ja|月夜見宮|8=ja|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} outside the precincts. Kazamiya was the last to be designated as a Betsugū in {{ill|1293|ja|1293rd year|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Shōō 6) and is Ranked fourth among the four Betsugū.
Originally, it was known as **Kazeya-shiro** (風社) or **Kazejinja** (風神社), a shrine of lesser Rank. However, following the {{ill|Mongol invasions of Japan|ja|元寇|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in {{ill|1281|ja|1281st year|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Kōan 4), when divine winds (*kamikaze*) protected Japan, it was elevated to the status of Betsugū.
== Enshrined Deities == The enshrined deities of {{ill|Kaze-no-Miya|ja|風宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} are {{ill|Shinatsuhiko|ja|シナツヒコ|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Shinatobe|ja|シナトベ|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, gods who govern wind and rain. Originally worshipped as deities bringing favorable weather for agriculture, after the Mongol invasions, they became objects of prayers for the salvation of Japan in times of national crisis.
== History == The origins of Kaze-no-Miya are unclear, but the earliest reference appears in the "Chōtoku Kenroku" from {{ill|997|ja|997th year|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Chōtoku 3), citing it as "Kaze-sha," Ranked 4th among 32 rice field shrines under the jurisdiction of the Gekū, as noted in {{ill|Watarai Ieyuki|ja|度会家行|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}'s "Ruiju Jingi Hongen." "Rice field shrines" correspond to the current末社 (massha), considered lower than sessha (auxiliary shrines).
Although initially Ranked as a末社, due to its deities’ importance in agriculture, the {{ill|Kazahi-no-Matsuri|ja|風日祈祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} was held, and offerings of "kakechikara" (bundled rice stalks) were made during the {{ill|Kannamesai|ja|神嘗祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
During the {{ill|Mongol invasions of Japan|ja|元寇|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in {{ill|1281|ja|1281st year|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Kōan 4), the court dispatched the Dainagon Tameuji to perform prayers at both the Naikū's Kaze-sha and this shrine. After the retreat of the Mongol forces, the victory was attributed to the "Divine Wind" ({{ill|Kamikaze|ja|神風|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}), and in {{ill|1293|ja|1293rd year|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Shōō 6), the shrine was elevated to a betsu-gū (detached shrine), named {{ill|Kazahi-no-Miya|ja|風日祈宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and Kaze-no-Miya respectively.
During the late {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, when Western nations visited Japan, the court held 15 days of prayers for the expulsion of foreigners at Kaze-no-Miya and Kazahi-no-Miya in {{ill|1863|ja|1863rd year|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Bunkyū 3).
In {{ill|1876|ja|1876th year|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Meiji 9), during the {{ill|Ise Riot|ja|伊勢暴動|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the sacred object from {{ill|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|ja|月夜見宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} was temporarily moved to Kaze-no-Miya to protect it from a nearby fire.<ref>Kaneko (1983): p.214</ref>
== Festivals == The {{ill|Kazahi-no-Matsuri|ja|風日祈祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, praying for favorable weather and abundant harvests, is held annually on May 14 and August 4.
== Shrine Building == The shrine building of Kaze-no-Miya faces south and is constructed in the {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|ja|神明造|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} style with a thatched roof, featuring {{ill|Chigi and Katsuogi|ja|千木・鰹木|lt=chigi and katsuogi|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} with five odd-numbered katsuogi, following the Gekū’s design. The old shrine site for reconstruction (shikinen sengū) is adjacent to the east and west.
== Access == * Nearest Station: About a 12-minute walk from {{ill|Ise-shi Station|ja|伊勢市駅|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} via {{ill|JR Sangu Line|ja|参宮線|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Kintetsu|ja|近畿日本鉄道|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. * Nearest Bus Stop: About a 5-minute walk from {{ill|Mie Kotsu|ja|三重交通|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} Gekū-mae Bus Stop. * Nearest Interchange: About 2 km from {{ill|Ise Nishi Interchange|ja|伊勢西インターチェンジ|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} on the {{ill|Ise Expressway|ja|伊勢自動車道|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. * Parking: Free parking available.
== References == * Kaneko Nobuo. *Tamaki Town History, Volume II – History and Traditions of South Ise.* Mie Prefecture Local History Series No. 91, Mie Prefecture Local History Publication Association, December 10, 1983. * {{ill|Katsunoshin Sakurai|ja|桜井勝之進|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. *Ise Grand Shrine.* Gakuseisha, May 20, 1969. * Katsunoshin Sakurai. *The Shrine of the Great deity of Ise.* Hori Shoten, March 25, 1973.
== Related Pages == {{commonscat|Kaze-no-miya (Geku)}} * {{ill|Shinatsuhiko|ja|シナツヒコ|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Kamikaze|ja|神風|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Mongol invasions of Japan|ja|元寇|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|List of Shrines and Facilities of Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮を構成する神社と施設|1=List of 125 Shrines of Ise|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}
== External Links == * [http://www.isejingu.or.jp/gegu/gegu5.htm Ise Jingu Official Site]

The term *Betsugū* (別宮) means "separate shrine," considered second in reverence only to the main shrine. Apart from '''Kazenomiya''', the Gekū has other Betsugū: {{ill|Taka-no-Miya|ja|多賀宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taka-no-Miya|qq=}} and {{ill|Tsuchi-no-Miya|ja|土宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchi-no-Miya|qq=}} within the precincts, and {{ill|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|ja|月夜見宮|8=ja|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|qq=}} outside the precincts. Kazamiya was the last to be designated as a Betsugū in {{ill|1293|ja|1293rd year|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1293|qq=}} (Shōō 6) and is Ranked fourth among the four Betsugū.
Originally, it was known as **Kazeya-shiro** (風社) or **Kazejinja** (風神社), a shrine of lesser Rank. However, following the {{ill|Mongol invasions of Japan|ja|元寇|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mongol invasions of Japan|qq=}} in {{ill|1281|ja|1281st year|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1281|qq=}} (Kōan 4), when divine winds (*kamikaze*) protected Japan, it was elevated to the status of Betsugū.
== Enshrined Deities == The enshrined deities of {{ill|Kaze-no-Miya|ja|風宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaze-no-Miya|qq=}} are {{ill|Shinatsuhiko|ja|シナツヒコ|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinatsuhiko|qq=}} and {{ill|Shinatobe|ja|シナトベ|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinatobe|qq=}}, gods who govern wind and rain. Originally worshipped as deities bringing favorable weather for agriculture, after the Mongol invasions, they became objects of prayers for the salvation of Japan in times of national crisis.
== History == The origins of Kaze-no-Miya are unclear, but the earliest reference appears in the "Chōtoku Kenroku" from {{ill|997|ja|997th year|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/997|qq=}} (Chōtoku 3), citing it as "Kaze-sha," Ranked 4th among 32 rice field shrines under the jurisdiction of the Gekū, as noted in {{ill|Watarai Ieyuki|ja|度会家行|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Watarai Ieyuki|qq=}}'s "Ruiju Jingi Hongen." "Rice field shrines" correspond to the current末社 (massha), considered lower than sessha (auxiliary shrines).
Although initially Ranked as a末社, due to its deities’ importance in agriculture, the {{ill|Kazahi-no-Matsuri|ja|風日祈祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kazahi-no-Matsuri|qq=}} was held, and offerings of "kakechikara" (bundled rice stalks) were made during the {{ill|Kannamesai|ja|神嘗祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kannamesai|qq=}}.
During the {{ill|Mongol invasions of Japan|ja|元寇|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mongol invasions of Japan|qq=}} in {{ill|1281|ja|1281st year|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1281|qq=}} (Kōan 4), the court dispatched the Dainagon Tameuji to perform prayers at both the Naikū's Kaze-sha and this shrine. After the retreat of the Mongol forces, the victory was attributed to the "Divine Wind" ({{ill|Kamikaze|ja|神風|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamikaze|qq=}}), and in {{ill|1293|ja|1293rd year|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1293|qq=}} (Shōō 6), the shrine was elevated to a betsu-gū (detached shrine), named {{ill|Kazahi-no-Miya|ja|風日祈宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kazahi-no-Miya|qq=}} and Kaze-no-Miya respectively.
During the late {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Edo period|qq=}}, when Western nations visited Japan, the court held 15 days of prayers for the expulsion of foreigners at Kaze-no-Miya and Kazahi-no-Miya in {{ill|1863|ja|1863rd year|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1863|qq=}} (Bunkyū 3).
In {{ill|1876|ja|1876th year|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1876|qq=}} (Meiji 9), during the {{ill|Ise Riot|ja|伊勢暴動|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Riot|qq=}}, the sacred object from {{ill|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|ja|月夜見宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|qq=}} was temporarily moved to Kaze-no-Miya to protect it from a nearby fire.<ref>Kaneko (1983): p.214</ref>
== Festivals == The {{ill|Kazahi-no-Matsuri|ja|風日祈祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kazahi-no-Matsuri|qq=}}, praying for favorable weather and abundant harvests, is held annually on May 14 and August 4.
== Shrine Building == The shrine building of Kaze-no-Miya faces south and is constructed in the {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|ja|神明造|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinmei-zukuri|qq=}} style with a thatched roof, featuring {{ill|Chigi and Katsuogi|ja|千木・鰹木|lt=chigi and katsuogi|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chigi and Katsuogi|qq=}} with five odd-numbered katsuogi, following the Gekū’s design. The old shrine site for reconstruction (shikinen sengū) is adjacent to the east and west.
== Access == * Nearest Station: About a 12-minute walk from {{ill|Ise-shi Station|ja|伊勢市駅|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise-shi Station|qq=}} via {{ill|JR Sangu Line|ja|参宮線|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/JR Sangu Line|qq=}} and {{ill|Kintetsu|ja|近畿日本鉄道|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kintetsu|qq=}}. * Nearest Bus Stop: About a 5-minute walk from {{ill|Mie Kotsu|ja|三重交通|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mie Kotsu|qq=}} Gekū-mae Bus Stop. * Nearest Interchange: About 2 km from {{ill|Ise Nishi Interchange|ja|伊勢西インターチェンジ|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Nishi Interchange|qq=}} on the {{ill|Ise Expressway|ja|伊勢自動車道|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Expressway|qq=}}. * Parking: Free parking available.
== References == * Kaneko Nobuo. *Tamaki Town History, Volume II – History and Traditions of South Ise.* Mie Prefecture Local History Series No. 91, Mie Prefecture Local History Publication Association, December 10, 1983. * {{ill|Katsunoshin Sakurai|ja|桜井勝之進|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katsunoshin Sakurai|qq=}}. *Ise Grand Shrine.* Gakuseisha, May 20, 1969. * Katsunoshin Sakurai. *The Shrine of the Great deity of Ise.* Hori Shoten, March 25, 1973.
== Related Pages == {{commonscat|Kaze-no-miya (Geku)}} * {{ill|Shinatsuhiko|ja|シナツヒコ|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinatsuhiko|qq=}} * {{ill|Kamikaze|ja|神風|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamikaze|qq=}} * {{ill|Mongol invasions of Japan|ja|元寇|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mongol invasions of Japan|qq=}} * {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke Daijingu|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke Daijingu|qq=}} * {{ill|List of Shrines and Facilities of Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮を構成する神社と施設|1=List of 125 Shrines of Ise|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Shrines and Facilities of Ise Grand Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/List of 125 Shrines of Ise|qq=}}
== External Links == * [http://www.isejingu.or.jp/gegu/gegu5.htm Ise Jingu Official Site]

{{Infobox shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = | Name = Kazenomiya | location = Toyokawa-cho, [[Ise]], [[Mie Prefecture]], Japan | deity = [[Shinatsuhiko]] and [[Shinatobe]] | type = {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke Daijingu|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke Daijingu|qq=}} auxiliary shrine (*betsugū*) | Established = Before 997 CE | Main Hall = [[Shinmei-zukuri]] | coordinates = {{coord|34|29|9.1|N|136|42|14.2|E|region:JP|display=inline,title}} | festival = Kazahi-no-Matsuri (*Wind Day Prayer Festival*) }}
'''Kaze-no-Miya''' (風宮) is an auxiliary shrine (*betsugū*) located within the precincts of {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke Daijingu|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke Daijingu|qq=}} of the [[Ise Grand Shrine]] in [[Toyokawa-cho]], [[Ise]], [[Mie Prefecture]], Japan. == Overview== [[File:Kame-ish 01.JPG|left|thumb|180px|Kame-ishi (Turtle Stone)]] '''Kazenomiya''' is a {{ill|Betsugū (detached shrine)|ja|別宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Betsugū (detached shrine)|qq=}} of the {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke Daijingu|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke Daijingu|qq=}} (Gekū), located at the foot of **Hinokioyama** (檜尾山), south of the main shrine. The enshrined deities are {{ill|Shinatsuhiko|ja|級長津彦命|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinatsuhiko|qq=}} and {{ill|Shinatobe|ja|級長戸辺命|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinatobe|qq=}}, the same as those in the {{ill|Kazahinomi-no-Miya|ja|風日祈宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kazahinomi-no-Miya|qq=}}, a detached shrine of the {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|qq=}} (Naikū).
'''Kazenomiya''' is situated to the left after crossing the **Kame-ishi** (亀石), a stone along the approach to {{ill|Taga-no-Miya|ja|多賀宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taga-no-Miya|qq=}}, next to the pond in front of the main shrine. It is said that Kame-ishi was transported from the entrance of the **Ama-no-Iwato** (天岩戸) on {{ill|Mount Takakura (Ise)|ja|高倉山 (伊勢市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Takakura (Ise)|qq=}}.
The term *Betsugū* (別宮) means "separate shrine," considered second in reverence only to the main shrine. Apart from '''Kazenomiya''', the Gekū has other Betsugū: {{ill|Taka-no-Miya|ja|多賀宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taka-no-Miya|qq=}} and {{ill|Tsuchi-no-Miya|ja|土宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchi-no-Miya|qq=}} within the precincts, and {{ill|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|ja|月夜見宮|8=ja|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|qq=}} outside the precincts. Kazamiya was the last to be designated as a Betsugū in {{ill|1293|ja|1293rd year|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1293|qq=}} (Shōō 6) and is Ranked fourth among the four Betsugū.
Originally, it was known as **Kazeya-shiro** (風社) or **Kazejinja** (風神社), a shrine of lesser Rank. However, following the {{ill|Mongol invasions of Japan|ja|元寇|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mongol invasions of Japan|qq=}} in {{ill|1281|ja|1281st year|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1281|qq=}} (Kōan 4), when divine winds (*kamikaze*) protected Japan, it was elevated to the status of Betsugū.
== Enshrined Deities == The enshrined deities of {{ill|Kaze-no-Miya|ja|風宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaze-no-Miya|qq=}} are {{ill|Shinatsuhiko|ja|シナツヒコ|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinatsuhiko|qq=}} and {{ill|Shinatobe|ja|シナトベ|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinatobe|qq=}}, gods who govern wind and rain. Originally worshipped as deities bringing favorable weather for agriculture, after the Mongol invasions, they became objects of prayers for the salvation of Japan in times of national crisis.
== History == The origins of Kaze-no-Miya are unclear, but the earliest reference appears in the "Chōtoku Kenroku" from {{ill|997|ja|997th year|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/997|qq=}} (Chōtoku 3), citing it as "Kaze-sha," Ranked 4th among 32 rice field shrines under the jurisdiction of the Gekū, as noted in {{ill|Watarai Ieyuki|ja|度会家行|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Watarai Ieyuki|qq=}}'s "Ruiju Jingi Hongen." "Rice field shrines" correspond to the current末社 (massha), considered lower than sessha (auxiliary shrines).
Although initially Ranked as a末社, due to its deities’ importance in agriculture, the {{ill|Kazahi-no-Matsuri|ja|風日祈祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kazahi-no-Matsuri|qq=}} was held, and offerings of "kakechikara" (bundled rice stalks) were made during the {{ill|Kannamesai|ja|神嘗祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kannamesai|qq=}}.
During the {{ill|Mongol invasions of Japan|ja|元寇|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mongol invasions of Japan|qq=}} in {{ill|1281|ja|1281st year|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1281|qq=}} (Kōan 4), the court dispatched the Dainagon Tameuji to perform prayers at both the Naikū's Kaze-sha and this shrine. After the retreat of the Mongol forces, the victory was attributed to the "Divine Wind" ({{ill|Kamikaze|ja|神風|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamikaze|qq=}}), and in {{ill|1293|ja|1293rd year|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1293|qq=}} (Shōō 6), the shrine was elevated to a betsu-gū (detached shrine), named {{ill|Kazahi-no-Miya|ja|風日祈宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kazahi-no-Miya|qq=}} and Kaze-no-Miya respectively.
During the late {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Edo period|qq=}}, when Western nations visited Japan, the court held 15 days of prayers for the expulsion of foreigners at Kaze-no-Miya and Kazahi-no-Miya in {{ill|1863|ja|1863rd year|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1863|qq=}} (Bunkyū 3).
In {{ill|1876|ja|1876th year|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1876|qq=}} (Meiji 9), during the {{ill|Ise Riot|ja|伊勢暴動|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Riot|qq=}}, the sacred object from {{ill|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|ja|月夜見宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|qq=}} was temporarily moved to Kaze-no-Miya to protect it from a nearby fire.<ref>Kaneko (1983): p.214</ref>
== Festivals == The {{ill|Kazahi-no-Matsuri|ja|風日祈祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kazahi-no-Matsuri|qq=}}, praying for favorable weather and abundant harvests, is held annually on May 14 and August 4.
== Shrine Building == The shrine building of Kaze-no-Miya faces south and is constructed in the {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|ja|神明造|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinmei-zukuri|qq=}} style with a thatched roof, featuring {{ill|Chigi and Katsuogi|ja|千木・鰹木|lt=chigi and katsuogi|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chigi and Katsuogi|qq=}} with five odd-numbered katsuogi, following the Gekū’s design. The old shrine site for reconstruction (shikinen sengū) is adjacent to the east and west.
== Access == * Nearest Station: About a 12-minute walk from {{ill|Ise-shi Station|ja|伊勢市駅|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise-shi Station|qq=}} via {{ill|JR Sangu Line|ja|参宮線|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/JR Sangu Line|qq=}} and {{ill|Kintetsu|ja|近畿日本鉄道|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kintetsu|qq=}}. * Nearest Bus Stop: About a 5-minute walk from {{ill|Mie Kotsu|ja|三重交通|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mie Kotsu|qq=}} Gekū-mae Bus Stop. * Nearest Interchange: About 2 km from {{ill|Ise Nishi Interchange|ja|伊勢西インターチェンジ|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Nishi Interchange|qq=}} on the {{ill|Ise Expressway|ja|伊勢自動車道|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Expressway|qq=}}. * Parking: Free parking available.
== References == * Kaneko Nobuo. *Tamaki Town History, Volume II – History and Traditions of South Ise.* Mie Prefecture Local History Series No. 91, Mie Prefecture Local History Publication Association, December 10, 1983. * {{ill|Katsunoshin Sakurai|ja|桜井勝之進|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katsunoshin Sakurai|qq=}}. *Ise Grand Shrine.* Gakuseisha, May 20, 1969. * Katsunoshin Sakurai. *The Shrine of the Great deity of Ise.* Hori Shoten, March 25, 1973.

{{Infobox shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = | Name = Kazenomiya | location = Toyokawa-cho, [[Ise]], [[Mie Prefecture]], Japan | deity = [[Shinatsuhiko]] and [[Shinatobe]] | type = {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke Daijingu|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke Daijingu|qq=}} auxiliary shrine (*betsugū*) | Established = Before 997 CE | Main Hall = [[Shinmei-zukuri]] | coordinates = {{coord|34|29|9.1|N|136|42|14.2|E|region:JP|display=inline,title}} | festival = Kazahi-no-Matsuri (*Wind Day Prayer Festival*) }}
'''Kaze-no-Miya''' (風宮) is an auxiliary shrine (*betsugū*) located within the precincts of {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke Daijingu|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke Daijingu|qq=}} of the [[Ise Grand Shrine]] in [[Toyokawa-cho]], [[Ise]], [[Mie Prefecture]], Japan. == Overview== [[File:Kame-ish 01.JPG|left|thumb|180px|Kame-ishi (Turtle Stone)]] '''Kazenomiya''' is a {{ill|Betsugū (detached shrine)|ja|別宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Betsugū (detached shrine)|qq=}} of the {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke Daijingu|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke Daijingu|qq=}} (Gekū), located at the foot of **Hinokioyama** (檜尾山), south of the main shrine. The enshrined deities are {{ill|Shinatsuhiko|ja|級長津彦命|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinatsuhiko|qq=}} and {{ill|Shinatobe|ja|級長戸辺命|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinatobe|qq=}}, the same as those in the {{ill|Kazahinomi-no-Miya|ja|風日祈宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kazahinomi-no-Miya|qq=}}, a detached shrine of the {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|qq=}} (Naikū).
'''Kazenomiya''' is situated to the left after crossing the **Kame-ishi** (亀石), a stone along the approach to {{ill|Taga-no-Miya|ja|多賀宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taga-no-Miya|qq=}}, next to the pond in front of the main shrine. It is said that Kame-ishi was transported from the entrance of the **Ama-no-Iwato** (天岩戸) on {{ill|Mount Takakura (Ise)|ja|高倉山 (伊勢市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Takakura (Ise)|qq=}}.
The term *Betsugū* (別宮) means "separate shrine," considered second in reverence only to the main shrine. Apart from '''Kazenomiya''', the Gekū has other Betsugū: {{ill|Taka-no-Miya|ja|多賀宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taka-no-Miya|qq=}} and {{ill|Tsuchi-no-Miya|ja|土宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchi-no-Miya|qq=}} within the precincts, and {{ill|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|ja|月夜見宮|1=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|2=ja|3=月夜見宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|8=ja|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|qq=}} outside the precincts. Kazamiya was the last to be designated as a Betsugū in {{ill|1293|ja|1293rd year|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1293|qq=}} (Shōō 6) and is Ranked fourth among the four Betsugū.
Originally, it was known as **Kazeya-shiro** (風社) or **Kazejinja** (風神社), a shrine of lesser Rank. However, following the {{ill|Mongol invasions of Japan|ja|元寇|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mongol invasions of Japan|qq=}} in {{ill|1281|ja|1281st year|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1281|qq=}} (Kōan 4), when divine winds (*kamikaze*) protected Japan, it was elevated to the status of Betsugū.
== Enshrined Deities == The enshrined deities of {{ill|Kaze-no-Miya|ja|風宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaze-no-Miya|qq=}} are {{ill|Shinatsuhiko|ja|シナツヒコ|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinatsuhiko|qq=}} and {{ill|Shinatobe|ja|シナトベ|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinatobe|qq=}}, gods who govern wind and rain. Originally worshipped as deities bringing favorable weather for agriculture, after the Mongol invasions, they became objects of prayers for the salvation of Japan in times of national crisis.
== History == The origins of Kaze-no-Miya are unclear, but the earliest reference appears in the "Chōtoku Kenroku" from {{ill|997|ja|997th year|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/997|qq=}} (Chōtoku 3), citing it as "Kaze-sha," Ranked 4th among 32 rice field shrines under the jurisdiction of the Gekū, as noted in {{ill|Watarai Ieyuki|ja|度会家行|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Watarai Ieyuki|qq=}}'s "Ruiju Jingi Hongen." "Rice field shrines" correspond to the current末社 (massha), considered lower than sessha (auxiliary shrines).
Although initially Ranked as a末社, due to its deities’ importance in agriculture, the {{ill|Kazahi-no-Matsuri|ja|風日祈祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kazahi-no-Matsuri|qq=}} was held, and offerings of "kakechikara" (bundled rice stalks) were made during the {{ill|Kannamesai|ja|神嘗祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kannamesai|qq=}}.
During the {{ill|Mongol invasions of Japan|ja|元寇|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mongol invasions of Japan|qq=}} in {{ill|1281|ja|1281st year|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1281|qq=}} (Kōan 4), the court dispatched the Dainagon Tameuji to perform prayers at both the Naikū's Kaze-sha and this shrine. After the retreat of the Mongol forces, the victory was attributed to the "Divine Wind" ({{ill|Kamikaze|ja|神風|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamikaze|qq=}}), and in {{ill|1293|ja|1293rd year|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1293|qq=}} (Shōō 6), the shrine was elevated to a betsu-gū (detached shrine), named {{ill|Kazahi-no-Miya|ja|風日祈宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kazahi-no-Miya|qq=}} and Kaze-no-Miya respectively.
During the late {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Edo period|qq=}}, when Western nations visited Japan, the court held 15 days of prayers for the expulsion of foreigners at Kaze-no-Miya and Kazahi-no-Miya in {{ill|1863|ja|1863rd year|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1863|qq=}} (Bunkyū 3).
In {{ill|1876|ja|1876th year|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1876|qq=}} (Meiji 9), during the {{ill|Ise Riot|ja|伊勢暴動|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Riot|qq=}}, the sacred object from {{ill|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|ja|月夜見宮|1=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|2=ja|3=月夜見宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|qq=}} was temporarily moved to Kaze-no-Miya to protect it from a nearby fire.<ref>Kaneko (1983): p.214</ref>
== Festivals == The {{ill|Kazahi-no-Matsuri|ja|風日祈祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kazahi-no-Matsuri|qq=}}, praying for favorable weather and abundant harvests, is held annually on May 14 and August 4.
== Shrine Building == The shrine building of Kaze-no-Miya faces south and is constructed in the {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|ja|神明造|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinmei-zukuri|qq=}} style with a thatched roof, featuring {{ill|Chigi and Katsuogi|ja|千木・鰹木|lt=chigi and katsuogi|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chigi and Katsuogi|qq=}} with five odd-numbered katsuogi, following the Gekū’s design. The old shrine site for reconstruction (shikinen sengū) is adjacent to the east and west.
== Access == * Nearest Station: About a 12-minute walk from {{ill|Ise-shi Station|ja|伊勢市駅|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise-shi Station|qq=}} via {{ill|JR Sangu Line|ja|参宮線|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/JR Sangu Line|qq=}} and {{ill|Kintetsu|ja|近畿日本鉄道|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kintetsu|qq=}}. * Nearest Bus Stop: About a 5-minute walk from {{ill|Mie Kotsu|ja|三重交通|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mie Kotsu|qq=}} Gekū-mae Bus Stop. * Nearest Interchange: About 2 km from {{ill|Ise Nishi Interchange|ja|伊勢西インターチェンジ|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Nishi Interchange|qq=}} on the {{ill|Ise Expressway|ja|伊勢自動車道|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Expressway|qq=}}. * Parking: Free parking available.
== References == * Kaneko Nobuo. *Tamaki Town History, Volume II – History and Traditions of South Ise.* Mie Prefecture Local History Series No. 91, Mie Prefecture Local History Publication Association, December 10, 1983. * {{ill|Katsunoshin Sakurai|ja|桜井勝之進|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katsunoshin Sakurai|qq=}}. *Ise Grand Shrine.* Gakuseisha, May 20, 1969. * Katsunoshin Sakurai. *The Shrine of the Great deity of Ise.* Hori Shoten, March 25, 1973.
== Related Pages == {{commonscat|Kaze-no-miya (Geku)}} * {{ill|Shinatsuhiko|ja|シナツヒコ|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinatsuhiko|qq=}} * {{ill|Kamikaze|ja|神風|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamikaze|qq=}} * {{ill|Mongol invasions of Japan|ja|元寇|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mongol invasions of Japan|qq=}} * {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke Daijingu|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke Daijingu|qq=}} * {{ill|List of Shrines and Facilities of Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮を構成する神社と施設|1=List of 125 Shrines of Ise|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Shrines and Facilities of Ise Grand Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/List of 125 Shrines of Ise|qq=}}
== External Links == * [http://www.isejingu.or.jp/gegu/gegu5.htm Ise Jingu Official Site]

== Shrine Building == The shrine building of Kaze-no-Miya faces south and is constructed in the {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|ja|神明造|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinmei-zukuri|qq=}} style with a thatched roof, featuring {{ill|Chigi and Katsuogi|ja|千木・鰹木|lt=chigi and katsuogi|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chigi and Katsuogi|qq=}} with five odd-numbered katsuogi, following the Gekū’s design. The old shrine site for reconstruction (shikinen sengū) is adjacent to the east and west.
== Access == * Nearest Station: About a 12-minute walk from {{ill|Ise-shi Station|ja|伊勢市駅|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise-shi Station|qq=}} via {{ill|JR Sangu Line|ja|参宮線|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/JR Sangu Line|qq=}} and {{ill|Kintetsu|ja|近畿日本鉄道|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kintetsu|qq=}}. * Nearest Bus Stop: About a 5-minute walk from {{ill|Mie Kotsu|ja|三重交通|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mie Kotsu|qq=}} Gekū-mae Bus Stop. * Nearest Interchange: About 2 km from {{ill|Ise Nishi Interchange|ja|伊勢西インターチェンジ|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Nishi Interchange|qq=}} on the {{ill|Ise Expressway|ja|伊勢自動車道|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Expressway|qq=}}. * Parking: Free parking available.
== References == * Kaneko Nobuo. *Tamaki Town History, Volume II – History and Traditions of South Ise.* Mie Prefecture Local History Series No. 91, Mie Prefecture Local History Publication Association, December 10, 1983. * {{ill|Katsunoshin Sakurai|ja|桜井勝之進|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katsunoshin Sakurai|qq=}}. *Ise Grand Shrine.* Gakuseisha, May 20, 1969. * Katsunoshin Sakurai. *The Shrine of the Great deity of Ise.* Hori Shoten, March 25, 1973.

[[image:Ise-Shrine Geku-Kazenomiya.jpg|256px|Kazenomiya]] [[sn:Kaze-no-Miya]] [[ja:風宮]] [[en:Kaze-no-Miya]]
<!--Kaze-no-Miya-->

{{Infobox shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = | Name = Kazenomiya | location = Toyokawa-cho, [[Ise]], [[Mie Prefecture]], Japan | deity = [[Shinatsuhiko]] and [[Shinatobe]] | type = {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke Daijingu|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke Daijingu|qq=}} auxiliary shrine (*betsugū*) | Established = Before 997 CE | Main Hall = [[Shinmei-zukuri]] | coordinates = {{coord|34|29|9.1|N|136|42|14.2|E|region:JP|display=inline,title}} | festival = Kazahi-no-Matsuri (*Wind Day Prayer Festival*) }}
'''Kaze-no-Miya''' (風宮) is an auxiliary shrine (*betsugū*) located within the precincts of {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke Daijingu|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke Daijingu|qq=}} of the [[Ise Grand Shrine]] in [[Toyokawa-cho]], [[Ise]], [[Mie Prefecture]], Japan. == Overview== [[File:Kame-ish 01.JPG|left|thumb|180px|Kame-ishi (Turtle Stone)]] '''Kazenomiya''' is a {{ill|Betsugū (detached shrine)|ja|別宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Betsugū (detached shrine)|qq=}} of the {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke Daijingu|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke Daijingu|qq=}} (Gekū), located at the foot of **Hinokioyama** (檜尾山), south of the main shrine. The enshrined deities are {{ill|Shinatsuhiko|ja|級長津彦命|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinatsuhiko|qq=}} and {{ill|Shinatobe|ja|級長戸辺命|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinatobe|qq=}}, the same as those in the {{ill|Kazahinomi-no-Miya|ja|風日祈宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kazahinomi-no-Miya|qq=}}, a detached shrine of the {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|qq=}} (Naikū).
'''Kazenomiya''' is situated to the left after crossing the **Kame-ishi** (亀石), a stone along the approach to {{ill|Taga-no-Miya|ja|多賀宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taga-no-Miya|qq=}}, next to the pond in front of the main shrine. It is said that Kame-ishi was transported from the entrance of the **Ama-no-Iwato** (天岩戸) on {{ill|Mount Takakura (Ise)|ja|高倉山 (伊勢市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Takakura (Ise)|qq=}}.
The term *Betsugū* (別宮) means "separate shrine," considered second in reverence only to the main shrine. Apart from '''Kazenomiya''', the Gekū has other Betsugū: {{ill|Taka-no-Miya|ja|多賀宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taka-no-Miya|qq=}} and {{ill|Tsuchi-no-Miya|ja|土宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchi-no-Miya|qq=}} within the precincts, and {{ill|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|1=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|2=ja|3=月夜見宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|ja|月夜見宮|1=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|2=ja|3=月夜見宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|8=ja|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|1=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|2=ja|3=月夜見宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|qq=}} outside the precincts. Kazamiya was the last to be designated as a Betsugū in {{ill|1293|ja|1293rd year|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1293|qq=}} (Shōō 6) and is Ranked fourth among the four Betsugū.
Originally, it was known as **Kazeya-shiro** (風社) or **Kazejinja** (風神社), a shrine of lesser Rank. However, following the {{ill|Mongol invasions of Japan|ja|元寇|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mongol invasions of Japan|qq=}} in {{ill|1281|ja|1281st year|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1281|qq=}} (Kōan 4), when divine winds (*kamikaze*) protected Japan, it was elevated to the status of Betsugū.
== Enshrined Deities == The enshrined deities of {{ill|Kaze-no-Miya|ja|風宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaze-no-Miya|qq=}} are {{ill|Shinatsuhiko|ja|シナツヒコ|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinatsuhiko|qq=}} and {{ill|Shinatobe|ja|シナトベ|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinatobe|qq=}}, gods who govern wind and rain. Originally worshipped as deities bringing favorable weather for agriculture, after the Mongol invasions, they became objects of prayers for the salvation of Japan in times of national crisis.
== History == The origins of Kaze-no-Miya are unclear, but the earliest reference appears in the "Chōtoku Kenroku" from {{ill|997|ja|997th year|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/997|qq=}} (Chōtoku 3), citing it as "Kaze-sha," Ranked 4th among 32 rice field shrines under the jurisdiction of the Gekū, as noted in {{ill|Watarai Ieyuki|ja|度会家行|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Watarai Ieyuki|qq=}}'s "Ruiju Jingi Hongen." "Rice field shrines" correspond to the current末社 (massha), considered lower than sessha (auxiliary shrines).
Although initially Ranked as a末社, due to its deities’ importance in agriculture, the {{ill|Kazahi-no-Matsuri|ja|風日祈祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kazahi-no-Matsuri|qq=}} was held, and offerings of "kakechikara" (bundled rice stalks) were made during the {{ill|Kannamesai|ja|神嘗祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kannamesai|qq=}}.
During the {{ill|Mongol invasions of Japan|ja|元寇|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mongol invasions of Japan|qq=}} in {{ill|1281|ja|1281st year|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1281|qq=}} (Kōan 4), the court dispatched the Dainagon Tameuji to perform prayers at both the Naikū's Kaze-sha and this shrine. After the retreat of the Mongol forces, the victory was attributed to the "Divine Wind" ({{ill|Kamikaze|ja|神風|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamikaze|qq=}}), and in {{ill|1293|ja|1293rd year|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1293|qq=}} (Shōō 6), the shrine was elevated to a betsu-gū (detached shrine), named {{ill|Kazahi-no-Miya|ja|風日祈宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kazahi-no-Miya|qq=}} and Kaze-no-Miya respectively.
During the late {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Edo period|qq=}}, when Western nations visited Japan, the court held 15 days of prayers for the expulsion of foreigners at Kaze-no-Miya and Kazahi-no-Miya in {{ill|1863|ja|1863rd year|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1863|qq=}} (Bunkyū 3).
In {{ill|1876|ja|1876th year|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1876|qq=}} (Meiji 9), during the {{ill|Ise Riot|ja|伊勢暴動|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Riot|qq=}}, the sacred object from {{ill|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|1=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|2=ja|3=月夜見宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|ja|月夜見宮|1=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|2=ja|3=月夜見宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|qq=}} was temporarily moved to Kaze-no-Miya to protect it from a nearby fire.<ref>Kaneko (1983): p.214</ref>
== Festivals == The {{ill|Kazahi-no-Matsuri|ja|風日祈祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kazahi-no-Matsuri|qq=}}, praying for favorable weather and abundant harvests, is held annually on May 14 and August 4.
== Shrine Building == The shrine building of Kaze-no-Miya faces south and is constructed in the {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|ja|神明造|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinmei-zukuri|qq=}} style with a thatched roof, featuring {{ill|Chigi and Katsuogi|ja|千木・鰹木|lt=chigi and katsuogi|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chigi and Katsuogi|qq=}} with five odd-numbered katsuogi, following the Gekū’s design. The old shrine site for reconstruction (shikinen sengū) is adjacent to the east and west.
== Access == * Nearest Station: About a 12-minute walk from {{ill|Ise-shi Station|ja|伊勢市駅|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise-shi Station|qq=}} via {{ill|JR Sangu Line|ja|参宮線|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/JR Sangu Line|qq=}} and {{ill|Kintetsu|ja|近畿日本鉄道|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kintetsu|qq=}}. * Nearest Bus Stop: About a 5-minute walk from {{ill|Mie Kotsu|ja|三重交通|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mie Kotsu|qq=}} Gekū-mae Bus Stop. * Nearest Interchange: About 2 km from {{ill|Ise Nishi Interchange|ja|伊勢西インターチェンジ|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Nishi Interchange|qq=}} on the {{ill|Ise Expressway|ja|伊勢自動車道|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Expressway|qq=}}. * Parking: Free parking available.
== References == * Kaneko Nobuo. *Tamaki Town History, Volume II – History and Traditions of South Ise.* Mie Prefecture Local History Series No. 91, Mie Prefecture Local History Publication Association, December 10, 1983. * {{ill|Katsunoshin Sakurai|ja|桜井勝之進|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katsunoshin Sakurai|qq=}}. *Ise Grand Shrine.* Gakuseisha, May 20, 1969. * Katsunoshin Sakurai. *The Shrine of the Great deity of Ise.* Hori Shoten, March 25, 1973.

== Enshrined Deities == The enshrined deities of {{ill|Kaze-no-Miya|ja|風宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaze-no-Miya|qq=}} are {{ill|Shinatsuhiko|ja|シナツヒコ|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinatsuhiko|qq=}} and {{ill|Shinatobe|ja|シナトベ|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinatobe|qq=}}, gods who govern wind and rain. Originally worshipped as deities bringing favorable weather for agriculture, after the Mongol invasions, they became objects of prayers for the salvation of Japan in times of national crisis.
== History == The origins of Kaze-no-Miya are unclear, but the earliest reference appears in the "Chōtoku Kenroku" from {{ill|997|ja|997th year|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/997|qq=}} (Chōtoku 3), citing it as "Kaze-sha," Ranked 4th among 32 rice field shrines under the jurisdiction of the Gekū, as noted in {{ill|Watarai Ieyuki|ja|度会家行|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Watarai Ieyuki|qq=}}'s "Ruiju Jingi Hongen." "Rice field shrines" correspond to the current末社 (massha), considered lower than sessha (auxiliary shrines).
Although initially Ranked as a末社, due to its deities’ importance in agriculture, the {{ill|Kazahi-no-Matsuri|ja|風日祈祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kazahi-no-Matsuri|qq=}} was held, and offerings of "kakechikara" (bundled rice stalks) were made during the {{ill|Kannamesai Festival|lt=Kannamesai Festival|lt=Kannamesai|ja|神嘗祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kannamesai|qq=}}.
During the {{ill|Mongol invasions of Japan|ja|元寇|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mongol invasions of Japan|qq=}} in {{ill|1281|ja|1281st year|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1281|qq=}} (Kōan 4), the court dispatched the Dainagon Tameuji to perform prayers at both the Naikū's Kaze-sha and this shrine. After the retreat of the Mongol forces, the victory was attributed to the "Divine Wind" ({{ill|Kamikaze|ja|神風|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamikaze|qq=}}), and in {{ill|1293|ja|1293rd year|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1293|qq=}} (Shōō 6), the shrine was elevated to a betsu-gū (detached shrine), named {{ill|Kazahi-no-Miya|ja|風日祈宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kazahi-no-Miya|qq=}} and Kaze-no-Miya respectively.
During the late {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Edo period|qq=}}, when Western nations visited Japan, the court held 15 days of prayers for the expulsion of foreigners at Kaze-no-Miya and Kazahi-no-Miya in {{ill|1863|ja|1863rd year|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1863|qq=}} (Bunkyū 3).
In {{ill|1876|ja|1876th year|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1876|qq=}} (Meiji 9), during the {{ill|Ise Riot|ja|伊勢暴動|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Riot|qq=}}, the sacred object from {{ill|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|1=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|2=ja|3=月夜見宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|ja|月夜見宮|1=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|2=ja|3=月夜見宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|qq=}} was temporarily moved to Kaze-no-Miya to protect it from a nearby fire.<ref>Kaneko (1983): p.214</ref>
== Festivals == The {{ill|Kazahi-no-Matsuri|ja|風日祈祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kazahi-no-Matsuri|qq=}}, praying for favorable weather and abundant harvests, is held annually on May 14 and August 4.
== Shrine Building == The shrine building of Kaze-no-Miya faces south and is constructed in the {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|ja|神明造|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinmei-zukuri|qq=}} style with a thatched roof, featuring {{ill|Chigi and Katsuogi|ja|千木・鰹木|lt=chigi and katsuogi|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chigi and Katsuogi|qq=}} with five odd-numbered katsuogi, following the Gekū’s design. The old shrine site for reconstruction (shikinen sengū) is adjacent to the east and west.
== Access == * Nearest Station: About a 12-minute walk from {{ill|Ise-shi Station|ja|伊勢市駅|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise-shi Station|qq=}} via {{ill|JR Sangu Line|ja|参宮線|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/JR Sangu Line|qq=}} and {{ill|Kintetsu|ja|近畿日本鉄道|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kintetsu|qq=}}. * Nearest Bus Stop: About a 5-minute walk from {{ill|Mie Kotsu|ja|三重交通|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mie Kotsu|qq=}} Gekū-mae Bus Stop. * Nearest Interchange: About 2 km from {{ill|Ise Nishi Interchange|ja|伊勢西インターチェンジ|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Nishi Interchange|qq=}} on the {{ill|Ise Expressway|ja|伊勢自動車道|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Expressway|qq=}}. * Parking: Free parking available.
== References == * Kaneko Nobuo. *Tamaki Town History, Volume II – History and Traditions of South Ise.* Mie Prefecture Local History Series No. 91, Mie Prefecture Local History Publication Association, December 10, 1983. * {{ill|Katsunoshin Sakurai|ja|桜井勝之進|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katsunoshin Sakurai|qq=}}. *Ise Grand Shrine.* Gakuseisha, May 20, 1969. * Katsunoshin Sakurai. *The Shrine of the Great deity of Ise.* Hori Shoten, March 25, 1973.
== Related Pages == {{commonscat|Kaze-no-miya (Geku)}} * {{ill|Shinatsuhiko|ja|シナツヒコ|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinatsuhiko|qq=}} * {{ill|Kamikaze|ja|神風|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamikaze|qq=}} * {{ill|Mongol invasions of Japan|ja|元寇|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mongol invasions of Japan|qq=}} * {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke Daijingu|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke Daijingu|qq=}} * {{ill|List of Shrines and Facilities of Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮を構成する神社と施設|1=List of 125 Shrines of Ise|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Shrines and Facilities of Ise Grand Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/List of 125 Shrines of Ise|qq=}}
== External Links == * [http://www.isejingu.or.jp/gegu/gegu5.htm Ise Jingu Official Site]

revision.text:
{{Infobox shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = | Name = Kazenomiya | location = Toyokawa-cho, [[Ise]], [[Mie Prefecture]], Japan | deity = [[Shinatsuhiko]] and [[Shinatobe]] | type = {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke Daijingu|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke Daijingu|qq=}} auxiliary shrine (*betsugū*) | Established = Before 997 CE | Main Hall = [[Shinmei-zukuri]] | coordinates = {{coord|34|29|9.1|N|136|42|14.2|E|region:JP|display=inline,title}} | festival = Kazahi-no-Matsuri (*Wind Day Prayer Festival*) }}
'''Kaze-no-Miya''' (風宮) is an auxiliary shrine (*betsugū*) located within the precincts of {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke Daijingu|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke Daijingu|qq=}} of the [[Ise Grand Shrine]] in [[Toyokawa-cho]], [[Ise]], [[Mie Prefecture]], Japan. == Overview== [[File:Kame-ish 01.JPG|left|thumb|180px|Kame-ishi (Turtle Stone)]] '''Kazenomiya''' is a {{ill|Betsugū (detached shrine)|ja|別宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Betsugū (detached shrine)|qq=}} of the {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke Daijingu|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke Daijingu|qq=}} (Gekū), located at the foot of **Hinokioyama** (檜尾山), south of the main shrine. The enshrined deities are {{ill|Shinatsuhiko|ja|級長津彦命|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinatsuhiko|qq=}} and {{ill|Shinatobe|ja|級長戸辺命|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinatobe|qq=}}, the same as those in the {{ill|Kazahinomi-no-Miya|ja|風日祈宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kazahinomi-no-Miya|qq=}}, a detached shrine of the {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|qq=}} (Naikū).
'''Kazenomiya''' is situated to the left after crossing the **Kame-ishi** (亀石), a stone along the approach to {{ill|Taga-no-Miya|ja|多賀宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taga-no-Miya|qq=}}, next to the pond in front of the main shrine. It is said that Kame-ishi was transported from the entrance of the **Ama-no-Iwato** (天岩戸) on {{ill|Mount Takakura (Ise)|ja|高倉山 (伊勢市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Takakura (Ise)|qq=}}.
The term *Betsugū* (別宮) means "separate shrine," considered second in reverence only to the main shrine. Apart from '''Kazenomiya''', the Gekū has other Betsugū: {{ill|Taka-no-Miya|ja|多賀宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taka-no-Miya|qq=}} and {{ill|Tsuchi-no-Miya|ja|土宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchi-no-Miya|qq=}} within the precincts, and {{ill|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|1=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|2=ja|3=月夜見宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|ja|月夜見宮|1=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|2=ja|3=月夜見宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|8=ja|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|1=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|2=ja|3=月夜見宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|qq=}} outside the precincts. Kazamiya was the last to be designated as a Betsugū in {{ill|1293|ja|1293rd year|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1293|qq=}} (Shōō 6) and is Ranked fourth among the four Betsugū.
Originally, it was known as **Kazeya-shiro** (風社) or **Kazejinja** (風神社), a shrine of lesser Rank. However, following the {{ill|Mongol invasions of Japan|ja|元寇|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mongol invasions of Japan|qq=}} in {{ill|1281|ja|1281st year|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1281|qq=}} (Kōan 4), when divine winds (*kamikaze*) protected Japan, it was elevated to the status of Betsugū.
== Enshrined Deities == The enshrined deities of {{ill|Kaze-no-Miya|ja|風宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaze-no-Miya|qq=}} are {{ill|Shinatsuhiko|ja|シナツヒコ|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinatsuhiko|qq=}} and {{ill|Shinatobe|ja|シナトベ|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinatobe|qq=}}, gods who govern wind and rain. Originally worshipped as deities bringing favorable weather for agriculture, after the Mongol invasions, they became objects of prayers for the salvation of Japan in times of national crisis.
== History == The origins of Kaze-no-Miya are unclear, but the earliest reference appears in the "Chōtoku Kenroku" from {{ill|997|ja|997th year|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/997|qq=}} (Chōtoku 3), citing it as "Kaze-sha," Ranked 4th among 32 rice field shrines under the jurisdiction of the Gekū, as noted in {{ill|Watarai Ieyuki|ja|度会家行|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Watarai Ieyuki|qq=}}'s "Ruiju Jingi Hongen." "Rice field shrines" correspond to the current末社 (massha), considered lower than sessha (auxiliary shrines).
Although initially Ranked as a末社, due to its deities’ importance in agriculture, the {{ill|Kazahi-no-Matsuri|ja|風日祈祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kazahi-no-Matsuri|qq=}} was held, and offerings of "kakechikara" (bundled rice stalks) were made during the {{ill|Kannamesai Festival|lt=Kannamesai Festival|lt=Kannamesai|ja|神嘗祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kannamesai|qq=}}.
During the {{ill|Mongol invasions of Japan|ja|元寇|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mongol invasions of Japan|qq=}} in {{ill|1281|ja|1281st year|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1281|qq=}} (Kōan 4), the court dispatched the Dainagon Tameuji to perform prayers at both the Naikū's Kaze-sha and this shrine. After the retreat of the Mongol forces, the victory was attributed to the "Divine Wind" ({{ill|Kamikaze|ja|神風|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamikaze|qq=}}), and in {{ill|1293|ja|1293rd year|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1293|qq=}} (Shōō 6), the shrine was elevated to a betsu-gū (detached shrine), named {{ill|Kazahi-no-Miya|ja|風日祈宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kazahi-no-Miya|qq=}} and Kaze-no-Miya respectively.
During the late {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Edo period|qq=}}, when Western nations visited Japan, the court held 15 days of prayers for the expulsion of foreigners at Kaze-no-Miya and Kazahi-no-Miya in {{ill|1863|ja|1863rd year|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1863|qq=}} (Bunkyū 3).
In {{ill|1876|ja|1876th year|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1876|qq=}} (Meiji 9), during the {{ill|Ise Riot|ja|伊勢暴動|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Riot|qq=}}, the sacred object from {{ill|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|1=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|2=ja|3=月夜見宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|ja|月夜見宮|1=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|2=ja|3=月夜見宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|qq=}} was temporarily moved to Kaze-no-Miya to protect it from a nearby fire.<ref>Kaneko (1983): p.214</ref>
== Festivals == The {{ill|Kazahi-no-Matsuri|ja|風日祈祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kazahi-no-Matsuri|qq=}}, praying for favorable weather and abundant harvests, is held annually on May 14 and August 4.
== Shrine Building == The shrine building of Kaze-no-Miya faces south and is constructed in the {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|ja|神明造|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinmei-zukuri|qq=}} style with a thatched roof, featuring {{ill|Chigi and Katsuogi|ja|千木・鰹木|lt=chigi and katsuogi|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chigi and Katsuogi|qq=}} with five odd-numbered katsuogi, following the Gekū’s design. The old shrine site for reconstruction (shikinen sengū) is adjacent to the east and west.
== Access == * Nearest Station: About a 12-minute walk from {{ill|Ise-shi Station|ja|伊勢市駅|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise-shi Station|qq=}} via {{ill|JR Sangu Line|ja|参宮線|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/JR Sangu Line|qq=}} and {{ill|Kintetsu|ja|近畿日本鉄道|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kintetsu|qq=}}. * Nearest Bus Stop: About a 5-minute walk from {{ill|Mie Kotsu|ja|三重交通|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mie Kotsu|qq=}} Gekū-mae Bus Stop. * Nearest Interchange: About 2 km from {{ill|Ise Nishi Interchange|ja|伊勢西インターチェンジ|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Nishi Interchange|qq=}} on the {{ill|Ise Expressway|ja|伊勢自動車道|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Expressway|qq=}}. * Parking: Free parking available.
== References == * Kaneko Nobuo. *Tamaki Town History, Volume II – History and Traditions of South Ise.* Mie Prefecture Local History Series No. 91, Mie Prefecture Local History Publication Association, December 10, 1983. * {{ill|Katsunoshin Sakurai|ja|桜井勝之進|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katsunoshin Sakurai|qq=}}. *Ise Grand Shrine.* Gakuseisha, May 20, 1969. * Katsunoshin Sakurai. *The Shrine of the Great deity of Ise.* Hori Shoten, March 25, 1973.
== Related Pages == {{commonscat|Kaze-no-miya (Geku)}} * {{ill|Shinatsuhiko|ja|シナツヒコ|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinatsuhiko|qq=}} * {{ill|Kamikaze|ja|神風|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamikaze|qq=}} * {{ill|Mongol invasions of Japan|ja|元寇|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mongol invasions of Japan|qq=}} * {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke Daijingu|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke Daijingu|qq=}} * {{ill|List of Shrines and Facilities of Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮を構成する神社と施設|1=List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|lt=List of 125 Shrines of Ise|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Shrines and Facilities of Ise Grand Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/List of 125 Shrines of Ise|qq=}}
