{{Infobox shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = | Name = Kazenomiya | location = Toyokawa-cho, [[Ise]], [[Mie Prefecture]], Japan | deity = [[Shinatsuhiko]] and [[Shinatobe]] | type = {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke Daijingu|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke Daijingu|qq=}} auxiliary shrine (*betsugū*) | Established = Before 997 CE | Main Hall = [[Shinmei-zukuri]] | coordinates = {{coord|34|29|9.1|N|136|42|14.2|E|region:JP|display=inline,title}} | festival = Kazahi-no-Matsuri (*Wind Day Prayer Festival*) }}
'''Kaze-no-Miya''' (風宮) is an auxiliary shrine (*betsugū*) located within the precincts of {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke Daijingu|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke Daijingu|qq=}} of the [[Ise Grand Shrine]] in [[Toyokawa-cho]], [[Ise]], [[Mie Prefecture]], Japan. == Overview== [[File:Kame-ish 01.JPG|left|thumb|180px|Kame-ishi (Turtle Stone)]] '''Kazenomiya''' is a {{ill|Betsugū (detached shrine)|ja|別宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Betsugū (detached shrine)|qq=}} of the {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke Daijingu|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke Daijingu|qq=}} (Gekū), located at the foot of **Hinokioyama** (檜尾山), south of the main shrine. The enshrined deities are {{ill|Shinatsuhiko|ja|級長津彦命|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinatsuhiko|qq=}} and {{ill|Shinatobe|ja|級長戸辺命|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinatobe|qq=}}, the same as those in the {{ill|Kazahinomi-no-Miya|ja|風日祈宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kazahinomi-no-Miya|qq=}}, a detached shrine of the {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|qq=}} (Naikū).
'''Kazenomiya''' is situated to the left after crossing the **Kame-ishi** (亀石), a stone along the approach to {{ill|Taka-no-Miya|lt=Taka-no-Miya|lt=Taga-no-Miya|ja|多賀宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taga-no-Miya|qq=}}, next to the pond in front of the main shrine. It is said that Kame-ishi was transported from the entrance of the **Ama-no-Iwato** (天岩戸) on {{ill|Mount Takakura (Ise)|ja|高倉山 (伊勢市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Takakura (Ise)|qq=}}.
The term *Betsugū* (別宮) means "separate shrine," considered second in reverence only to the main shrine. Apart from '''Kazenomiya''', the Gekū has other Betsugū: {{ill|Taka-no-Miya|ja|多賀宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taka-no-Miya|qq=}} and {{ill|Tsuchi-no-Miya|ja|土宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchi-no-Miya|qq=}} within the precincts, and {{ill|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|1=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|2=ja|3=月夜見宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|ja|月夜見宮|1=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|2=ja|3=月夜見宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|8=ja|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|1=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|2=ja|3=月夜見宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|qq=}} outside the precincts. Kazamiya was the last to be designated as a Betsugū in {{ill|1293|ja|1293rd year|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1293|qq=}} (Shōō 6) and is Ranked fourth among the four Betsugū.
Originally, it was known as **Kazeya-shiro** (風社) or **Kazejinja** (風神社), a shrine of lesser Rank. However, following the {{ill|Mongol invasions of Japan|ja|元寇|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mongol invasions of Japan|qq=}} in {{ill|1281|ja|1281st year|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1281|qq=}} (Kōan 4), when divine winds (*kamikaze*) protected Japan, it was elevated to the status of Betsugū.
== Enshrined Deities == The enshrined deities of {{ill|Kaze-no-Miya|ja|風宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaze-no-Miya|qq=}} are {{ill|Shinatsuhiko|ja|シナツヒコ|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinatsuhiko|qq=}} and {{ill|Shinatobe|ja|シナトベ|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinatobe|qq=}}, gods who govern wind and rain. Originally worshipped as deities bringing favorable weather for agriculture, after the Mongol invasions, they became objects of prayers for the salvation of Japan in times of national crisis.
== History == The origins of Kaze-no-Miya are unclear, but the earliest reference appears in the "Chōtoku Kenroku" from {{ill|997|ja|997th year|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/997|qq=}} (Chōtoku 3), citing it as "Kaze-sha," Ranked 4th among 32 rice field shrines under the jurisdiction of the Gekū, as noted in {{ill|Watarai Ieyuki|ja|度会家行|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Watarai Ieyuki|qq=}}'s "Ruiju Jingi Hongen." "Rice field shrines" correspond to the current末社 (massha), considered lower than sessha (auxiliary shrines).
Although initially Ranked as a末社, due to its deities’ importance in agriculture, the {{ill|Kazahi-no-Matsuri|ja|風日祈祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kazahi-no-Matsuri|qq=}} was held, and offerings of "kakechikara" (bundled rice stalks) were made during the {{ill|Kannamesai Festival|lt=Kannamesai Festival|lt=Kannamesai|ja|神嘗祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kannamesai|qq=}}.
During the {{ill|Mongol invasions of Japan|ja|元寇|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mongol invasions of Japan|qq=}} in {{ill|1281|ja|1281st year|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1281|qq=}} (Kōan 4), the court dispatched the Dainagon Tameuji to perform prayers at both the Naikū's Kaze-sha and this shrine. After the retreat of the Mongol forces, the victory was attributed to the "Divine Wind" ({{ill|Kamikaze|ja|神風|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamikaze|qq=}}), and in {{ill|1293|ja|1293rd year|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1293|qq=}} (Shōō 6), the shrine was elevated to a betsu-gū (detached shrine), named {{ill|Kazahi-no-Miya|ja|風日祈宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kazahi-no-Miya|qq=}} and Kaze-no-Miya respectively.
During the late {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Edo period|qq=}}, when Western nations visited Japan, the court held 15 days of prayers for the expulsion of foreigners at Kaze-no-Miya and Kazahi-no-Miya in {{ill|1863|ja|1863rd year|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1863|qq=}} (Bunkyū 3).
In {{ill|1876|ja|1876th year|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1876|qq=}} (Meiji 9), during the {{ill|Ise Riot|ja|伊勢暴動|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Riot|qq=}}, the sacred object from {{ill|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|1=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|2=ja|3=月夜見宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|ja|月夜見宮|1=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|2=ja|3=月夜見宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|qq=}} was temporarily moved to Kaze-no-Miya to protect it from a nearby fire.<ref>Kaneko (1983): p.214</ref>
== Festivals == The {{ill|Kazahi-no-Matsuri|ja|風日祈祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kazahi-no-Matsuri|qq=}}, praying for favorable weather and abundant harvests, is held annually on May 14 and August 4.
== Shrine Building == The shrine building of Kaze-no-Miya faces south and is constructed in the {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|ja|神明造|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinmei-zukuri|qq=}} style with a thatched roof, featuring {{ill|Chigi and Katsuogi|ja|千木・鰹木|lt=chigi and katsuogi|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chigi and Katsuogi|qq=}} with five odd-numbered katsuogi, following the Gekū’s design. The old shrine site for reconstruction (shikinen sengū) is adjacent to the east and west.
== Access == * Nearest Station: About a 12-minute walk from {{ill|Ise-shi Station|ja|伊勢市駅|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise-shi Station|qq=}} via {{ill|JR Sangu Line|ja|参宮線|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/JR Sangu Line|qq=}} and {{ill|Kintetsu|ja|近畿日本鉄道|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kintetsu|qq=}}. * Nearest Bus Stop: About a 5-minute walk from {{ill|Mie Kotsu|ja|三重交通|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mie Kotsu|qq=}} Gekū-mae Bus Stop. * Nearest Interchange: About 2 km from {{ill|Ise Nishi Interchange|ja|伊勢西インターチェンジ|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Nishi Interchange|qq=}} on the {{ill|Ise Expressway|ja|伊勢自動車道|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Expressway|qq=}}. * Parking: Free parking available.
== References == * Kaneko Nobuo. *Tamaki Town History, Volume II – History and Traditions of South Ise.* Mie Prefecture Local History Series No. 91, Mie Prefecture Local History Publication Association, December 10, 1983. * {{ill|Katsunoshin Sakurai|ja|桜井勝之進|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katsunoshin Sakurai|qq=}}. *Ise Grand Shrine.* Gakuseisha, May 20, 1969. * Katsunoshin Sakurai. *The Shrine of the Great deity of Ise.* Hori Shoten, March 25, 1973.
== Related Pages == {{commonscat|Kaze-no-miya (Geku)}} * {{ill|Shinatsuhiko|ja|シナツヒコ|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinatsuhiko|qq=}} * {{ill|Kamikaze|ja|神風|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamikaze|qq=}} * {{ill|Mongol invasions of Japan|ja|元寇|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mongol invasions of Japan|qq=}} * {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke Daijingu|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke Daijingu|qq=}} * {{ill|List of Shrines and Facilities of Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮を構成する神社と施設|1=List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|lt=List of 125 Shrines of Ise|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Shrines and Facilities of Ise Grand Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/List of 125 Shrines of Ise|qq=}}

== Festivals == The {{ill|Kazahi-no-Matsuri|ja|風日祈祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kazahi-no-Matsuri|qq=}}, praying for favorable weather and abundant harvests, is held annually on May 14 and August 4.
== Shrine Building == The shrine building of Kaze-no-Miya faces south and is constructed in the {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|ja|神明造|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinmei-zukuri|qq=}} style with a thatched roof, featuring {{ill|Chigi and Katsuogi|ja|千木・鰹木|lt=chigi and katsuogi|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chigi and Katsuogi|qq=}} with five odd-numbered katsuogi, following the Gekū’s design. The old shrine site for reconstruction (shikinen sengū) is adjacent to the east and west.
== Access == * Nearest Station: About a 12-minute walk from {{ill|Ise-shi Station|ja|伊勢市駅|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise-shi Station|qq=}} via {{ill|JR Sangu Line|ja|参宮線|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/JR Sangu Line|qq=}} and {{ill|Kintetsu|ja|近畿日本鉄道|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kintetsu|qq=}}. * Nearest Bus Stop: About a 5-minute walk from {{ill|Mie Kotsu|ja|三重交通|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mie Kotsu|qq=}} Gekū-mae Bus Stop. * Nearest Interchange: About 2 km from {{ill|Ise Nishi Interchange|ja|伊勢西インターチェンジ|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Nishi Interchange|qq=}} on the {{ill|Ise Expressway|ja|伊勢自動車道|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Expressway|qq=}}. * Parking: Free parking available.
== References == * Kaneko Nobuo. *Tamaki Town History, Volume II – History and Traditions of South Ise.* Mie Prefecture Local History Series No. 91, Mie Prefecture Local History Publication Association, December 10, 1983. * {{ill|Katsunoshin Sakurai|ja|桜井勝之進|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katsunoshin Sakurai|qq=}}. *Ise Grand Shrine.* Gakuseisha, May 20, 1969. * Katsunoshin Sakurai. *The Shrine of the Great deity of Ise.* Hori Shoten, March 25, 1973.

[[image:Ise-Shrine Geku-Kazenomiya.jpg|256px|Kazenomiya]] [[sn:Kaze-no-Miya]] [[ja:風宮]] [[en:Kaze-no-Miya]]
<!--Kaze-no-Miya-->

{{Infobox shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = | Name = Kazenomiya | location = Toyokawa-cho, [[Ise]], [[Mie Prefecture]], Japan | deity = [[Shinatsuhiko]] and [[Shinatobe]] | type = {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke Daijingu|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke Daijingu|qq=}} auxiliary shrine (*betsugū*) | Established = Before 997 CE | Main Hall = [[Shinmei-zukuri]] | coordinates = {{coord|34|29|9.1|N|136|42|14.2|E|region:JP|display=inline,title}} | festival = Kazahi-no-Matsuri (*Wind Day Prayer Festival*) }}
'''Kaze-no-Miya''' (風宮) is an auxiliary shrine (*betsugū*) located within the precincts of {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke Daijingu|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke Daijingu|qq=}} of the [[Ise Grand Shrine]] in [[Toyokawa-cho]], [[Ise]], [[Mie Prefecture]], Japan. == Overview== [[File:Kame-ish 01.JPG|left|thumb|180px|Kame-ishi (Turtle Stone)]] '''Kazenomiya''' is a {{ill|Betsugū (detached shrine)|ja|別宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Betsugū (detached shrine)|qq=}} of the {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke Daijingu|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke Daijingu|qq=}} (Gekū), located at the foot of **Hinokioyama** (檜尾山), south of the main shrine. The enshrined deities are {{ill|Shinatsuhiko|ja|級長津彦命|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinatsuhiko|qq=}} and {{ill|Shinatobe|ja|級長戸辺命|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinatobe|qq=}}, the same as those in the {{ill|Kazahinomi-no-Miya|ja|風日祈宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kazahinomi-no-Miya|qq=}}, a detached shrine of the {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|qq=}} (Naikū).
'''Kazenomiya''' is situated to the left after crossing the **Kame-ishi** (亀石), a stone along the approach to {{ill|Taka-no-Miya|lt=Taka-no-Miya|lt=Taga-no-Miya|ja|多賀宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taga-no-Miya|qq=}}, next to the pond in front of the main shrine. It is said that Kame-ishi was transported from the entrance of the **Ama-no-Iwato** (天岩戸) on {{ill|Mount Takakura (Ise)|ja|高倉山 (伊勢市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Takakura (Ise)|qq=}}.
The term *Betsugū* (別宮) means "separate shrine," considered second in reverence only to the main shrine. Apart from '''Kazenomiya''', the Gekū has other Betsugū: {{ill|Taka-no-Miya|ja|多賀宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taka-no-Miya|qq=}} and {{ill|Tsuchi-no-Miya|ja|土宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchi-no-Miya|qq=}} within the precincts, and {{ill|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|1=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|2=ja|3=月夜見宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|ja|月夜見宮|1=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|2=ja|3=月夜見宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|8=ja|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|1=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|2=ja|3=月夜見宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|qq=}} outside the precincts. Kazamiya was the last to be designated as a Betsugū in {{ill|1293|ja|1293年|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1293|qq=}} (Shōō 6) and is Ranked fourth among the four Betsugū.
Originally, it was known as **Kazeya-shiro** (風社) or **Kazejinja** (風神社), a shrine of lesser Rank. However, following the {{ill|Mongol invasions of Japan|ja|元寇|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mongol invasions of Japan|qq=}} in {{ill|1281|ja|1281st year|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1281|qq=}} (Kōan 4), when divine winds (*kamikaze*) protected Japan, it was elevated to the status of Betsugū.
== Enshrined Deities == The enshrined deities of {{ill|Kaze-no-Miya|ja|風宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaze-no-Miya|qq=}} are {{ill|Shinatsuhiko|ja|シナツヒコ|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinatsuhiko|qq=}} and {{ill|Shinatobe|ja|シナトベ|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinatobe|qq=}}, gods who govern wind and rain. Originally worshipped as deities bringing favorable weather for agriculture, after the Mongol invasions, they became objects of prayers for the salvation of Japan in times of national crisis.
== History == The origins of Kaze-no-Miya are unclear, but the earliest reference appears in the "Chōtoku Kenroku" from {{ill|997|ja|997年|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/997|qq=}} (Chōtoku 3), citing it as "Kaze-sha," Ranked 4th among 32 rice field shrines under the jurisdiction of the Gekū, as noted in {{ill|Watarai Ieyuki|ja|度会家行|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Watarai Ieyuki|qq=}}'s "Ruiju Jingi Hongen." "Rice field shrines" correspond to the current末社 (massha), considered lower than sessha (auxiliary shrines).
Although initially Ranked as a末社, due to its deities’ importance in agriculture, the {{ill|Kazahi-no-Matsuri|ja|風日祈祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kazahi-no-Matsuri|qq=}} was held, and offerings of "kakechikara" (bundled rice stalks) were made during the {{ill|Kannamesai Festival|lt=Kannamesai Festival|lt=Kannamesai|ja|神嘗祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kannamesai|qq=}}.
During the {{ill|Mongol invasions of Japan|ja|元寇|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mongol invasions of Japan|qq=}} in {{ill|1281|ja|1281st year|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1281|qq=}} (Kōan 4), the court dispatched the Dainagon Tameuji to perform prayers at both the Naikū's Kaze-sha and this shrine. After the retreat of the Mongol forces, the victory was attributed to the "Divine Wind" ({{ill|Kamikaze|ja|神風|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamikaze|qq=}}), and in {{ill|1293|ja|1293年|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1293|qq=}} (Shōō 6), the shrine was elevated to a betsu-gū (detached shrine), named {{ill|Kazahi-no-Miya|ja|風日祈宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kazahi-no-Miya|qq=}} and Kaze-no-Miya respectively.
During the late {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Edo period|qq=}}, when Western nations visited Japan, the court held 15 days of prayers for the expulsion of foreigners at Kaze-no-Miya and Kazahi-no-Miya in {{ill|1863|ja|1863年|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1863|qq=}} (Bunkyū 3).
In {{ill|1876|ja|1876年|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1876|qq=}} (Meiji 9), during the {{ill|Ise Riot|ja|伊勢暴動|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Riot|qq=}}, the sacred object from {{ill|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|1=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|2=ja|3=月夜見宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|ja|月夜見宮|1=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|2=ja|3=月夜見宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|qq=}} was temporarily moved to Kaze-no-Miya to protect it from a nearby fire.<ref>Kaneko (1983): p.214</ref>
== Festivals == The {{ill|Kazahi-no-Matsuri|ja|風日祈祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kazahi-no-Matsuri|qq=}}, praying for favorable weather and abundant harvests, is held annually on May 14 and August 4.
== Shrine Building == The shrine building of Kaze-no-Miya faces south and is constructed in the {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|ja|神明造|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinmei-zukuri|qq=}} style with a thatched roof, featuring {{ill|Chigi and Katsuogi|ja|千木・鰹木|lt=chigi and katsuogi|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chigi and Katsuogi|qq=}} with five odd-numbered katsuogi, following the Gekū’s design. The old shrine site for reconstruction (shikinen sengū) is adjacent to the east and west.
== Access == * Nearest Station: About a 12-minute walk from {{ill|Ise-shi Station|ja|伊勢市駅|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise-shi Station|qq=}} via {{ill|JR Sangu Line|ja|参宮線|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/JR Sangu Line|qq=}} and {{ill|Kintetsu|ja|近畿日本鉄道|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kintetsu|qq=}}. * Nearest Bus Stop: About a 5-minute walk from {{ill|Mie Kotsu|ja|三重交通|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mie Kotsu|qq=}} Gekū-mae Bus Stop. * Nearest Interchange: About 2 km from {{ill|Ise Nishi Interchange|ja|伊勢西インターチェンジ|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Nishi Interchange|qq=}} on the {{ill|Ise Expressway|ja|伊勢自動車道|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Expressway|qq=}}. * Parking: Free parking available.
== References == * Kaneko Nobuo. *Tamaki Town History, Volume II – History and Traditions of South Ise.* Mie Prefecture Local History Series No. 91, Mie Prefecture Local History Publication Association, December 10, 1983. * {{ill|Katsunoshin Sakurai|ja|桜井勝之進|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katsunoshin Sakurai|qq=}}. *Ise Grand Shrine.* Gakuseisha, May 20, 1969. * Katsunoshin Sakurai. *The Shrine of the Great deity of Ise.* Hori Shoten, March 25, 1973.

{{Infobox shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = | Name = Kazenomiya | location = Toyokawa-cho, [[Ise]], [[Mie Prefecture]], Japan | deity = [[Shinatsuhiko]] and [[Shinatobe]] | type = {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke Daijingu|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke Daijingu|qq=}} auxiliary shrine (*betsugū*) | Established = Before 997 CE | Main Hall = [[Shinmei-zukuri]] | coordinates = {{coord|34|29|9.1|N|136|42|14.2|E|region:JP|display=inline,title}} | festival = Kazahi-no-Matsuri (*Wind Day Prayer Festival*) }}

However, following the {{ill|Mongol invasions of Japan|ja|元寇|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mongol invasions of Japan|qq=}} in {{ill|1281|ja|1281年|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1281|qq=}} (Kōan 4), when divine winds (*kamikaze*) protected Japan, it was elevated to the status of Betsugū.
== Enshrined Deities == The enshrined deities of {{ill|Kaze-no-Miya|ja|風宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaze-no-Miya|qq=}} are {{ill|Shinatsuhiko|ja|シナツヒコ|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinatsuhiko|qq=}} and {{ill|Shinatobe|ja|シナトベ|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinatobe|qq=}}, gods who govern wind and rain. Originally worshipped as deities bringing favorable weather for agriculture, after the Mongol invasions, they became objects of prayers for the salvation of Japan in times of national crisis.
== History == The origins of Kaze-no-Miya are unclear, but the earliest reference appears in the "Chōtoku Kenroku" from {{ill|997|ja|997年|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/997|qq=}} (Chōtoku 3), citing it as "Kaze-sha," Ranked 4th among 32 rice field shrines under the jurisdiction of the Gekū, as noted in {{ill|Watarai Ieyuki|ja|度会家行|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Watarai Ieyuki|qq=}}'s "Ruiju Jingi Hongen." "Rice field shrines" correspond to the current末社 (massha), considered lower than sessha (auxiliary shrines).
Although initially Ranked as a末社, due to its deities’ importance in agriculture, the {{ill|Kazahi-no-Matsuri|ja|風日祈祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kazahi-no-Matsuri|qq=}} was held, and offerings of "kakechikara" (bundled rice stalks) were made during the {{ill|Kannamesai Festival|lt=Kannamesai Festival|lt=Kannamesai|ja|神嘗祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kannamesai|qq=}}.
During the {{ill|Mongol invasions of Japan|ja|元寇|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mongol invasions of Japan|qq=}} in {{ill|1281|ja|1281年|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1281|qq=}} (Kōan 4), the court dispatched the Dainagon Tameuji to perform prayers at both the Naikū's Kaze-sha and this shrine. After the retreat of the Mongol forces, the victory was attributed to the "Divine Wind" ({{ill|Kamikaze|ja|神風|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamikaze|qq=}}), and in {{ill|1293|ja|1293年|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1293|qq=}} (Shōō 6), the shrine was elevated to a betsu-gū (detached shrine), named {{ill|Kazahi-no-Miya|ja|風日祈宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kazahi-no-Miya|qq=}} and Kaze-no-Miya respectively.
During the late {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Edo period|qq=}}, when Western nations visited Japan, the court held 15 days of prayers for the expulsion of foreigners at Kaze-no-Miya and Kazahi-no-Miya in {{ill|1863|ja|1863年|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1863|qq=}} (Bunkyū 3).
In {{ill|1876|ja|1876年|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1876|qq=}} (Meiji 9), during the {{ill|Ise Riot|ja|伊勢暴動|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Riot|qq=}}, the sacred object from {{ill|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|1=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|2=ja|3=月夜見宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|ja|月夜見宮|1=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|2=ja|3=月夜見宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|qq=}} was temporarily moved to Kaze-no-Miya to protect it from a nearby fire.<ref>Kaneko (1983): p.214</ref>
== Festivals == The {{ill|Kazahi-no-Matsuri|ja|風日祈祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kazahi-no-Matsuri|qq=}}, praying for favorable weather and abundant harvests, is held annually on May 14 and August 4.
== Shrine Building == The shrine building of Kaze-no-Miya faces south and is constructed in the {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|ja|神明造|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinmei-zukuri|qq=}} style with a thatched roof, featuring {{ill|Chigi and Katsuogi|ja|千木・鰹木|lt=chigi and katsuogi|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chigi and Katsuogi|qq=}} with five odd-numbered katsuogi, following the Gekū’s design. The old shrine site for reconstruction (shikinen sengū) is adjacent to the east and west.
== Access == * Nearest Station: About a 12-minute walk from {{ill|Ise-shi Station|ja|伊勢市駅|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise-shi Station|qq=}} via {{ill|JR Sangu Line|ja|参宮線|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/JR Sangu Line|qq=}} and {{ill|Kintetsu|ja|近畿日本鉄道|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kintetsu|qq=}}. * Nearest Bus Stop: About a 5-minute walk from {{ill|Mie Kotsu|ja|三重交通|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mie Kotsu|qq=}} Gekū-mae Bus Stop. * Nearest Interchange: About 2 km from {{ill|Ise Nishi Interchange|ja|伊勢西インターチェンジ|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Nishi Interchange|qq=}} on the {{ill|Ise Expressway|ja|伊勢自動車道|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Expressway|qq=}}. * Parking: Free parking available.
== References == * Kaneko Nobuo. *Tamaki Town History, Volume II – History and Traditions of South Ise.* Mie Prefecture Local History Series No. 91, Mie Prefecture Local History Publication Association, December 10, 1983. * {{ill|Katsunoshin Sakurai|ja|桜井勝之進|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katsunoshin Sakurai|qq=}}. *Ise Grand Shrine.* Gakuseisha, May 20, 1969. * Katsunoshin Sakurai. *The Shrine of the Great deity of Ise.* Hori Shoten, March 25, 1973.
== Related Pages == {{commonscat|Kaze-no-miya (Geku)}} * {{ill|Shinatsuhiko|ja|シナツヒコ|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinatsuhiko|qq=}} * {{ill|Kamikaze|ja|神風|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamikaze|qq=}} * {{ill|Mongol invasions of Japan|ja|元寇|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mongol invasions of Japan|qq=}} * {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke Daijingu|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke Daijingu|qq=}} * {{ill|List of Shrines and Facilities of Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮を構成する神社と施設|1=List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|lt=List of 125 Shrines of Ise|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Shrines and Facilities of Ise Grand Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/List of 125 Shrines of Ise|qq=}}
== External Links == * [http://www.isejingu.or.jp/gegu/gegu5.htm Ise Jingu Official Site]

revision.text:
{{Infobox shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = | Name = Kazenomiya | location = Toyokawa-cho, [[Ise]], [[Mie Prefecture]], Japan | deity = [[Shinatsuhiko]] and [[Shinatobe]] | type = {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke Daijingu|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke Daijingu|qq=}} auxiliary shrine (*betsugū*) | Established = Before 997 CE | Main Hall = [[Shinmei-zukuri]] | coordinates = {{coord|34|29|9.1|N|136|42|14.2|E|region:JP|display=inline,title}} | festival = Kazahi-no-Matsuri (*Wind Day Prayer Festival*) }}
'''Kaze-no-Miya''' (風宮) is an auxiliary shrine (*betsugū*) located within the precincts of {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke Daijingu|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke Daijingu|qq=}} of the [[Ise Grand Shrine]] in [[Toyokawa-cho]], [[Ise]], [[Mie Prefecture]], Japan. == Overview== [[File:Kame-ish 01.JPG|left|thumb|180px|Kame-ishi (Turtle Stone)]] '''Kazenomiya''' is a {{ill|Betsugū (detached shrine)|ja|別宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Betsugū (detached shrine)|qq=}} of the {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke Daijingu|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke Daijingu|qq=}} (Gekū), located at the foot of **Hinokioyama** (檜尾山), south of the main shrine. The enshrined deities are {{ill|Shinatsuhiko|ja|級長津彦命|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinatsuhiko|qq=}} and {{ill|Shinatobe|ja|級長戸辺命|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinatobe|qq=}}, the same as those in the {{ill|Kazahinomi-no-Miya|ja|風日祈宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kazahinomi-no-Miya|qq=}}, a detached shrine of the {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|qq=}} (Naikū).
'''Kazenomiya''' is situated to the left after crossing the **Kame-ishi** (亀石), a stone along the approach to {{ill|Taka-no-Miya|lt=Taka-no-Miya|lt=Taga-no-Miya|ja|多賀宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taga-no-Miya|qq=}}, next to the pond in front of the main shrine. It is said that Kame-ishi was transported from the entrance of the **Ama-no-Iwato** (天岩戸) on {{ill|Mount Takakura (Ise)|ja|高倉山 (伊勢市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Takakura (Ise)|qq=}}.
The term *Betsugū* (別宮) means "separate shrine," considered second in reverence only to the main shrine. Apart from '''Kazenomiya''', the Gekū has other Betsugū: {{ill|Taka-no-Miya|ja|多賀宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taka-no-Miya|qq=}} and {{ill|Tsuchi-no-Miya|ja|土宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchi-no-Miya|qq=}} within the precincts, and {{ill|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|1=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|2=ja|3=月夜見宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|ja|月夜見宮|1=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|2=ja|3=月夜見宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|8=ja|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|1=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|2=ja|3=月夜見宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|qq=}} outside the precincts. Kazamiya was the last to be designated as a Betsugū in [[:en:1293|1293]] (Shōō 6) and is Ranked fourth among the four Betsugū.
Originally, it was known as **Kazeya-shiro** (風社) or **Kazejinja** (風神社), a shrine of lesser Rank. However, following the [[:en:Mongol invasions of Japan|Mongol invasions of Japan]] in [[:en:1281|1281]] (Kōan 4), when divine winds (*kamikaze*) protected Japan, it was elevated to the status of Betsugū.
== Enshrined Deities == The enshrined deities of {{ill|Kaze-no-Miya|ja|風宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaze-no-Miya|qq=}} are [[:en:Shinatsuhiko|Shinatsuhiko]] and {{ill|Shinatobe|ja|シナトベ|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinatobe|qq=}}, gods who govern wind and rain. Originally worshipped as deities bringing favorable weather for agriculture, after the Mongol invasions, they became objects of prayers for the salvation of Japan in times of national crisis.
== History == The origins of Kaze-no-Miya are unclear, but the earliest reference appears in the "Chōtoku Kenroku" from [[:en:997|997]] (Chōtoku 3), citing it as "Kaze-sha," Ranked 4th among 32 rice field shrines under the jurisdiction of the Gekū, as noted in {{ill|Watarai Ieyuki|ja|度会家行|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Watarai Ieyuki|qq=}}'s "Ruiju Jingi Hongen." "Rice field shrines" correspond to the current末社 (massha), considered lower than sessha (auxiliary shrines).
Although initially Ranked as a末社, due to its deities’ importance in agriculture, the {{ill|Kazahi-no-Matsuri|ja|風日祈祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kazahi-no-Matsuri|qq=}} was held, and offerings of "kakechikara" (bundled rice stalks) were made during the [[:en:Kannamesai Festival|Kannamesai]].
During the [[:en:Mongol invasions of Japan|Mongol invasions of Japan]] in [[:en:1281|1281]] (Kōan 4), the court dispatched the Dainagon Tameuji to perform prayers at both the Naikū's Kaze-sha and this shrine. After the retreat of the Mongol forces, the victory was attributed to the "Divine Wind" ([[:en:Kamikaze (typhoon)|Kamikaze]]), and in [[:en:1293|1293]] (Shōō 6), the shrine was elevated to a betsu-gū (detached shrine), named {{ill|Kazahi-no-Miya|ja|風日祈宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kazahi-no-Miya|qq=}} and Kaze-no-Miya respectively.
During the late [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], when Western nations visited Japan, the court held 15 days of prayers for the expulsion of foreigners at Kaze-no-Miya and Kazahi-no-Miya in [[:en:1863|1863]] (Bunkyū 3).
In [[:en:1876|1876]] (Meiji 9), during the {{ill|Ise Riot|ja|伊勢暴動|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Riot|qq=}}, the sacred object from {{ill|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|1=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|2=ja|3=月夜見宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|ja|月夜見宮|1=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|2=ja|3=月夜見宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|qq=}} was temporarily moved to Kaze-no-Miya to protect it from a nearby fire.<ref>Kaneko (1983): p.214</ref>
== Festivals == The {{ill|Kazahi-no-Matsuri|ja|風日祈祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kazahi-no-Matsuri|qq=}}, praying for favorable weather and abundant harvests, is held annually on May 14 and August 4.
== Shrine Building == The shrine building of Kaze-no-Miya faces south and is constructed in the [[:en:Shinmei-zukuri|Shinmei-zukuri]] style with a thatched roof, featuring {{ill|Chigi and Katsuogi|ja|千木・鰹木|lt=chigi and katsuogi|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chigi and Katsuogi|qq=}} with five odd-numbered katsuogi, following the Gekū’s design. The old shrine site for reconstruction (shikinen sengū) is adjacent to the east and west.
== Access == * Nearest Station: About a 12-minute walk from [[:en:Iseshi Station|Ise-shi Station]] via [[:en:Sangū Line|JR Sangu Line]] and [[:en:Kintetsu Railway|Kintetsu]]. * Nearest Bus Stop: About a 5-minute walk from [[:en:Mie Kotsu|Mie Kotsu]] Gekū-mae Bus Stop. * Nearest Interchange: About 2 km from {{ill|Ise Nishi Interchange|ja|伊勢西インターチェンジ|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Nishi Interchange|qq=}} on the [[:en:Ise Expressway|Ise Expressway]]. * Parking: Free parking available.
== References == * Kaneko Nobuo. *Tamaki Town History, Volume II – History and Traditions of South Ise.* Mie Prefecture Local History Series No. 91, Mie Prefecture Local History Publication Association, December 10, 1983. * {{ill|Katsunoshin Sakurai|ja|桜井勝之進|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katsunoshin Sakurai|qq=}}. *Ise Grand Shrine.* Gakuseisha, May 20, 1969. * Katsunoshin Sakurai. *The Shrine of the Great deity of Ise.* Hori Shoten, March 25, 1973.
== Related Pages == {{commonscat|Kaze-no-miya (Geku)}} * [[:en:Shinatsuhiko|Shinatsuhiko]] * [[:en:Kamikaze (typhoon)|Kamikaze]] * [[:en:Mongol invasions of Japan|Mongol invasions of Japan]] * {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke Daijingu|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke Daijingu|qq=}} * {{ill|List of Shrines and Facilities of Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮を構成する神社と施設|1=List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|lt=List of 125 Shrines of Ise|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Shrines and Facilities of Ise Grand Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/List of 125 Shrines of Ise|qq=}}

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11665463]] Wikidata name: Kaze-no-miya [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:28, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11665463]] Wikidata name: Kaze-no-miya [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:08, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image =File:Ise-Shrine Geku-Kazenomiya.jpg | Name = Kazenomiya | location = Toyokawa-cho, [[Ise]], [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]], Japan | deity = {{ill|Shinatsuhiko|qid=Q1073925}} | type = {{ill|Toyōke Daijingū|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyōke Daijingū|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} auxiliary shrine (*betsugū*) | Established = Before 997 CE | Main Hall = [[Shinmei-zukuri]] | coordinates = {{Coord|34.48586111111111|N|136.70394444444443|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | festival = Kazahi-no-Matsuri (*Wind Day Prayer Festival*) | location = {{ill|Toyokawachō|qid=Q17223204}} | architecture_style = {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|qid=Q930545}} }}
'''Kaze-no-Miya''' (風宮) is an auxiliary shrine (*betsugū*) located within the precincts of {{ill|Toyōke Daijingū|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyōke Daijingū|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} of the [[Ise Grand Shrine]] in [[Toyokawa-cho]], [[Ise]], [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]], Japan. == Overview== [[File:Kame-ish 01.JPG|left|thumb|180px|Kame-ishi (Turtle Stone)]] '''Kazenomiya''' is a {{ill|Betsugū (detached shrine)|ja|別宮|lt=Betsugū|1=Setsumatsusha|2=ja|3=摂末社|4=zh|5=攝末社|6=es|7=Setsumatsusha|8=fr|9=Setsumatsusha|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Betsugū (detached shrine)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} of the {{ill|Toyōke Daijingū|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyōke Daijingū|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (Gekū), located at the foot of **Hinokioyama** (檜尾山), south of the main shrine. The enshrined deities are {{ill|Shinatsuhiko|ja|級長津彦命|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinatsuhiko|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to シナツヒコ|1=Shinatsuhiko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1073925}} and {{ill|Shinatobe|ja|級長戸辺命|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinatobe|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to シナツヒコ|1=Shinatsuhiko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1073925}}, the same as those in the {{ill|Kazahinomi-no-Miya|ja|風日祈宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kazahinomi-no-Miya|qq=}}, a detached shrine of the {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (Naikū).
'''Kazenomiya''' is situated to the left after crossing the **Kame-ishi** (亀石), a stone along the approach to {{ill|Taka-no-Miya|lt=Taka-no-Miya|lt=Taga-no-Miya|ja|多賀宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taga-no-Miya|qq=}}, next to the pond in front of the main shrine. It is said that Kame-ishi was transported from the entrance of the **Ama-no-Iwato** (天岩戸) on {{ill|Mount Takakura (Ise)|ja|高倉山 (伊勢市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Takakura (Ise)|qq=|1=Mount Takakura|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11668768}}.
The term *Betsugū* (別宮) means "separate shrine," considered second in reverence only to the main shrine. Apart from '''Kazenomiya''', the Gekū has other Betsugū: {{ill|Taka-no-Miya|ja|多賀宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taka-no-Miya|qq=}} and {{ill|Tsuchi-no-Miya|ja|土宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchi-no-Miya|qq=}} within the precincts, and {{ill|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|1=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|2=ja|3=月夜見宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|ja|月夜見宮|1=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|2=ja|3=月夜見宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|8=ja|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|1=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|2=ja|3=月夜見宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|qq=}} outside the precincts. Kazamiya was the last to be designated as a Betsugū in [[:en:1293|1293]] (Shōō 6) and is Ranked fourth among the four Betsugū.
Originally, it was known as **Kazeya-shiro** (風社) or **Kazejinja** (風神社), a shrine of lesser Rank. However, following the [[:en:Mongol invasions of Japan|Mongol invasions of Japan]] in [[:en:1281|1281]] (Kōan 4), when divine winds (*kamikaze*) protected Japan, it was elevated to the status of Betsugū.
== Enshrined Deities == The enshrined deities of {{ill|Kaze-no-Miya|ja|風宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaze-no-Miya|qq=}} are [[:en:Shinatsuhiko|Shinatsuhiko]] and {{ill|Shinatobe|ja|シナトベ|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinatobe|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to シナツヒコ|1=Shinatsuhiko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1073925}}, gods who govern wind and rain. Originally worshipped as deities bringing favorable weather for agriculture, after the Mongol invasions, they became objects of prayers for the salvation of Japan in times of national crisis.
== History == The origins of Kaze-no-Miya are unclear, but the earliest reference appears in the "Chōtoku Kenroku" from [[:en:997|997]] (Chōtoku 3), citing it as "Kaze-sha," Ranked 4th among 32 rice field shrines under the jurisdiction of the Gekū, as noted in {{ill|Watarai Ieyuki|ja|度会家行|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Watarai Ieyuki|qq=|1=Watarai Ieyuki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11486121}}'s "Ruiju Jingi Hongen." "Rice field shrines" correspond to the current末社 (massha), considered lower than sessha (auxiliary shrines).
Although initially Ranked as a末社, due to its deities’ importance in agriculture, the {{ill|Kazahi-no-Matsuri|ja|風日祈祭|1=Kazahi-no-Misai|2=ja|3=風日祈祭|qid=Q135524493|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kazahi-no-Matsuri|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135524493}} was held, and offerings of "kakechikara" (bundled rice stalks) were made during the [[:en:Kannamesai Festival|Kannamesai]].
During the [[:en:Mongol invasions of Japan|Mongol invasions of Japan]] in [[:en:1281|1281]] (Kōan 4), the court dispatched the Dainagon Tameuji to perform prayers at both the Naikū's Kaze-sha and this shrine. After the retreat of the Mongol forces, the victory was attributed to the "Divine Wind" ([[:en:Kamikaze (typhoon)|Kamikaze]]), and in [[:en:1293|1293]] (Shōō 6), the shrine was elevated to a betsu-gū (detached shrine), named {{ill|Kazahi-no-Miya|ja|風日祈宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kazahi-no-Miya|qq=|1=Kazahi Prayer Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11665488}} and Kaze-no-Miya respectively.
During the late [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], when Western nations visited Japan, the court held 15 days of prayers for the expulsion of foreigners at Kaze-no-Miya and Kazahi-no-Miya in [[:en:1863|1863]] (Bunkyū 3).
In [[:en:1876|1876]] (Meiji 9), during the {{ill|Ise Riot|ja|伊勢暴動|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Riot|qq=|1=Ise Riot|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11379085}}, the sacred object from {{ill|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|1=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|2=ja|3=月夜見宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|ja|月夜見宮|1=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|2=ja|3=月夜見宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|qq=}} was temporarily moved to Kaze-no-Miya to protect it from a nearby fire.<ref>Kaneko (1983): p.214</ref>
== Festivals == The {{ill|Kazahi-no-Matsuri|ja|風日祈祭|1=Kazahi-no-Misai|2=ja|3=風日祈祭|qid=Q135524493|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kazahi-no-Matsuri|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135524493}}, praying for favorable weather and abundant harvests, is held annually on May 14 and August 4.
== Shrine Building == The shrine building of Kaze-no-Miya faces south and is constructed in the [[:en:Shinmei-zukuri|Shinmei-zukuri]] style with a thatched roof, featuring {{ill|Chigi and Katsuogi|ja|千木・鰹木|lt=chigi and katsuogi|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chigi and Katsuogi|qq=|1=chigi and katsuogi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q87220234}} with five odd-numbered katsuogi, following the Gekū’s design. The old shrine site for reconstruction (shikinen sengū) is adjacent to the east and west.
== Access == * Nearest Station: About a 12-minute walk from [[:en:Iseshi Station|Ise-shi Station]] via [[:en:Sangū Line|JR Sangu Line]] and [[:en:Kintetsu Railway|Kintetsu]]. * Nearest Bus Stop: About a 5-minute walk from [[:en:Mie Kotsu|Mie Kotsu]] Gekū-mae Bus Stop. * Nearest Interchange: About 2 km from {{ill|Ise Nishi Interchange|ja|伊勢西インターチェンジ|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Nishi Interchange|qq=|1=Ise-nishi itnerchange|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10884865}} on the [[:en:Ise Expressway|Ise Expressway]]. * Parking: Free parking available.
== References == * Kaneko Nobuo. *Tamaki Town History, Volume II – History and Traditions of South Ise.* Mie Prefecture Local History Series No. 91, Mie Prefecture Local History Publication Association, December 10, 1983. * {{ill|Katsunoshin Sakurai|ja|桜井勝之進|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katsunoshin Sakurai|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 櫻井勝之進|1=Katsunoshin Sakurai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11544758}}. *Ise Grand Shrine.* Gakuseisha, May 20, 1969. * Katsunoshin Sakurai. *The Shrine of the Great deity of Ise.* Hori Shoten, March 25, 1973.
== Related Pages == {{commonscat|Kaze-no-miya (Geku)}} * [[:en:Shinatsuhiko|Shinatsuhiko]] * [[:en:Kamikaze (typhoon)|Kamikaze]] * [[:en:Mongol invasions of Japan|Mongol invasions of Japan]] * {{ill|Toyōke Daijingū|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyōke Daijingū|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} * {{ill|List of Shrines and Facilities of Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮を構成する神社と施設|1=List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|lt=List of 125 Shrines of Ise|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Shrines and Facilities of Ise Grand Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/List of 125 Shrines of Ise|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 伊勢神宮}}

'''Kaze-no-Miya''' (風宮) is an auxiliary shrine (*betsugū*) located within the precincts of {{ill|Toyōke Daijingū|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyōke Daijingū|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} of the [[Ise Grand Shrine]] in [[Toyokawa-cho]], [[Ise]], [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]], Japan. == Overview== [[File:Kame-ish 01.JPG|left|thumb|180px|Kame-ishi (Turtle Stone)]] '''Kazenomiya''' is a {{ill|Betsugū (detached shrine)|ja|別宮|lt=Betsugū|1=Setsumatsusha|2=ja|3=摂末社|4=zh|5=攝末社|6=es|7=Setsumatsusha|8=fr|9=Setsumatsusha|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Betsugū (detached shrine)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} of the {{ill|Toyōke Daijingū|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyōke Daijingū|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (Gekū), located at the foot of **Hinokioyama** (檜尾山), south of the main shrine. The enshrined deities are {{ill|Shinatsuhiko|ja|級長津彦命|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinatsuhiko|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to シナツヒコ|1=Shinatsuhiko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1073925}} and {{ill|Shinatobe|ja|級長戸辺命|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinatobe|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to シナツヒコ|1=Shinatsuhiko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1073925}}, the same as those in the {{ill|Kazahinomi-no-Miya|ja|風日祈宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kazahinomi-no-Miya|qq=}}, a detached shrine of the {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (Naikū).
'''Kazenomiya''' is situated to the left after crossing the **Kame-ishi** (亀石), a stone along the approach to {{ill|Taka-no-Miya|lt=Taka-no-Miya|lt=Taga-no-Miya|ja|多賀宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taga-no-Miya|qq=}}, next to the pond in front of the main shrine. It is said that Kame-ishi was transported from the entrance of the **Ama-no-Iwato** (天岩戸) on {{ill|Mount Takakura (Ise)|ja|高倉山 (伊勢市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Takakura (Ise)|qq=|1=Mount Takakura|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11668768}}.
The term *Betsugū* (別宮) means "separate shrine," considered second in reverence only to the main shrine. Apart from '''Kazenomiya''', the Gekū has other Betsugū: {{ill|Taka-no-Miya|ja|多賀宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taka-no-Miya|qq=}} and {{ill|Tsuchi-no-Miya|ja|土宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchi-no-Miya|qq=}} within the precincts, and {{ill|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|1=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|2=ja|3=月夜見宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|ja|月夜見宮|1=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|2=ja|3=月夜見宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|8=ja|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|1=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|2=ja|3=月夜見宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|qq=}} outside the precincts. Kazamiya was the last to be designated as a Betsugū in [[:en:1293|1293]] (Shōō 6) and is Ranked fourth among the four Betsugū.
Originally, it was known as **Kazeya-shiro** (風社) or **Kazejinja** (風神社), a shrine of lesser Rank. However, following the [[:en:Mongol invasions of Japan|Mongol invasions of Japan]] in [[:en:1281|1281]] (Kōan 4), when divine winds (*kamikaze*) protected Japan, it was elevated to the status of Betsugū.
== Enshrined Deities == The enshrined deities of {{ill|Kaze-no-Miya|ja|風宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaze-no-Miya|qq=}} are [[:en:Shinatsuhiko|Shinatsuhiko]] and {{ill|Shinatobe|ja|シナトベ|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinatobe|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to シナツヒコ|1=Shinatsuhiko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1073925}}, gods who govern wind and rain. Originally worshipped as deities bringing favorable weather for agriculture, after the Mongol invasions, they became objects of prayers for the salvation of Japan in times of national crisis.
== History == The origins of Kaze-no-Miya are unclear, but the earliest reference appears in the "Chōtoku Kenroku" from [[:en:997|997]] (Chōtoku 3), citing it as "Kaze-sha," Ranked 4th among 32 rice field shrines under the jurisdiction of the Gekū, as noted in {{ill|Watarai Ieyuki|ja|度会家行|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Watarai Ieyuki|qq=|1=Watarai Ieyuki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11486121}}'s "Ruiju Jingi Hongen." "Rice field shrines" correspond to the current末社 (massha), considered lower than sessha (auxiliary shrines).
Although initially Ranked as a末社, due to its deities’ importance in agriculture, the {{ill|Kazahi-no-Matsuri|ja|風日祈祭|1=Kazahi-no-Misai|2=ja|3=風日祈祭|qid=Q135524493|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kazahi-no-Matsuri|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135524493}} was held, and offerings of "kakechikara" (bundled rice stalks) were made during the [[:en:Kannamesai Festival|Kannamesai]].
During the [[:en:Mongol invasions of Japan|Mongol invasions of Japan]] in [[:en:1281|1281]] (Kōan 4), the court dispatched the Dainagon Tameuji to perform prayers at both the Naikū's Kaze-sha and this shrine. After the retreat of the Mongol forces, the victory was attributed to the "Divine Wind" ([[:en:Kamikaze (typhoon)|Kamikaze]]), and in [[:en:1293|1293]] (Shōō 6), the shrine was elevated to a betsu-gū (detached shrine), named {{ill|Kazahi-no-Miya|ja|風日祈宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kazahi-no-Miya|qq=|1=Kazahi Prayer Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11665488}} and Kaze-no-Miya respectively.
During the late [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], when Western nations visited Japan, the court held 15 days of prayers for the expulsion of foreigners at Kaze-no-Miya and Kazahi-no-Miya in [[:en:1863|1863]] (Bunkyū 3).
In [[:en:1876|1876]] (Meiji 9), during the {{ill|Ise Riot|ja|伊勢暴動|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Riot|qq=|1=Ise Riot|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11379085}}, the sacred object from {{ill|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|1=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|2=ja|3=月夜見宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|ja|月夜見宮|1=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|2=ja|3=月夜見宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|qq=}} was temporarily moved to Kaze-no-Miya to protect it from a nearby fire.<ref>Kaneko (1983): p.214</ref>
== Festivals == The {{ill|Kazahi-no-Matsuri|ja|風日祈祭|1=Kazahi-no-Misai|2=ja|3=風日祈祭|qid=Q135524493|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kazahi-no-Matsuri|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135524493}}, praying for favorable weather and abundant harvests, is held annually on May 14 and August 4.
== Shrine Building == The shrine building of Kaze-no-Miya faces south and is constructed in the [[:en:Shinmei-zukuri|Shinmei-zukuri]] style with a thatched roof, featuring {{ill|Chigi and Katsuogi|ja|千木・鰹木|lt=chigi and katsuogi|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chigi and Katsuogi|qq=|1=chigi and katsuogi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q87220234}} with five odd-numbered katsuogi, following the Gekū’s design. The old shrine site for reconstruction (shikinen sengū) is adjacent to the east and west.
== Access == * Nearest Station: About a 12-minute walk from [[:en:Iseshi Station|Ise-shi Station]] via [[:en:Sangū Line|JR Sangu Line]] and [[:en:Kintetsu Railway|Kintetsu]]. * Nearest Bus Stop: About a 5-minute walk from [[:en:Mie Kotsu|Mie Kotsu]] Gekū-mae Bus Stop. * Nearest Interchange: About 2 km from {{ill|Ise Nishi Interchange|ja|伊勢西インターチェンジ|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Nishi Interchange|qq=|1=Ise-nishi itnerchange|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10884865}} on the [[:en:Ise Expressway|Ise Expressway]]. * Parking: Free parking available.
== References == * Kaneko Nobuo. *Tamaki Town History, Volume II – History and Traditions of South Ise.* Mie Prefecture Local History Series No. 91, Mie Prefecture Local History Publication Association, December 10, 1983. * {{ill|Katsunoshin Sakurai|ja|桜井勝之進|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katsunoshin Sakurai|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 櫻井勝之進|1=Katsunoshin Sakurai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11544758}}. *Ise Grand Shrine.* Gakuseisha, May 20, 1969. * Katsunoshin Sakurai. *The Shrine of the Great deity of Ise.* Hori Shoten, March 25, 1973.
== Related Pages == {{commonscat|Kaze-no-miya (Geku)}} * [[:en:Shinatsuhiko|Shinatsuhiko]] * [[:en:Kamikaze (typhoon)|Kamikaze]] * [[:en:Mongol invasions of Japan|Mongol invasions of Japan]] * {{ill|Toyōke Daijingū|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyōke Daijingū|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} * {{ill|List of Shrines and Facilities of Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮を構成する神社と施設|1=List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|lt=List of 125 Shrines of Ise|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Shrines and Facilities of Ise Grand Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/List of 125 Shrines of Ise|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 伊勢神宮}}

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11665463]] Wikidata name: Kaze-no-miya [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:28, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11665463]] Wikidata name: Kaze-no-miya [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:08, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image =File:Ise-Shrine Geku-Kazenomiya.jpg | Name = Kazenomiya | location = Toyokawa-cho, [[Ise]], [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]], Japan | deity = {{ill|Shinatsuhiko|qid=Q1073925}} | type = {{ill|Toyōke Daijingū|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyōke Daijingū|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} auxiliary shrine (*betsugū*) | Established = Before 997 CE | Main Hall = [[Shinmei-zukuri]] | coordinates = {{Coord|34.48586111111111|N|136.70394444444443|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | festival = Kazahi-no-Matsuri (*Wind Day Prayer Festival*) | location = {{ill|Toyokawachō|qid=Q17223204}} | architecture_style = {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|qid=Q930545}} }}
'''Kaze-no-Miya''' (風宮) is an auxiliary shrine (*betsugū*) located within the precincts of {{ill|Toyōke Daijingū|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyōke Daijingū|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} of the [[Ise Grand Shrine]] in [[Toyokawa-cho]], [[Ise]], [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]], Japan. == Overview== [[File:Kame-ish 01.JPG|left|thumb|180px|Kame-ishi (Turtle Stone)]] '''Kazenomiya''' is a {{ill|Betsugū (detached shrine)|ja|別宮|lt=Betsugū|1=Setsumatsusha|2=ja|3=摂末社|4=zh|5=攝末社|6=es|7=Setsumatsusha|8=fr|9=Setsumatsusha|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Betsugū (detached shrine)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} of the {{ill|Toyōke Daijingū|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyōke Daijingū|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (Gekū), located at the foot of **Hinokioyama** (檜尾山), south of the main shrine. The enshrined deities are {{ill|Shinatsuhiko|ja|級長津彦命|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinatsuhiko|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to シナツヒコ|1=Shinatsuhiko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1073925}} and {{ill|Shinatobe|ja|級長戸辺命|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinatobe|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to シナツヒコ|1=Shinatsuhiko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1073925}}, the same as those in the {{ill|Kazahinomi-no-Miya|ja|風日祈宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kazahinomi-no-Miya|qq=}}, a detached shrine of the {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (Naikū).
'''Kazenomiya''' is situated to the left after crossing the **Kame-ishi** (亀石), a stone along the approach to {{ill|Taka-no-Miya|lt=Taka-no-Miya|lt=Taga-no-Miya|ja|多賀宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taga-no-Miya|qq=}}, next to the pond in front of the main shrine. It is said that Kame-ishi was transported from the entrance of the **Ama-no-Iwato** (天岩戸) on {{ill|Mount Takakura (Ise)|ja|高倉山 (伊勢市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Takakura (Ise)|qq=|1=Mount Takakura|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11668768}}.
The term *Betsugū* (別宮) means "separate shrine," considered second in reverence only to the main shrine. Apart from '''Kazenomiya''', the Gekū has other Betsugū: {{ill|Taka-no-Miya|ja|多賀宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taka-no-Miya|qq=}} and {{ill|Tsuchi-no-Miya|ja|土宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchi-no-Miya|qq=}} within the precincts, and {{ill|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|1=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|2=ja|3=月夜見宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|ja|月夜見宮|1=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|2=ja|3=月夜見宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|8=ja|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|1=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|2=ja|3=月夜見宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|qq=}} outside the precincts. Kazamiya was the last to be designated as a Betsugū in [[:en:1293|1293]] (Shōō 6) and is Ranked fourth among the four Betsugū.
Originally, it was known as **Kazeya-shiro** (風社) or **Kazejinja** (風神社), a shrine of lesser Rank. However, following the [[:en:Mongol invasions of Japan|Mongol invasions of Japan]] in [[:en:1281|1281]] (Kōan 4), when divine winds (*kamikaze*) protected Japan, it was elevated to the status of Betsugū.
== Enshrined Deities == The enshrined deities of {{ill|Kaze-no-Miya|ja|風宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaze-no-Miya|qq=}} are [[:en:Shinatsuhiko|Shinatsuhiko]] and {{ill|Shinatobe|ja|シナトベ|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinatobe|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to シナツヒコ|1=Shinatsuhiko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1073925}}, gods who govern wind and rain. Originally worshipped as deities bringing favorable weather for agriculture, after the Mongol invasions, they became objects of prayers for the salvation of Japan in times of national crisis.
== History == The origins of Kaze-no-Miya are unclear, but the earliest reference appears in the "Chōtoku Kenroku" from [[:en:997|997]] (Chōtoku 3), citing it as "Kaze-sha," Ranked 4th among 32 rice field shrines under the jurisdiction of the Gekū, as noted in {{ill|Watarai Ieyuki|ja|度会家行|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Watarai Ieyuki|qq=|1=Watarai Ieyuki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11486121}}'s "Ruiju Jingi Hongen." "Rice field shrines" correspond to the current末社 (massha), considered lower than sessha (auxiliary shrines).
Although initially Ranked as a末社, due to its deities’ importance in agriculture, the {{ill|Kazahi-no-Matsuri|ja|風日祈祭|1=Kazahi-no-Misai|2=ja|3=風日祈祭|qid=Q135524493|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kazahi-no-Matsuri|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135524493}} was held, and offerings of "kakechikara" (bundled rice stalks) were made during the [[:en:Kannamesai Festival|Kannamesai]].
During the [[:en:Mongol invasions of Japan|Mongol invasions of Japan]] in [[:en:1281|1281]] (Kōan 4), the court dispatched the Dainagon Tameuji to perform prayers at both the Naikū's Kaze-sha and this shrine. After the retreat of the Mongol forces, the victory was attributed to the "Divine Wind" ([[:en:Kamikaze (typhoon)|Kamikaze]]), and in [[:en:1293|1293]] (Shōō 6), the shrine was elevated to a betsu-gū (detached shrine), named {{ill|Kazahi-no-Miya|ja|風日祈宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kazahi-no-Miya|qq=|1=Kazahi Prayer Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11665488}} and Kaze-no-Miya respectively.
During the late [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], when Western nations visited Japan, the court held 15 days of prayers for the expulsion of foreigners at Kaze-no-Miya and Kazahi-no-Miya in [[:en:1863|1863]] (Bunkyū 3).
In [[:en:1876|1876]] (Meiji 9), during the {{ill|Ise Riot|ja|伊勢暴動|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Riot|qq=|1=Ise Riot|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11379085}}, the sacred object from {{ill|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|1=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|2=ja|3=月夜見宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|ja|月夜見宮|1=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|2=ja|3=月夜見宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|qq=}} was temporarily moved to Kaze-no-Miya to protect it from a nearby fire.<ref>Kaneko (1983): p.214</ref>
== Festivals == The {{ill|Kazahi-no-Matsuri|ja|風日祈祭|1=Kazahi-no-Misai|2=ja|3=風日祈祭|qid=Q135524493|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kazahi-no-Matsuri|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135524493}}, praying for favorable weather and abundant harvests, is held annually on May 14 and August 4.
== Shrine Building == The shrine building of Kaze-no-Miya faces south and is constructed in the [[:en:Shinmei-zukuri|Shinmei-zukuri]] style with a thatched roof, featuring {{ill|Chigi and Katsuogi|ja|千木・鰹木|lt=chigi and katsuogi|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chigi and Katsuogi|qq=|1=chigi and katsuogi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q87220234}} with five odd-numbered katsuogi, following the Gekū’s design. The old shrine site for reconstruction (shikinen sengū) is adjacent to the east and west.
== Access == * Nearest Station: About a 12-minute walk from [[:en:Iseshi Station|Ise-shi Station]] via [[:en:Sangū Line|JR Sangu Line]] and [[:en:Kintetsu Railway|Kintetsu]]. * Nearest Bus Stop: About a 5-minute walk from [[:en:Mie Kotsu|Mie Kotsu]] Gekū-mae Bus Stop. * Nearest Interchange: About 2 km from {{ill|Ise Nishi Interchange|ja|伊勢西インターチェンジ|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Nishi Interchange|qq=|1=Ise-nishi itnerchange|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10884865}} on the [[:en:Ise Expressway|Ise Expressway]]. * Parking: Free parking available.
== References == * Kaneko Nobuo. *Tamaki Town History, Volume II – History and Traditions of South Ise.* Mie Prefecture Local History Series No. 91, Mie Prefecture Local History Publication Association, December 10, 1983. * {{ill|Katsunoshin Sakurai|ja|桜井勝之進|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katsunoshin Sakurai|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 櫻井勝之進|1=Katsunoshin Sakurai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11544758}}. *Ise Grand Shrine.* Gakuseisha, May 20, 1969. * Katsunoshin Sakurai. *The Shrine of the Great deity of Ise.* Hori Shoten, March 25, 1973.
== Related Pages == {{commonscat|Kaze-no-miya (Geku)}} * [[:en:Shinatsuhiko|Shinatsuhiko]] * [[:en:Kamikaze (typhoon)|Kamikaze]] * [[:en:Mongol invasions of Japan|Mongol invasions of Japan]] * {{ill|Toyōke Daijingū|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyōke Daijingū|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} * {{ill|List of Shrines and Facilities of Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮を構成する神社と施設|1=List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|lt=List of 125 Shrines of Ise|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Shrines and Facilities of Ise Grand Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/List of 125 Shrines of Ise|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 伊勢神宮}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Kaze-no-miya (Geku)]][[ja:風宮]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11665463]] Wikidata name: Kaze-no-miya [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:28, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11665463]] Wikidata name: Kaze-no-miya [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:08, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image =File:Ise-Shrine Geku-Kazenomiya.jpg | Name = Kazenomiya | location = Toyokawa-cho, [[Ise]], [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]], Japan | deity = {{ill|Shinatsuhiko|qid=Q1073925}} | type = {{ill|Toyōke Daijingū|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyōke Daijingū|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} auxiliary shrine (*betsugū*) | Established = Before 997 CE | Main Hall = [[Shinmei-zukuri]] | coordinates = {{Coord|34.48586111111111|N|136.70394444444443|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | festival = Kazahi-no-Matsuri (*Wind Day Prayer Festival*) | location = {{ill|Toyokawachō|qid=Q17223204}} | architecture_style = {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|qid=Q930545}} }}
'''Kaze-no-Miya''' (風宮) is an auxiliary shrine (*betsugū*) located within the precincts of {{ill|Toyōke Daijingū|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyōke Daijingū|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} of the [[Ise Grand Shrine]] in [[Toyokawa-cho]], [[Ise]], [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]], Japan. == Overview== [[File:Kame-ish 01.JPG|left|thumb|180px|Kame-ishi (Turtle Stone)]] '''Kazenomiya''' is a {{ill|Betsugū (detached shrine)|ja|別宮|lt=Betsugū|1=Setsumatsusha|2=ja|3=摂末社|4=zh|5=攝末社|6=es|7=Setsumatsusha|8=fr|9=Setsumatsusha|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Betsugū (detached shrine)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} of the {{ill|Toyōke Daijingū|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyōke Daijingū|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (Gekū), located at the foot of **Hinokioyama** (檜尾山), south of the main shrine. The enshrined deities are {{ill|Shinatsuhiko|ja|級長津彦命|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinatsuhiko|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to シナツヒコ|1=Shinatsuhiko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1073925}} and {{ill|Shinatobe|ja|級長戸辺命|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinatobe|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to シナツヒコ|1=Shinatsuhiko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1073925}}, the same as those in the {{ill|Kazahinomi-no-Miya|ja|風日祈宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kazahinomi-no-Miya|qq=}}, a detached shrine of the {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (Naikū).
'''Kazenomiya''' is situated to the left after crossing the **Kame-ishi** (亀石), a stone along the approach to {{ill|Taka-no-Miya|lt=Taka-no-Miya|lt=Taga-no-Miya|ja|多賀宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taga-no-Miya|qq=}}, next to the pond in front of the main shrine. It is said that Kame-ishi was transported from the entrance of the **Ama-no-Iwato** (天岩戸) on {{ill|Mount Takakura (Ise)|ja|高倉山 (伊勢市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Takakura (Ise)|qq=|1=Mount Takakura|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11668768}}.
The term *Betsugū* (別宮) means "separate shrine," considered second in reverence only to the main shrine. Apart from '''Kazenomiya''', the Gekū has other Betsugū: {{ill|Taka-no-Miya|ja|多賀宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taka-no-Miya|qq=}} and {{ill|Tsuchi-no-Miya|ja|土宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchi-no-Miya|qq=}} within the precincts, and {{ill|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|1=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|2=ja|3=月夜見宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|ja|月夜見宮|1=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|2=ja|3=月夜見宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|8=ja|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|1=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|2=ja|3=月夜見宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|qq=}} outside the precincts. Kazamiya was the last to be designated as a Betsugū in [[:en:1293|1293]] (Shōō 6) and is Ranked fourth among the four Betsugū.
Originally, it was known as **Kazeya-shiro** (風社) or **Kazejinja** (風神社), a shrine of lesser Rank. However, following the [[:en:Mongol invasions of Japan|Mongol invasions of Japan]] in [[:en:1281|1281]] (Kōan 4), when divine winds (*kamikaze*) protected Japan, it was elevated to the status of Betsugū.
== Enshrined Deities == The enshrined deities of {{ill|Kaze-no-Miya|ja|風宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaze-no-Miya|qq=}} are [[:en:Shinatsuhiko|Shinatsuhiko]] and {{ill|Shinatobe|ja|シナトベ|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinatobe|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to シナツヒコ|1=Shinatsuhiko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1073925}}, gods who govern wind and rain. Originally worshipped as deities bringing favorable weather for agriculture, after the Mongol invasions, they became objects of prayers for the salvation of Japan in times of national crisis.
== History == The origins of Kaze-no-Miya are unclear, but the earliest reference appears in the "Chōtoku Kenroku" from [[:en:997|997]] (Chōtoku 3), citing it as "Kaze-sha," Ranked 4th among 32 rice field shrines under the jurisdiction of the Gekū, as noted in {{ill|Watarai Ieyuki|ja|度会家行|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Watarai Ieyuki|qq=|1=Watarai Ieyuki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11486121}}'s "Ruiju Jingi Hongen." "Rice field shrines" correspond to the current末社 (massha), considered lower than sessha (auxiliary shrines).
Although initially Ranked as a末社, due to its deities’ importance in agriculture, the {{ill|Kazahi-no-Matsuri|ja|風日祈祭|1=Kazahi-no-Misai|2=ja|3=風日祈祭|qid=Q135524493|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kazahi-no-Matsuri|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135524493}} was held, and offerings of "kakechikara" (bundled rice stalks) were made during the [[:en:Kannamesai Festival|Kannamesai]].
During the [[:en:Mongol invasions of Japan|Mongol invasions of Japan]] in [[:en:1281|1281]] (Kōan 4), the court dispatched the Dainagon Tameuji to perform prayers at both the Naikū's Kaze-sha and this shrine. After the retreat of the Mongol forces, the victory was attributed to the "Divine Wind" ([[:en:Kamikaze (typhoon)|Kamikaze]]), and in [[:en:1293|1293]] (Shōō 6), the shrine was elevated to a betsu-gū (detached shrine), named {{ill|Kazahi-no-Miya|ja|風日祈宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kazahi-no-Miya|qq=|1=Kazahi Prayer Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11665488}} and Kaze-no-Miya respectively.
During the late [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], when Western nations visited Japan, the court held 15 days of prayers for the expulsion of foreigners at Kaze-no-Miya and Kazahi-no-Miya in [[:en:1863|1863]] (Bunkyū 3).
In [[:en:1876|1876]] (Meiji 9), during the {{ill|Ise Riot|ja|伊勢暴動|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Riot|qq=|1=Ise Riot|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11379085}}, the sacred object from {{ill|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|1=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|2=ja|3=月夜見宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|ja|月夜見宮|1=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|2=ja|3=月夜見宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|qq=}} was temporarily moved to Kaze-no-Miya to protect it from a nearby fire.<ref>Kaneko (1983): p.214</ref>
== Festivals == The {{ill|Kazahi-no-Matsuri|ja|風日祈祭|1=Kazahi-no-Misai|2=ja|3=風日祈祭|qid=Q135524493|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kazahi-no-Matsuri|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135524493}}, praying for favorable weather and abundant harvests, is held annually on May 14 and August 4.
== Shrine Building == The shrine building of Kaze-no-Miya faces south and is constructed in the [[:en:Shinmei-zukuri|Shinmei-zukuri]] style with a thatched roof, featuring {{ill|Chigi and Katsuogi|ja|千木・鰹木|lt=chigi and katsuogi|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chigi and Katsuogi|qq=|1=chigi and katsuogi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q87220234}} with five odd-numbered katsuogi, following the Gekū’s design. The old shrine site for reconstruction (shikinen sengū) is adjacent to the east and west.
== Access == * Nearest Station: About a 12-minute walk from [[:en:Iseshi Station|Ise-shi Station]] via [[:en:Sangū Line|JR Sangu Line]] and [[:en:Kintetsu Railway|Kintetsu]]. * Nearest Bus Stop: About a 5-minute walk from [[:en:Mie Kotsu|Mie Kotsu]] Gekū-mae Bus Stop. * Nearest Interchange: About 2 km from {{ill|Ise Nishi Interchange|ja|伊勢西インターチェンジ|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Nishi Interchange|qq=|1=Ise-nishi itnerchange|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10884865}} on the [[:en:Ise Expressway|Ise Expressway]]. * Parking: Free parking available.
== References == * Kaneko Nobuo. *Tamaki Town History, Volume II – History and Traditions of South Ise.* Mie Prefecture Local History Series No. 91, Mie Prefecture Local History Publication Association, December 10, 1983. * {{ill|Katsunoshin Sakurai|ja|桜井勝之進|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katsunoshin Sakurai|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 櫻井勝之進|1=Katsunoshin Sakurai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11544758}}. *Ise Grand Shrine.* Gakuseisha, May 20, 1969. * Katsunoshin Sakurai. *The Shrine of the Great deity of Ise.* Hori Shoten, March 25, 1973.
== Related Pages == {{commonscat|Kaze-no-miya (Geku)}} * [[:en:Shinatsuhiko|Shinatsuhiko]] * [[:en:Kamikaze (typhoon)|Kamikaze]] * [[:en:Mongol invasions of Japan|Mongol invasions of Japan]] * {{ill|Toyōke Daijingū|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyōke Daijingū|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} * {{ill|List of Shrines and Facilities of Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮を構成する神社と施設|1=List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|lt=List of 125 Shrines of Ise|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Shrines and Facilities of Ise Grand Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/List of 125 Shrines of Ise|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 伊勢神宮}}
